question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust recently put a new roof on the old Newtownards Minor Injury Unit when it was judged to be at the end of its serviceable life; and (ii) the cost of this work.
answer_text: The South Eastern HSC Trust have not recently replaced the roof on the former Newtownards Minor Injury Unit. The recent roof replacement project was carried out on the Outpatients Building which is adjacent to the former Newtownards Minor Injury Unit.  This building remains in use and currently facilitates a number of outpatient clinics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the regeneration of Queens Parade, Bangor.
answer_text: My officials, in the Department’s Regional Development Office, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work closely with the developer, Bangor Marine, and other stakeholders in an effort to progress this scheme. The Bangor Marine team is working through the pre-commencement conditions that emerged from the planning approval, and there has been positive engagement with the Crown Estate Commission regarding the necessary approvals for the proposals on the Marine Gardens area of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of previous badger culls conducted by his Department, for his assessment of whether a more humane badger vaccination programme should be introduced.
answer_text: My Department does not currently have a wildlife intervention policy in place. This area remains policy in development and will require further public consultation.  My officials are currently reflecting on the issues identified by the judicial review ruling of October 2023 and assessing the scientific, financial, and legislative aspects of each potential wildlife intervention option.Once this work is complete, they will provide detailed advice on these options for my consideration. I have asked the new Chief Veterinary Officer for Northern Ireland to undertake a review of our current bovine Tuberculosis strategy, so that I can be satisfied that the approach we are taking is both science-led and cost-effective.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the badger vaccination programme being developed by his Department.
answer_text: The bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland, that was launched in March 2022, had included a wildlife intervention involving a non-selective cull of badgers.  The cull was to be carried out in areas with a high bTB incidence and high badger density, across Northern Ireland, by farmer led companies using controlled shooting.  Once the infection load in badgers had been reduced, the cull was to be replaced by a badger vaccination programme.		However, this element of the Strategy was overturned in October 2023 following a judicial review taken by Wild Justice and the Northern Ireland Badger Group.  Consequently, my Department does not have a wildlife intervention policy in place.  My officials are currently reflecting on the issues and gaps identified by the judicial review ruling of October 2023 in order to provide detailed advice on wildlife options for my consideration.  Any wildlife measure will require further public consultation before it can be implemented. 	I am committed to finding new, affordable, evidence-based ways of tackling bTB and I have asked the new Chief Veterinary Officer for Northern Ireland to undertake a review of our current bTB programme, so that I can be satisfied that the approach we are taking is both science-led and cost-effective.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the schools in the (i) North Down constituency; and (ii) Strangford constituency that still have Year 8 places available for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: As at 30 May, Year 8 places for the 2024-25 academic year remain available at St Columbanus’ College in the North Down constituency and at Glastry College; Movilla High School; St Colman’s High School & Sixth Form College; St Columba’s College; and The High School Ballynahinch, in the Strangford constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Year 1 places that are available in schools in North Down for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: At the conclusion of the primary admissions process on 25 April 2024, a total of 236 Year 1 places for the 2024-25 academic year remained available at North Down schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of preschool places that are available in schools in North Down for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that as at the close of Stage One of the pre-school admissions process on 25 April 2024, the following pre-school education providers located within the parliamentary constituency of North Down still had funded pre-school places available for the 2024-25 academic year:Bangor Integrated Nursery School;Bloomfield Primary School Nursery Unit;Donaghadee Primary School Nursery Unit;Holywood Nursery School;Kilcooley Primary School Nursery Unit; Rathmore Primary School Nursery Unit;St Malachy’s Primary School Nursery Unit; Sleepy Hollow Early Childhood Centre, Holywood; andTowerview Primary School Nursery Unit.The EA has also confirmed that there is currently a total of 843 funded pre-school places within the parliamentary constituency of North Down for the academic year 2024-25.  These places are comprised of both statutory and non-statutory places and are a mix of full-time and part-time provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what preschools in North Down still have places available for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that as at the close of Stage One of the pre-school admissions process on 25 April 2024, the following pre-school education providers located within the parliamentary constituency of North Down still had funded pre-school places available for the 2024-25 academic year:Bangor Integrated Nursery School;Bloomfield Primary School Nursery Unit;Donaghadee Primary School Nursery Unit;Holywood Nursery School;Kilcooley Primary School Nursery Unit; Rathmore Primary School Nursery Unit;St Malachy’s Primary School Nursery Unit; Sleepy Hollow Early Childhood Centre, Holywood; andTowerview Primary School Nursery Unit.The EA has also confirmed that there is currently a total of 843 funded pre-school places within the parliamentary constituency of North Down for the academic year 2024-25.  These places are comprised of both statutory and non-statutory places and are a mix of full-time and part-time provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Year 8 places that are available in schools in North Down for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: As at 30 May, one Year 8 place remains available in the North Down constituency for the 2024-25 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to undertake a review of planning laws.
answer_text: The Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (RIPA) in 2021/22 focused on the implementation of the legislative provisions of the Planning Act itself and the extent to which the original objectives of the Act have been achieved. The RIPA report was published on 27th January 2022 and made a number of recommendations to bring forward practical and effective fixes and improvements to the current system. All of which are subject to competing priorities and available resources.  Provisions within the Planning Act (NI) 2011 state that further reviews should be undertaken at least once every 5 years after the published 2022 RIPA report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to increase sentences for animal cruelty offences.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for animal cruelty offences.Policy responsibility for the welfare of animals sits with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA).  Any decision to increase the penalties available for animal cruelty offences under the Welfare of Animals (Northern Ireland) Act 2011  is, therefore, a matter for that Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cases of bovine TB recorded in North Down in the last two years.
answer_text: Information relating to bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is not held on a constituency basis, rather, it is held by the geographical regions covered by DAERA Direct Offices. However, I can inform you that in the area covered by the Newtownards DAERA Direct Office, which covers the entirety of the North Down constituency, a total of 225 herds were affected by a new bTB breakdown in the 12 months to the end of April 2024, while 262 herds were affected during the 12 months prior to this (to end of April 2023).The Department publishes statistics on the bovine Tuberculosis programme on a monthly basis on the DAERA website in EXCEL and PDF format:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to introduce stricter sanctions for breaches of planning laws.
answer_text: The current sanctions for failing to comply with enforcement action were introduced, under the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, to create a more significant deterrent by increasing the maximum level of fines.  The maximum fines for a range of offences on summary conviction were increased from £30,000 to £100,000, and for certain other offences, the fines were raised from Level 3 (£1,000) to Level 5 (£5,000).  There are also unlimited fines for certain offences following conviction on indictment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the Caravans Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The 2021 Review of the Caravans Act completed with the publication of a Summary Report with recommendations in March 2023.  I have included a link to this report below for your convenience:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/2021-review-caravans-act-northern-ireland-2011The Act cuts across the remit of my Department, the Department for the Economy and the Department for Infrastructure, and the onus is on each to take forward those recommendations which align to their responsibilities. Those recommendations which come under the remit of my Department relate to:the production of factsheets for the residential caravan sector to clarify legislative responsibilities and provide useful signposting information; anda potential licensing exemption for Housing Executive Irish Traveller sites.  It is anticipated that the factsheets will be ready for publication in the next few months. The licensing exemption will take considerably longer to progress, given it involves a number of policy considerations and, depending on the outcome of this work, will require an amendment to primary legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of people that have been prosecuted for river pollution in North Down in each of the last three years.
answer_text: No people have been prosecuted for water pollution offences in North Down over the last three years. One case is currently under consideration for enforcement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the future of Grey Point Fort, Helen's Bay.
answer_text: My Department has bid for monies to undertake a feasibility study in 24/25.  If this report can proceed it will provide a roadmap for future development.  At this time, I can only assure you of our commitment to progress plans for the fort once funding becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of police officers currently suspended on full pay.
answer_text: The suspension of police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I respect the Chief Constable’s independence and the role of the Policing Board; therefore, it would not be appropriate for me to make comment on these matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions his Department is carrying out to address the lack of electric vehicle charge points in North Down.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the charge point operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. My Department is also leading an EV Infrastructure Task Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider the barriers to enhancing our EV charging infrastructure requirements.  There are now 577 publicly available EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 48% in the total number of available EV charge points here since April 2023. Relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points in North Down.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils including Ards and North Down Borough Council. They are currently preparing a tender for the installation of 127 charge points across those nine Council areas and may be in a position to outline their plans within your constituency should you wish to contact them.My Department continues to work with colleagues in OZEV, to promote the provision of a range of grants made available to support the rollout of additional EV charging infrastructure, such as the recently announced Workplace Charging Scheme for State Funded Education Institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to recruit more nurses into the health service.
answer_text: My Department has undertaken sustained investment in pre-registration nursing and midwifery training over recent years, reaching an all-time high annual intake of 1335 places, across local universities, in recent years. At the time of publication of the most recent data, this investment is bearing fruit, with a 16% increase in nursing and midwifery staff in post across the HSC between March 2019 and March 2024.Additionally, internationally recruited Nurses are a key avenue in addressing our resource issues in the future and ensuring that the health and social care service can deliver the safe and effective service for patients.  Since 2016 1,666 international nurses have been recruited regionally across the five HSC Trusts through the well-established and highly successful International Nurse Recruitment project

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the grass verges along the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor, will be cut.
answer_text: I can confirm that the grass verges along the A2 East Circular Road are scheduled to be cut during the second half of June 2024, weather permitting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to introduce a General Practitioner service for Millisle.
answer_text: Although there is not a GP Practice located in Millisle, there is sufficient provision of GP services provided by neighbouring practices.Currently, Millisle is included in the practice boundary area of several GP Practices in close proximity, ensuring that the people of Millisle are able to avail of GP services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Housing Executive properties that have been tested for (i) defective cavity wall insulation; (ii) partial fill cavity wall insulation; and (iii) the absence of cavity wall insulation in the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has not put in place a specific survey programme to inspect all of cavity wall constructed properties for defective, partially filled or absent cavity wall insulation.  This is because the resources required to do so are not available given its funding shortfall and stock investment priorities.However, in the period 2019/20 to 2023/24 NIHE undertook 1,514 inspections of cavity wall as a result of issues being reported by tenants.  In that same period NIHE also inspected 203 properties due to their inclusion in either energy efficiency pilot projects/programmes or schemes comprising multi-element works.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to resurface Union Street, Donaghadee.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the potholes on Albany Road, Bangor, will be repaired.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to resurface Southwell Road, Bangor.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of grass verge cutting DFI Roads has been authorised to carry out along main roads in 2024-25.
answer_text: It would be my intention for the Department to deliver a programme of grass cutting in accordance with the new approach to verge management across the road network that I announced in October 2022.  The new approach increases my Department’s focus on protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and will support the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Act passed by the Assembly in 2022, and can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/verge-management-grass-cutting-dem-19322

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans the Housing Executive has to resurface the car park situated at the back of Tower Park, Conlig.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The area in question is owned by the Housing Executive. It should be noted that is not a designated car parking area. Although some parking does take place on it, it is a hard blacktop area and is maintained by our ground’s maintenance staff on that basis. There are no current proposals to resurface the area although we address any potholes where and when they occur. Currently, a works order has been placed to fill a number of potholes at this location”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP vacancies in North Down.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my response of 10 April 2024 to his previous question on this issue (AQW 9416/22-27). As I advised, my Department does not hold information on GP vacancies.  GPs are independent contractors and are responsible for identifying and recruiting the GPs and other staff required within their own business to deliver services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Narrow Water Bridge project.
answer_text: In February 2024, the Government of Ireland agreed to provide funding through the Shared Island Fund to build the landmark Narrow Water Bridge connecting the Cooley Peninsula in Louth to the Mourne Mountains in Down. Louth County Council is leading the development of the Narrow Water Bridge project.My Department will continue to work closely with the Irish Government and Louth County Council to facilitate the linkage of Narrow Water Bridge on the Northern side.  The bridge will be a hugely transformative project with the potential to unlock the economic and tourism potential of one of our most beautiful regions. It will also facilitate cross border active travel, including the further development of the network of greenways, and local connectivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an ambulance to arrive in an emergency in the last twelve months.
answer_text: The number of Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) ambulances operational across Northern Ireland varies for the day of week and time of day. The core number of ambulances based on the funded establishment that NIAS plans to have available for day shift ranges from 60 – 72; and for night shifts between 41 and 48. NIAS does not employ ‘ambulance drivers’, rather we have a range of operational frontline roles whose job involves driving an ambulance.  Table 1 below, shows the current WTE and headcount of NIAS frontline operational staff as of 31/3/2024.  These figures do not include Bank staff, HEMS staff, HART staff or other clinical staff who may provide an emergency response.Table 1StaffWTEHeadcountBelfast DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant55.4057PCS Supervisor0.982Emergency Medical Technician33.8034Paramedic49.8851RRV Paramedic7.968Station Supervisor4.004DIVISIONAL TOTAL152.02156Northern DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant53.9956Emergency Medical Technician67.5268Paramedic96.3797RRV Paramedic8.008Station Supervisor10.0010DIVISIONAL TOTAL235.87239South Eastern DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant32.9334Emergency Medical Technician51.0353Paramedic77.4980RRV Paramedic8.649Station Supervisor8.899DIVISIONAL TOTAL178.99185Southern DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant39.8143Emergency Medical Technician53.9254Paramedic77.9679RRV Paramedic9.009Station Supervisor10.0010DIVISIONAL TOTAL190.69195Western DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant45.6046Emergency Medical Technician64.1665Paramedic74.4075RRV Paramedic12.0012Station Supervisor8.008DIVISIONAL TOTAL204.16206Regional Ambulance Clinical Training CentreNewly Qualified Paramedic -Trainee13.0013TRUST TOTAL974.73994The Trust has a range of plans in place to manage demand and improve performance.  This includes an increase in fleet size, as required, subject to business case approval and available funding.NIAS has recently finalised a recruitment drive aimed at filling vacancies across the Trust.  In 2024/2025, NIAS received funding to increase our workforce with 48 Newly Qualified Paramedics (NQPs) and plans are underway to receive those NQPs in September 2024.  These appointments are vital to ensuring the organisation's capacity to maintain and grow levels of cover across the region. NIAS have also received approval to implement an integrated clinical hub function within our Emergency Ambulance Control Room, with the introduction of an additional 15 clinical posts to enhance the existing team. This team is tasked with safely reducing the volume of patients being conveyed to Emergency Departments across Northern Ireland and with providing patients with access to an appropriate clinical pathwayTable 2 below, shows our average response time, by category of call, for the period 01/05/2023 to 30/04/2024.Table 2Category of CallAverage Response TimeCat10:11:31Cat20:48:05Cat32:14:44Cat43:36:58

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on steps being taken to ensure the award of intimidation points for social housing is not abused.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the introduction of Charlotte's Law.
answer_text: As per my previous answer on 22 April, it remains my intention to include legislative provision for ‘Charlotte’s Law’ as part of a Sentencing Bill, which I plan to introduce next year.  Officials in my Department have continued to develop the detail of the proposal, working closely with the affected families and other stakeholders during the absence of the Assembly, and since the return of the Executive.The key element of Charlotte’s Law will be a significant increase in the murderer’s life sentence tariff to reflect their ongoing failure to disclose critical information about the location of the victim’s remains.  This will be coupled with the opportunity for a proportionate reduction in their tariff should a post sentence disclosure be made by the prisoner.   Similar to ‘Helen’s Law’ in England and Wales, it will also require the Parole Commissioners to take the failure to disclose into account when considering the prisoner’s suitability for release on licence at the parole stage.A number of the administrative measures I announced in November 2020 have already been agreed with the relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to recruit more ambulance staff.
answer_text: The number of Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) ambulances operational across Northern Ireland varies for the day of week and time of day. The core number of ambulances based on the funded establishment that NIAS plans to have available for day shift ranges from 60 – 72; and for night shifts between 41 and 48. NIAS does not employ ‘ambulance drivers’, rather we have a range of operational frontline roles whose job involves driving an ambulance.  Table 1 below, shows the current WTE and headcount of NIAS frontline operational staff as of 31/3/2024.  These figures do not include Bank staff, HEMS staff, HART staff or other clinical staff who may provide an emergency response.Table 1StaffWTEHeadcountBelfast DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant55.4057PCS Supervisor0.982Emergency Medical Technician33.8034Paramedic49.8851RRV Paramedic7.968Station Supervisor4.004DIVISIONAL TOTAL152.02156Northern DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant53.9956Emergency Medical Technician67.5268Paramedic96.3797RRV Paramedic8.008Station Supervisor10.0010DIVISIONAL TOTAL235.87239South Eastern DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant32.9334Emergency Medical Technician51.0353Paramedic77.4980RRV Paramedic8.649Station Supervisor8.899DIVISIONAL TOTAL178.99185Southern DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant39.8143Emergency Medical Technician53.9254Paramedic77.9679RRV Paramedic9.009Station Supervisor10.0010DIVISIONAL TOTAL190.69195Western DivisionAmbulance Care Attendant45.6046Emergency Medical Technician64.1665Paramedic74.4075RRV Paramedic12.0012Station Supervisor8.008DIVISIONAL TOTAL204.16206Regional Ambulance Clinical Training CentreNewly Qualified Paramedic -Trainee13.0013TRUST TOTAL974.73994The Trust has a range of plans in place to manage demand and improve performance.  This includes an increase in fleet size, as required, subject to business case approval and available funding.NIAS has recently finalised a recruitment drive aimed at filling vacancies across the Trust.  In 2024/2025, NIAS received funding to increase our workforce with 48 Newly Qualified Paramedics (NQPs) and plans are underway to receive those NQPs in September 2024.  These appointments are vital to ensuring the organisation's capacity to maintain and grow levels of cover across the region. NIAS have also received approval to implement an integrated clinical hub function within our Emergency Ambulance Control Room, with the introduction of an additional 15 clinical posts to enhance the existing team. This team is tasked with safely reducing the volume of patients being conveyed to Emergency Departments across Northern Ireland and with providing patients with access to an appropriate clinical pathwayTable 2 below, shows our average response time, by category of call, for the period 01/05/2023 to 30/04/2024.Table 2Category of CallAverage Response TimeCat10:11:31Cat20:48:05Cat32:14:44Cat43:36:58

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of introducing sentencing legislation similar to Harper's Law.
answer_text: Harper’s Law introduced a mandatory life sentence for the killing of an emergency worker engaged in the course of their duties, in certain circumstances.  Anyone convicted of murder in Northern Ireland automatically receives a life sentence and, in circumstances considered appropriate by the court, those convicted of manslaughter may also receive a life sentence.Sentencing decisions are a matter for the independent judiciary who come to their decisions after consideration of all the evidence presented in the case, as well as any relevant sentencing guidance, sentencing principles and having regard to sentences passed in other similar cases.Sentencing in manslaughter cases can be particularly complex. It is not appropriate for the legislature to seek to fetter the independence of the judiciary unless there is strong evidence of the need for intervention.  I previously committed to monitor the operation of Harper’s Law in England and Wales.  To date I have found no reports of it being used. At present I do not consider, therefore, there is a need for similar legislation in this jurisdiction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schools in North Down that will be included in the 20mph speed limit zoning for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.  A review is underway to determine if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the news that Bright Sparks Preschool has been forced to close due to health and safety issues, to detail any assistance his Department can provide.
answer_text: Alternative temporary accommodation has been provided by the Education Authority (EA) for Bright Sparks within Crawfordsburn Primary School.A further condition survey is to be undertaken by the EA on the existing modular unit to ascertain statutory remedial works required.  A further meeting is scheduled to take place with Bright Sparks on 16 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacancies across the civil service, broken down by department.
answer_text: Details of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of April 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 3017 vacancies across the Civil Service. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new school build for Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: The new build school project is fully designed, planning approval has been granted, the additional land required to deliver the project has been acquired and Business Case approval is in place. The project will progress to building contractor procurement when funding is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he will address the lack of electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point rests solely with the Charge Point Operator. Current providers operating here include BP Pulse, Easygo, ESB, Maxol and Weev. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.In this commercially led market, my officials continue to provide advice to Charge Point Operators on operational matters such as procedural arrangements for planning and installation requirements. There are now 577 publicly available EV charge points in the North, this represents a growth of 48% in the total number of available EV charge points here since April 2023. Relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points in the North.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV)  ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils and are currently preparing a tender for charge point operators to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas.My Department continues to work with colleagues in OZEV, to promote the provision of a range of grants made available to support the rollout of additional EV charging infrastructure, such as the recently announced Workplace Charging Scheme for State Funded Education Institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children in care in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department publishes Official Statistics on the number of children in care in the annual report ‘Children's Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland’ (Children's social care statistics for Northern Ireland 2022/23 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). The next release of these Official Statistics for year ending 31 March 2024 is due in October.Additionally, my department publishes monthly provisional figures on children in care (Northern Ireland children's social services data (provisional) | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legal requirement in relation to the depth of a footpath defect for his Department to undertake repair work.
answer_text: Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a duty on my Department to maintain all public roads in reasonable condition. The physical extent of a ‘road’ has not been defined in the Order but it is widely accepted by the Courts that footways form part of the road. My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Where my Department becomes aware of defects on footpaths within the Department’s control, my officials will record the defect for repair in line with our current road maintenance standards which can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdfThe value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children entitled to free school meals in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Figures from the 2023/24 school census data collection exercise report that there were 94,975 pupils entitled to free school meals in Northern Ireland.  This figure includes pupils enrolled in nursery schools, primary (including nursery and reception classes), post primary, special schools and EOTAS centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the powers held by schools to address bullying.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 came into effect in 2021.  The Act makes the Board of Governors collectively responsible for development and implementation of Anti-Bullying or Addressing Bullying Policy and practices. It states that:Schools must ensure that an Anti-Bullying/Addressing Bullying Policy is in place and freely available to parents/carers and pupils.Policy must be followed correctly when incidents are reported.Schools must record incidents of bullying and alleged bullying type behaviours including the motivation, method, how each incident was addressed, together with the outcomes.Measures must be in place to ensure that the policy is working effectively to support pupils and reduce bullying type behaviour.The policy must be reviewed in consultation with stakeholders, in response to changing need and at least every four years.The Department established the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) in the Education Authority (EA) in 2022. ABSIT provides support for schools, educational settings and young people to help them understand the requirements of the Act and embed cultures that help eradicate bullying.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients in each GP practice in Bangor.
answer_text: I refer to my response to AQW 9964/22-27, which provides the number of patients registered to each GP Practice in Bangor at 31st December 2023; this is still the latest available data.   The Business Services Organisation publishes both quarterly and annual General Medical Services statistics, which include the number of patients registered to each GP practice.  The data at 31st December 2023 was published on 15th February 2024; the next release of the General Medical Services Statistics will be the annual publication, due to be published on the 6th June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to reinstate the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: Following the success of the scheme I introduced in June 2021, I had hoped to introduce a new reimbursement scheme this year. I included a bid for funding for a new replacement, which could have treated over 4,000 patients, during this year’s budget setting process. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding that will be provided to Housing Associations in the 2024-25 financial year for the building of new houses, broken down by Housing Association.
answer_text: The Executive has recently agreed the 2024/25 budget, I will now decide how this is allocated across my department. Funding for the for the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) has yet to be finalised. Funding provided to individual Housing Associations will only be available at the end of the financial year, as Housing Associations are paid the Housing Association Grant in tranches as social housing schemes are progressed rather than allocated a set amount at the start of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any new social housing that will be built by the Housing Executive in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The construction of new social homes will be dependent on the available budget. The Executive has recently agreed the 2024/25 budget, and I will decide how this is allocated across my Department. Funding for the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for 2024/25 has yet to be finalised. This must include funding for contractual liabilities for new social homes that were started in prior years.The detail of new social homes is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive Manages the Social Housing Development Programme on behalf of DfC. New build social housing is delivered by Housing Associations.There are currently 5,745 social homes under construction of which 2,627 are due to complete in 2024/25. There are 6,836 social homes programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2026/27. 3,188 of which are due to start in 2024/25. Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to his Department's pledge to plant 18 million trees, to detail (i) the number of trees planted (a) in North Down; and (b) in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the location of the trees planted in North Down.
answer_text: Since the launch of the Forests for Our Future Programme, my Department has grant aided the planting of 10 small new native woodlands in the North Down Assembly Area. 		(i)(a) Approximately 5,000 trees have been planted in these woodlands creating 3.4 hectares of new woodland in the North Down area.		(i)(b) Under the Forests for Our Future programme, to the end of March 2024, an estimated 3.5 million trees have been planted across Northern Ireland.		(ii) Nine of the new woodlands in the North Down area are planted on agricultural land to the west of Donaghadee and Millisle and one is on the Craigantlet Hills to the north of Ballysallagh Reservoir.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: The Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has for the old Donaghadee to Belfast railway line.
answer_text: The Strategic Plan for Greenways identifies a potential greenway loop between Bangor, Newtowards and Donaghadee. Delivery of greenways are generally council led, with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering approved projects. Ards and North Down Borough Council is considering proposals for a greenway between Bangor and Donaghadee however, these considerations are at an early stage and Council’s current focus is on delivery of a number of other greenways within the council area. A feasibility study is yet to be carried out to establish the most appropriate route and elements of the old railway line that still exist may be considered within that feasibility study in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department can do to help businesses become more Stoma Aware and Stoma Friendly.
answer_text: My Department has not issued any guidance to businesses on the development of Stoma Friendly toilets.  I am aware that the Equality Commission has produced a range of publications covering disability issues in relation to facilities and provision of services.  These are available on the Commission’s website or by contacting their enquiry line.  The Equality Commission can also provide advice and training on anti-discrimination legislation and good equality practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department can do to help people become more Stoma Aware and Stoma Friendly.
answer_text: My Department has not issued any guidance to businesses on the development of Stoma Friendly toilets.  I am aware that the Equality Commission has produced a range of publications covering disability issues in relation to facilities and provision of services.  These are available on the Commission’s website or by contacting their enquiry line.  The Equality Commission can also provide advice and training on anti-discrimination legislation and good equality practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the savings made by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust following the closure of the Minor Injury Units in Bangor and Newtownards.
answer_text: The Minor Injury Services at Bangor Hospital and Newtownards Hospital transferred to the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald in September 2023.  As previously advised in the response to your AQW 6796 22 27 on 26 February, the South Eastern Trust has confirmed that there were no savings made as a result of this service change. The public consultation stated that the decision was not taken to make financial savings but rather to reshape and sustain urgent and emergency care services in the North Down and Ards area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reopen the Minor Injury Units in Bangor and Newtownards in order to alleviate pressure on the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: These services, along with their associated resources, transferred last September following extensive consultation during 2023.The South Eastern Trust had been unable to fully staff the units for some years posing a risk that the service would have to be withdrawn at short notice due to staffing difficulties with potential adverse implications for hospital services in the area.  It was critical that the Trust addressed this risk in a planned and controlled manner to ensure the continuity of unscheduled care in the area.  Emergency Nurses need a multi-disciplinary environment to sustain their skills and expertise.  This was not provided by the standalone units which had difficulty attracting such highly skilled care professionals.  It was not only a challenge in relation to Emergency Nurse Practitioners (ENPs) but for diagnostic (radiology) and other care professionals as well.   The facilities at the two smaller hospital sites, were also no longer suited to providing modern day unscheduled care and required substantial capital investment to meet demand and continue as minor injury units.The new enhanced Minor Injury Service that has now been opened at the Ulster Hospital is more comprehensive and delivers significantly improved care for the residents of Ards and North Down.  It is now consultant led rather than nurse led with supporting medical staff.It is supported on site by a greatly increased level of diagnostics such as CT and MRI.It is open longer hours (7 days a week 8am – 6pm) There is no age restriction for attendees andPatients are less likely to transfer to another hospital for minor injury treatment.  Prior to the transfer of services, the Trust had to transfer patients with medical issues such as dislocated shoulders or head injuries to an acute setting. With the opening of an urgent care centre adjacent to the Trust’s ED, patient care will be further enhanced at the Ulster Hospital in these coming months.I am convinced that this change in the Trust’s minor injury service maximises the Trust’s available resources, optimises staff skills in a modern hospital environment, secures a sustainable model of care into the future and delivers a stepped improvement to unscheduled patient care for your constituents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to introduce new services to the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The South Eastern HSC Trust has identified the following new treatments/services that will commence in the Ulster Hospital during 2024/25.HOLEP (Holmium Laser Enucleation of the Prostate) surgery for urology patients.  This service will commence for residents of the South Eastern area prior to establishing a regional service when appropriate training is completed and additional resources are identified.Advanced therapeutic endoscopy service for the removal of large polyps. This procedure will be delivered as a day case using an endoscope, rather than admitting patients for an open surgical procedure as an inpatientThe Trust has also received funding to secure / develop 2 specialist nurses, and the supporting team, to carry out colposcopy and hysteroscopy procedures within Gynaecology to help address waiting times within the Gynaecology Service.Whilst these are the new services identified by the Trust at this stage, other initiatives may also commence in year when additional resources are confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to increase the number of GPs.
answer_text: Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the GP workforce.  The annual number of GP training places has been increased from 65 in 2015/16 to the current level of 121, an increase of 86%.  I will shortly be considering proposals from my officials for education and training commissioning for 2024/25, across the range of pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department.  These will have to be considered in the context of the very challenging financial situation facing my Department, and the many competing priorities. A number of schemes are in place to support the recruitment and retention of GPs.  These include the Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme.  The Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme was launched on 4th January 2023 to promote recruitment in hard to recruit areas. It aims to support practices in attracting and recruiting new GPs as well as retaining those who join their team.  GP practices can apply for a number of packages under the Scheme including a Golden Hello, relocation costs, recruitment costs, management costs, reimbursement costs for applying to the NI Performers List, and funding for international GPs.  As of March 2024, 45 GP Practices have applied to the Scheme and received approval in principle for funding.  25 GP Practices have applied and received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP.  Steps have also been taken to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the ROI, Australia, Canada, New Zealand and South Africa.  My Department is working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) to restructure the GP training programme which it is hoped will enhance the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.  The Ulster University’s School of Medicine at Magee Graduate Entry Programme places a very significant emphasis on primary care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west of Northern Ireland.  It is hoped that this will help ensure a supply of local students who wish to pursue a career in General Practice in these areas.My Department has also been taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists; all of which are making a difference to the capacity of primary care services and how those services are delivered; and are contributing to improved patient outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's plans to increase the number of four-bedroom properties in Housing Executive stock in North Down.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“This response refers to plans to increase the number of four-bedroom properties in housing association stock in North Down, as the social housing development programme relates to housing association development activity. There are currently no 4-bedroom properties included within the programme in the North Down Constituency. The proportion of applicants from single person, elderly and small family households is significantly higher; however, we continue to engage with Housing Associations to identify and progress schemes which will deliver a sustainable mix of house types to meet current and future needs.The Housing Executive publish a Commissioning Prospectus on an annual basis, which summarises the Housing Need Assessments (by Council and Settlement) and it is used to direct Housing Association activity to areas of unmet need where there is a shortage of proposals on the Social Housing Development Programme. The Commissioning Prospectus provides guidance on the type and mix of new social housing units required to help address housing need.In areas of housing need where there is a consistent shortage of new homes proposed, the Housing Executive’s Regional Place Shaping teams undertake Site Identification Studies which are shared with nominated housing associations. Site Identification Studies have been programmed for both Bangor and Holywood for the next three years (2024/24 – 2026/27).Annual Housing Need Assessments are also used to inform engagement with Council Planners on the preparation of the new Local Development Plans and continuing engagement with Council Planning departments on social housing applications. This ensures consistency between SHDP delivery, the implementation of wider Planning policy and the preparation of Local Development Plans and ensures that housing need is afforded the necessary priority.The Housing Executive also take a lead role on the delivery of housing related actions in the Ards and North Down Community Plan and aligned to this, continue to participate on the Community Planning Estates and Land Group. In addition, we continue to monitor the public sector trawl of surplus sites in areas of unmet housing need.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many incidents of food poisoning in restaurants have been reported to the Food Standards Agency in the last two years.
answer_text: In the last two years, (1 April 2022 – 1 April 2024) the Food Standards Agency (FSA) in Northern Ireland received three reports of alleged food poisoning potentially associated with restaurants in Northern Ireland. Each of these was referred to the relevant district council for investigation.However, the FSA is not the primary contact point for isolated reports of food poisoning associated with restaurants. In Northern Ireland, district councils, as competent authorities for food safety, lead on localised and non-serious food incident investigation. Therefore, isolated reports or unsubstantiated complaints of food poisoning are directed to district councils in the first instance. The FSA as the central competent authority for food and feed safety, must be notified by district councils only in the event of a non-localised (across more than one district council area, or internationally) or of a serious food incident, for example a food poisoning outbreak.Members of the public may also report suspected food poisoning incidents directly to their GP, who in turn report findings to the Public Health Agency (PHA) in Northern Ireland, which is the lead Agency in relation to outbreaks and holds a national picture of infectious disease. In the event of a food poisoning outbreak being declared by the PHA, it leads the response, in association with the FSA and relevant district councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the criteria an area must meet to be designated as an enterprise zone in North Down.
answer_text: The Department for Levelling Up in London published a high-level policy prospectus for an Enhanced Investment Zone tailored to the economy of the north at the end of March.  I have stated my preference to harness the policy to support our green technologies and skills for the green economy.   The prospectus makes clear that for initial policy development purposes, the entirety of the north will be treated as the potential geographic scope for the Zone.  My officials are currently scoping out the operational and resource requirements to commence a co-design process with the Levelling Up department.  I expect to appraise Executive colleagues as that process evolves over the coming months.

answer_text: The provision of non-emergency transport to facilitate individuals to access health and social care services is determined by a medical practitioner who carries out an assessment of an individual’s transport needs, as set out in Chapter 3 of “The Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in NI”.
