question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will seek additional funding to prevent the plans by the PSNI to reduce the number of operational PSNI Enquiry Offices from 28 to 17.
answer_text: As you are aware, budgets for 2024-25 have not yet been set by the NI Executive and therefore no decisions have been taken regarding funding levels for the next financial year.  The Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, has operational independence in the prioritisation of policing resources, including the closure of Enquiry Offices.   I remain committed to working with him to ensure that we have a police service that is, in all aspects, properly resourced for the challenges it faces. It is important to acknowledge that the resourcing challenges faced in policing are replicated across all parts of the justice system and that, without additional resources for my department, the current levels of service will not be able to be maintained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many grass cuts will be carried out by his Department this year.
answer_text: The upcoming programme for grass cutting cannot be confirmed until a budget has been agreed for 2024-25.  However, it would be my intention for the Department to deliver a programme of grass cutting in accordance with the new approach to verge management across the road network that I announced in October 2022.  The new approach increases my Department’s focus on protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and will support the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Act passed by the Assembly in 2022, and can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/verge-management-grass-cutting-dem-19322

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to numerous emails to his Department on the subject, when the foliage will be cut back at the Gransha roundabout, Bangor, which is blocking access for mobility scooter users.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular routine maintenance inspections on all roads. Inspectors will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction, obscuring road signs, or have the potential to create danger for the road user. In those instances where overgrown hedges are identified as causing a danger, officials will contact the landowner requesting their co-operation in having the necessary work undertaken. If the landowner fails to respond, the Department can instigate legal action to have the danger presented by the overgrown hedges addressed, although this action is not usually required.The Department seeks to work with landowners to proactively manage safety issues in this way, including by placing notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding landowners of their responsibility regarding hedges and the need to carry out maintenance during the early part of the year, avoiding where possible the bird nesting season from 1 March to 31 August.In this case the Department will seek to have the foliage cut back by the relevant landowner as required, bearing in mind our obligations in respect of the bird nesting season.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the road resurfacing programme for Kinnegar Drive, Holywood, will commence.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.  The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the weed spraying program for footpaths will commence.
answer_text: The upcoming programme for grass cutting and weed spraying cannot be confirmed until a budget has been agreed for 2024-25.  However, it would be my intention for the Department to deliver a programme of weed spraying in line with previous years and in accordance with the current road maintenance standards. A copy of these can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the extra resources he is allocating to provide respite care for parents of a child with a disability or terminal illness.
answer_text: Respite Care provides valuable opportunities for children with disability to spend time away from their parents / primary carers, whilst also allowing parents / primary carers to have a break from their caring responsibilities. Across Northern Ireland, respite support for children with disability or terminal illness is delivered by the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector. Access to and the frequency of respite care is dependent on individually assessed need.Respite provision may include overnight care in a short break residential unit or ‘Sharing the Care’ type respite services which are community based, for example, fostering support, befriending support services, community activities such as afterschool support / youth group activities / summer schemes. The respite service may be provided to children with disability directly by the HSC Trust, by a community organisation who may have a contract with the HSC Trust to deliver the service, or via Direct Payments to the primary carer to enable support to be sourced.There has been significant additional funding provided generally for services for Children with a Disability in recent years. Since approximately 2020, and including planned additional funding for 2023/24, there will be circa £7.4m specific recurrent funding provided for services and supports for Children with a Disability, which includes over £1m specifically in relation to respite (short breaks) and family support enhancement services.Respite Services are also provided by the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice for all children with a life limiting conditions within Northern Ireland, this includes respite support for parents. The funding model for the NICH is under review and discussions remain ongoing between my Department officials and the NICH, as the NICH has raised concerns regarding the sustainability of services. The funding provided to the NICH for 23/24 was in the region of 1.8 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether any decision on the way forward for a new strategy to eradicate bovine tuberculosis requires Executive approval; and (ii) whether a new strategy will be subject to a vote in the Assembly.
answer_text: My department continues to work on the implementation of the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland that was launched in March 2022. This Strategy contained 21 actions across six thematic areas and is designed to tackle the different causes that facilitate the transmission of bTB.  	The Strategy had included a wildlife intervention measure which sought to address the role of the badger population in disease maintenance and spread of bovine TB.  However, this element of the Strategy was overturned in October 2023 following a judicial review taken by Wild Justice and the Northern Ireland Badger Group. 	I have asked Brian Dooher, the new Chief Veterinary Officer, to take a fresh look at the issue of bTB.  My officials are currently working on advice to me on revised options for wildlife intervention and are also analysing the responses to the recent consultation on compensation reform, which had taken place at the direction of the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland. 		Any new or additional actions my department may take with regards to bTB eradication, including possible wildlife intervention or compensation reform, will require assessment in due course as to whether Executive or Assembly approval is required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many jobs have been created through foreign investment in the last three years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2023 being the most recent available.  During the period 2020-21 to 2022-23, Invest NI provided support to externally owned businesses that will lead to the creation of 5,564 new jobs over the next 3 to 5 years and will result in an increase of £158m in annual salaries.In addition to support offered towards job creation over the past three years, Invest NI secured investment of nearly £200m by externally owned businesses to undertake other business development activities such as R&D/innovation, skills development etc. This investment will underpin business competitiveness and lead to further growth and employment opportunities.For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many objections to badger culling were received as part of the 2021 consultation on the bovine tuberculosis eradication strategy.
answer_text: As part of the 2021 consultation on the Department's proposed implementation and next steps for the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) eradication strategy for Northern Ireland, views were sought on a proposal for a non-selective badger cull in areas of high bTB incidence and high badger density.  In response to the question ‘Do you agree with the Department's preferred option for Wildlife Intervention?’ 481 (14.29%) of the respondents replied No with 2,853 (84.73%) people responding Yes.  33 respondents (0.98%) did not answer this question.Concurrent with the consultation, two public petitions opposing the wildlife intervention proposal were presented to the Assembly.  On 4 October 2021, you presented a petition containing 3,606 signatures. On 5 October 2021, John Blair, MLA, presented a separate petition which had been organised by voluntary organisations opposing the policy and which contained 10,529 signatures.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration has been given to a Public Realm and Environmental Improvement Scheme for Main Street, Millisle.
answer_text: My Department has a regeneration policy focus on urban settlements with a population of 5,000 or more people. As Millisle has a population below the 5,000 threshold, it will not be possible for my Department to provide further funding for a Public Realm and Environmental Improvement project at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when traffic calming measures will be put in place at Ballyree Drive, Bloomfield Estate, Bangor.
answer_text: Traffic calming schemes are funded through my Department’s Local Transport Safety Measures (LTSM) budget which covers a range of measures aimed at providing a safer road transport system.  Regrettably, the available investment has not been sufficient to deliver all schemes that have been assessed as worthwhile.The planned programme of work for the 2024/25 year is currently being prepared and will be finalised when budgets are confirmed.  The programme will then be presented to Council in due course.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Cannreagh Road sewerage system upgrade plans in Donaghadee.
answer_text: The management of Northern Ireland’s wastewater infrastructure  is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding will be distributed to football clubs.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely with clubs at all levels to build strong relationships and understand needs and are developing proposals in partnership with an Advisory Working Group. I have asked my officials as a matter of priority to present me with proposals and options for consideration to take the Programme forward. Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's pledge to plant 18 million trees.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he plans to address the shortage of social housing in North Down.
answer_text: Housing need is generally addressed through the re-let of existing social housing accommodation (Stock turnover). Where the volume of re-lets is insufficient to meet housing need, then construction or acquisition of further (new) social housing is required. In 2023/2024 there were 20 new social housing units completed within the North Down parliamentary constituency.There are currently 61 social housing units under construction with the constituency. Additionally, there are 100 social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2026/27. The delivery of these and the wider Social Housing Development Programme will be subject to sufficient capital budget being made available to my Department.Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.Information on unmet housing need across North Down is available through the Commissioning Prospectus which is available on the Housing Executive website.  This is a guide for Housing Association and the development sector of the volume, household type, numbers and location for new homes across the constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's flood defence plans for Donaghadee.
answer_text: My Department has no planned works to construct flood defences within Donaghadee. However, to alleviate the impacts of flooding from rivers, my Department’s Rivers Directorate will continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has undertaken, or plans to undertake, any studies into endangered wildlife species in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Endangered and threatened wildlife species are identified in Irish Red Data lists and in the published list of Northern Ireland Priority Species, based on a wide range of studies commissioned by DAERA and NIEA.  However, there are data deficiencies on certain species and the impact of a wide range of pressures and threats facing our natural environment.  Currently, the status monitoring of threatened species carried out by NIEA focuses on species within protected areas (ASSIs, etc), with less coverage for the wider countryside.NIEA works in partnership with a number of organisations, including the Centre for Environmental Data and Recording (CEDaR), the Joint Nature Conservation Committee and specialist eNGOs to enhance species surveillance and conservation measures for species recovery.  NIEA is investigating the use of new monitoring techniques and methodologies to improve our evidence base on wildlife species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to list the (i) fauna; and (ii) flora, under possible threat of extinction in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red list of Threatened Species sets out the conservation status and extinction risk of biological species.  NIEA works with National Parks and Wildlife Service (ROI) to develop the Irish Red Lists, which can be accessed at Irish Red Lists | Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (daera-ni.gov.uk)The Wildlife and Natural Environment Act (NI) 2011 places a duty on DAERA to publish a list of the species of flora and fauna of principal importance for conserving biodiversity. These are primarily threatened and declining species which require conservation action because of their importance in an all-Ireland and UK context.  The Department must keep these lists under review and is working on Tranche 2 of the current Priority Species review. The Northern Ireland Priority Species list can be accessed at -https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/list-northern-ireland-priority-species-2023
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many cases of badger baiting have been recorded in North Down in each of the last two years.
answer_text: The Police Service Northern Ireland (PSNI) is the enforcing authority in relation to badger baiting under the Wildlife Order Northern Ireland 1985 (as amended). Therefore, my Department does not hold records relating to badger-baiting cases within Northern Ireland.  Statistics for prosecutions will be recorded by the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) and information about judicial outcomes are held by the Courts Service.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of Tree Preservation Orders in force in North Down; and (ii) the location of these orders.
answer_text: Details of Tree Preservation Orders are held on a public register maintained by each local council. As such, I would advise that you contact North Down and Ards Borough Council to request this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of teaching vacancies in North Down, broken down by school.
answer_text: The number of vacancies in teaching staff is not reported to Department of Education (DE) as DE is not the employer of school-based staff.*My officials asked Education Authority (EA) and Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), for information pertaining to their school-based staff.As of 11 April 2024, the EA has advised that there are currently 23 recruitment exercises in progress within North Down for controlled schools.  The list below breaks the current recruitment figures for vacancies in the North Down constituency by school as requested:Bangor – PrincipalsBallymagee Primary School			-	1Trinity Nursery School				-	1Bangor – TeachersBangor Academy and 6th Form College	-	3Bangor Central Integrated Primary School	-	3Glenlola Collegiate				-	5Kilcooley Primary School			-	1Kilmaine Primary School				-	3Donaghadee – TeachersKillard House Special Primary School		-	5Holywood – TeachersPriory Integrated College				-	1CCMS colleagues have provided the number of 9 vacancies within catholic maintained schools as follows:St Columbanus’ CollegePermanent vacancies	-	3Temporary vacancies	-	6*These figures do not include Voluntary Grammar, Grant Maintained Integrated or Irish Medium schools

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, despite numerous emails to his Department, why potholes on Castle Park Road, Bangor, have not been repaired or marked for repair.
answer_text: As a result of budgetary constraints, my Department has had to introduce a limited service maintenance policy meaning that only the highest priority road defects are repaired. This underfunding has unfortunately resulted in a deteriorating road network.Castle Park Road was last inspected on 9th April 2024 but there were no defects that met the intervention criteria in accordance with the current limited service maintenance policy. The defects will be considered again during the next routine inspection and will be prioritised for repair if the intervention criteria is met.My Department will continue to monitor this area and repair defects in accordance with the current policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the shortfall in police officer numbers in the Ards and North Down area.
answer_text: Police officer numbers are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the resurfacing of Clandeboye Road, Bangor.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what road calming measures are planned for North Down during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Traffic calming schemes are funded through my Department’s Local Transport Safety Measures (LTSM) budget which covers a range of measures aimed at providing a safer road transport system.  Regrettably, the available investment has not been sufficient to deliver all schemes that have been assessed as worthwhile.The planned programme of work for the 2024/25 year will be finalised when budgets are confirmed and the programme will be presented to Council in due course.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether GP practices prioritise private patients above NHS patients for a GP appointment; and (ii) any guidelines his Department provide in relation to this.
answer_text: A GMS GP Practice that is also providing a private GP service to non-registered patients of that practice needs to provide my Department with the necessary assurances that they will continue to provide the appropriate level of GMS services to the patients currently registered with the practice, for which they are funded. Patients currently registered should not be disadvantaged. Work is underway within my Department to develop guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when weed spraying on footpaths in North Down will commence.
answer_text: The upcoming programme for grass cutting and weed control cannot be finalised until a budget has been agreed for 2024-25.  However, it would be my intention for the Department to deliver a programme of weed control in line with previous years and in accordance with the current road maintenance standards. A copy of these can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the potholes on Market Street, Bangor, will be repaired.
answer_text: As a result of budgetary constraints, my Department has had to introduce a limited service maintenance policy meaning that only the highest priority road defects are repaired. This underfunding has unfortunately resulted in a deteriorating road network.Market Street was last inspected on 9th April 2024 with all defects that met the criteria in accordance with the current limited-service maintenance policy, identified and instructed for repair. Any remaining defects will be considered during the next routine inspection.My Department will continue to monitor this area and repair defects in accordance with the current policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why the old Donaghadee to Belfast railway line was not included in the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review considers how the rail network could contribute to the decarbonisation of the island’s transport systems, promote sustainable connectivity into and between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, and support balanced regional development. In doing so, it focuses on inter-city and inter-regional rail links across the island. Rail services to locations such as Donaghadee function as commuter links to Belfast and thus fell outside the scope of the Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what new street lighting schemes are planned in North Down in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has an ongoing capital programme that prioritises the replacement of street lighting systems that have been tested and deemed uneconomical to repair.The programme for 2024-25 is currently being developed and will be finalised and published once budgets for the incoming financial year have been confirmed and agreed.  In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair faults in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the GP surgeries in North Down that have vacancies for a GP.
answer_text: We do not hold the information on vacancies within GP practices.  GP practices are independent contractors who manage their own staffing levels and determine how many positions are required within their own business.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason for the upgrade to the Groomsport Road roundabout.
answer_text: There are a number of factors used when determining the need for road resurfacing, including road usage, general surface condition, structural deformation, public enquiries and public liability claims.Groomsport Road Roundabout forms part of the busy A2 corridor and had started to show signs of structural failure (severe cracking and deformation). If left untreated this would have resulted in the roundabout needing extensive reconstruction at much greater cost and inconvenience when compared to the recent resurfacing. The scheme was planned for inclusion in the 2024/25 resurfacing programme subject to available resources. However, following an in-year reallocation of capital funding the scheme has been brought forward into this 2023/24 year.All the schemes in the North Down Area which are listed in the 2023/24 Council Report have now been delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of court cases his Department (a) is currently involved in; and (b) has been involved in, over the last four years; and (ii) the overall cost for each court case.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is currently involved in 88 court cases. In the last four calendar years, the Department has been involved 269 cases.  The total costs incurred to date for finalised and ongoing cases is £2,594,048.95* .*Please note the costs provided refer to court action taken against the Department and includes costs for legal advice invoiced to date and court awarded costs for concluded cases. They do not include staff time, consumables, T&S or other resource requirements necessary for the administration of any cases.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many river pollution incidents in North Down in the last two years have had the source of the incident identified.
answer_text: I refer you to the answer provided to AQW 9314/22-27 which relates to water pollution issues in the North Down constituency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their priorities for the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: We have previously spoken about a number of priorities including: Childcare, Reducing Hospital Waiting Lists, Tackling Violence against women and girls, Special Educational Needs, Lough Neagh, Housing, and Developing a globally competitive economy. A considerable amount of other work is also underway on a range of issues.We are working at pace to develop a Programme for Government.  We will update the Assembly on this in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action is being taken to address the condition of Castle Park Road, Bangor.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a very difficult financial position with a number of years of underfunding. That has had a significant impact on the ability to deliver essential maintenance functions.Whilst my officials and maintenance crews have worked very hard to maintain the road network, they have, nonetheless, been forced to concentrate only on the highest-priority repairs. To be clear, this is not a position that any of us want to be in and does not represent good maintenance practice.In response to this situation, I recently addressed the Assembly to announce the allocation of an extra £1million to target areas of highest priority with small scale resurfacing schemes, designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective.I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council which will be published once budgets are finalised.Castle Park Road will continue to be inspected as part of planned routine inspections and any actionable defects will be noted and instructed for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many fines have been issued for river pollution incidents in North Down in the last two years.
answer_text: I refer you to the answer provided to AQW 9314/22-27 which relates to water pollution issues in the North Down constituency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Moss Road, Millisle, resurfacing programme.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council in due course.My Department will continue to monitor this area and all defects meeting current intervention levels shall be recorded for repairs in line with set maintenance policy guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether mobile safety camera vehicles are authorised to park illegally when measuring and recording vehicle speeds.
answer_text: In relation to the traffic restrictions managed by my Department, when a Safety Camera vehicle is being used for police purposes, Article 4(g) of the Roads (Restriction of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 1982 applies. This states: “Nothing in Article 3 shall render it unlawful to cause or permit a vehicle to wait in a public road for so long as may be necessary to enable the vehicle to be used for fire brigade, ambulance, police or customs purposes”.Safety Camera vehicles are operated by the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership who would be able to comment on the parking guidance given to their drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions his Department is taking to address benefit fraud.
answer_text: The Department’s Benefit Security Division takes the lead in driving counter fraud activity by employing a multi-layered approach to suspected benefit fraud. Actions range from telephone interventions to full criminal investigations for the most serious fraud, and the instigation of legal proceedings where appropriate. The Department has a robust strategy in place to tackle benefit fraud and analyses outcomes and emerging risks on an ongoing basis to ensure that resources are deployed to address areas of highest risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he is taking to review pay and grading for non-teaching support staff.
answer_text: A revised business case for the Pay & Grading Review for school support staff has been submitted to the Department of Finance (DoF) and my officials will work with their DoF counterparts to progress its approval.  Given the significant cost associated with the Pay & Grading Review, my Department will need additional funding from the Executive in order to implement its recommendations.  I will therefore be bidding for this funding during the Executive’s Budget 2024-25 process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the grants administered by his Department that farmers can apply for.
answer_text: My Department is currently administering the following grants for which farmers, who meet the relevant eligibility criteria, can apply:Forest Protection Scheme aimed at providing support for restoration of forests following an ash dieback outbreak. Grants are available for forest owners, including farmers and landowners. Woodland Investment Grant in place to support sustainable forest management including the improvement of the environmental value of woodlands and building resilience to climate change through restocking. Grants are available for farmers and landowners. Area Based Schemes (2024) which offer several payments to farm businesses based on the land that they farm, or actions they take on their land.More information, including how to apply for each of the aforementioned opportunities can be found online, on the DAERA grants and funding hub www.daera-ni.gov.uk/grants-and-funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the waiting time for cavity wall insulation work to be carried out by the Housing Executive (i) in North Down; and (ii) all constituencies.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:The Housing Executive is in the process of developing a cavity wall insulation programme as a proof of concept to 300 dwellings across all three Regions. The best method of delivery is currently being considered by the Housing Executive’s Procurement Section.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what his Department is doing to protect badger setts from badger baiting.
answer_text: Badgers are afforded protection in Northern Ireland under the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) has responsibility to investigate breaches of the Wildlife Order. The PSNI works closely with DAERA officials, who have relevant scientific knowledge, to gather information which assists prosecutions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to implement the recommendations from the Angiolini Inquiry report.
answer_text: I can confirm that the detail and recommendations contained within the Angiolini Inquiry report are actively being considered by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI). Discussions have been ongoing with the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB) on these matters.  Further, my Department will engage local criminal justice organisations to address matters which extend more widely within the criminal justice system.  The implementation of many of these issues is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I intend to respect both the Chief Constable’s independence and the role of the Policing Board.I have every confidence that the recommendations made by the inquiry will be given thorough consideration and handled appropriately by the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when potholes on Southwell Road, Bangor, will be fixed.
answer_text: Southwell Road was last inspected on 6th December 2023 with all defects that met the criteria in accordance with the current limited service maintenance policy, identified and instructed for repair.My Department will continue to monitor this area and repair defects in accordance with the current policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when potholes on Hawthorne Drive, Bangor, will be fixed.
answer_text: Hawthorne Drive was last inspected on 3rd February 2024 with all defects that met the criteria in accordance with the current limited service maintenance policy, identified and instructed for repair.My Department will continue to monitor this area and repair defects in accordance with the current policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to support the stroke workforce to deliver National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines on stroke rehabilitation, which now recommends at least three hours of needs-based rehabilitation per day following a stroke.
answer_text: Priority six in the Stroke Action Plan sets out a commitment to undertake a Stroke Workforce Review. The Review, which will identify the future workforce required to deliver a high-quality stroke service, will be informed by best practice guidance including the guidelines from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence on stroke rehabilitation.  While there has been some delay due to funding and resource pressures it is now hoped that the Review will commence later this year.In the interim, consideration is also being given to the potential use of technology to supplement statutory rehabilitation provision. This includes the use of virtual reality in the delivery of Occupational Therapy and the development of an app to support Speech and Language Therapy. It is important to note, however, that the introduction of such technology is subject to additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many prison officers (i) qualified for; and (ii) accepted the NI Prison Service voluntary early retirement scheme offered in 2012.
answer_text: The Voluntary Early Retirement (VER) Scheme was open to permanent staff in the Governor, Principal Officer, Senior Officer, and Main Grade Officer grades who were aged 50 or over at 8 November 2011.  There were (i) 645 staff in eligible grades aged 50 or over at 8 November 2011 who qualified and could have applied for consideration and (ii) 520 staff who accepted and left the Northern Ireland Prison Service under the terms of the VER Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether all prison officers, who accepted the NI Prison Service voluntary early retirement scheme in 2012, have received all of their redundancy payment, including (i) holiday pay; (ii) additional voluntary hours worked; and (iii) the correct tax deductions.
answer_text: All prison officers who left under the terms of the Voluntary Early Retirement Scheme received the payments they were due from the Northern Ireland Prison Service including compensation payments, payments for untaken annual leave, payment for overtime and additional voluntary hours. Staff had the relevant policies applied to them at the time of their departure, including deductions at the relevant rate in relation to taxation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there are any court cases pending over the NI Prison Service voluntary early retirement scheme.
answer_text: A High Court writ was lodged by a number of former prison officers in respect of the Voluntary Early Retirement Scheme. In light of this, it would not be appropriate to comment any further.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who NI Water sold Portavoe reservoir to.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what help his Department can provide food banks in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: My Department has currently no direct funding relationship with foodbanks and there are currently no funding programmes open.  The Department provides funding for Social Supermarkets, which are operated by community groups who offer access to food provision along with additional advice and support to address the underlying causes of food poverty.  The funding for Social Supermarkets is administered by local Councils, and there may be an opportunity in the future for organisations tackling food poverty across Ards and North Down Borough Council to access that.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what help his Department can provide in relation to the (i) restoration; and (ii) exhibition of the Sir Samuel Kelly Lifeboat in Donaghadee.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Museums Council is the lead body supporting local museums on behalf of my Department. Any support available in relation to restoration or exhibition would be for the Northern Ireland Museums Council to consider.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what support their Department can provide to Kilcooley Women’s Centre.
answer_text: The Executive Office supports a variety of programmes that seek to provide funding and other forms of assistance for the benefit of local groups. Further information on such programmes can be found on the Executive Office website: https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/tbuc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all grants that farmers can avail of through his Department.
answer_text: The following grants are currently available to farmers who meet the relevant eligibility criteria:Forest Protection Scheme aimed at providing support for restoration of forests following an ash dieback outbreak. Grants are available for forest owners, including farmers and landowners. Woodland Investment Grant in place to support sustainable forest management including the improvement of the environmental value of woodlands and building resilience to climate change through restocking. Grants are available for farmers and landowners. Area Based Schemes (2024) which offer several payments to farm businesses based on the land that they farm, or actions they take on their land.More information, including how to apply for each of the aforementioned opportunities can be found online, on the DAERA grants and funding hub www.daera-ni.gov.uk/grants-and-funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail all grants that community organisations can avail of through his Department.
answer_text: My Department supports community organisations under a wide range of grant programmes including Neighbourhood Renewal and voluntary and community sector infrastructure support programmes.  There are no grant programmes currently open to new applications.  My Department provides funding to NICVA to provide an up-to-date and comprehensive database of all funding schemes for voluntary and community organisations. The ‘GrantTracker’ funding database can be accessed at www.nicva.org/services/fundraising.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what measures are in place to ensure that the Central Good Relations Fund is distributed to groups across all constituencies.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to address child poverty.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities including our young people, right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and addressing the issues faced by children and young people remains a key focus of this ongoing work. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to encourage people in debt to seek advice and help.
answer_text: My department funds Advice NI and its affiliated front-line community-based centres to deliver a free, independent, and impartial debt advice service. Through extensive regular, regional and local promotion, people are encouraged to seek debt advice at the earliest opportunity to ensure the full range of options are available to them in assisting with problem debt.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans for the future of the Discretionary Support Scheme.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the Discretionary Support Scheme in assisting vulnerable people in a crisis situation.An Independent Review of the Scheme was conducted in 2021, with a number of recommendations made. These recommendations will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to reduce the Universal Credit payment time from the current five week timescale.
answer_text: The arrangements relating to Universal Credit assessment periods are set out in Regulation 22(1) of the Universal Credit Regulations (NI) 2016 and operated under the principle of parity.  Any proposal to change the assessment period would require legislative and system change, with consequential financial implications.		In Northern Ireland, financial support may be available to those customers who make a claim to Universal Credit from the Universal Credit Contingency Fund in the form of a non-repayable grant. 		Information about the Contingency Fund grant is provided to everyone making a Universal Credit claim through a welcome message on their online journal account. This message also includes a link to the Contingency Fund application form.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people have yet to move across from legacy benefits to Universal Credit.
answer_text: At November 2023, the latest published data, there were 60k households in Northern Ireland in receipt of a legacy benefit/tax credit who are due to receive a migration notice asking them to move to Universal Credit (UC) by March 2025.  To date, 27k migration notices have been issued. It is important to note that claimants have 3 months to make their UC claim from the date on their migration notice.There are currently 53k households in Northern Ireland in receipt of Employment and Support Allowance (Income Related) only and Employment and Support Allowance (Income Related) with Housing Benefit who will not move to Universal Credit until 2028/29.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action he intends to take to address the difficulties faced by people who are trying to book an MOT appointment through the online booking system.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved through a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available. In 2023, there were only seven days in the year when DVA did not offer MOT appointments. A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition also launched on 19 February and will be used to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres. Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.The DVA appreciates that in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.The PSNI has agreed not to penalise drivers whose MOT has expired so long as:your vehicle is in a roadworthy and safe condition and doesn’t have a statutory off-road notification (SORN) declarationyour vehicle is properly insuredyou can prove (by showing an MOT appointment notification/ reminder, either in electronic or paper form) that a test appointment has been booked for your vehicleAll other vehicle roadworthiness and insurance offences will be dealt with in the usual way.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers. The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; keep checking the booking system for an appointment before their MOT expires. If they get to within ten days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should complete the short notice appointment request form and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at Change, cancel or view your MOT/ vehicle test appointment online | nidirect.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's actions to create more NHS dental practices.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and are not required to register new patients. The Department does not commission either Health Service places in practices or new practices. Instead dentists are paid fees and allowances in accordance with the dental treatments they undertake for Health Service patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what is the legal position for drivers who are unable to book an MOT appointment due to appointments not being available, but need to use their vehicle to travel to their place of employment.
