question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the future of the cafe at Ballycopeland Windmill visitor centre, Millisle.
answer_text: The challenge of finding a viable operating model for the site, within a difficult budgetary environment, is one that I have instructed my officials to examine further. They are assembling a business case to assess a number of options.Decisions following the review may at that stage remain subject to confirmation of budget for 2024/25.The urgency reflects my appreciation both of the importance of my Department’s state care monuments to local communities and their role in attracting visitors and of supporting people with disabilities to move closer, into and sustain employment through a range or pre-employment and in-work support provision.Both of these elements of my Department’s responsibilities come together with regard to Ballycopeland Windmill, a state care monument where Ulster Supported Employment Limited (USEL) manage the facilities including the Ability Café.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being taken to ensure there are no flooding incidents at Grandmere Park, Bangor.
answer_text: It is not possible to guarantee that there will be no flooding incidents at Grandmere Park, Bangor. However, my Department’s Rivers Directorate will examine options to alleviate the risk of fluvial flooding at this location as part of a wider Bangor Significant Flood Risk Area Pre-feasibility Study, which is on the Department’s Rivers Directorate 10-year capital works programme. The study will be commissioned to an external flood risk consultant based on its current position on the prioritised programme. My Department will continue to inspect the Cotton River annually and carry out any maintenance identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place to recruit more part-time firefighters in Holywood.
answer_text: NIFRS has recently appointed two On-Call Firefighters to Holywood on 11 March 2024 and intends to administer a further competition later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why the houses bought by his Department at Kings Street, Bangor, have not been demolished as part of the Queens Parade project.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely with Ards and North Down Borough Council, and the developer, Bangor Marine, to bring forward plans for the development of the Queen’s Parade site by way of a Development Agreement. Consequently, the responsibility for the site-enabling and site-preparation works, including the demolition of the houses on King Street, sits with the appointed developer.  Bangor Marine has started clearing trees and shrubbery from the Marine Gardens part of the site in advance of the nesting season, to accommodate commencement of the construction of Phase 1 of the scheme, the public realm works, which is programmed to begin later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many weapons have been discovered in prisons in the last three years.
answer_text: One hundred and thirty two weapons have been recovered in prisons in the last three years.  These were fashioned from pool cues and furniture or other materials available within the prison.The harm caused to prisoners and staff resulting from weapons is low and the prison service work diligently in line with their Search Strategy to ensure the safety of all who live and work in our prisons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current prison population size; and (ii) how that compares with (a) England; (b) Scotland; and (c) Wales.
answer_text: A comparison of the prison populations at 8 March 2024 in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and England and Wales is set out in the table below:Northern IrelandScotlandEngland & WalesPrison Population1,9067,94888,220Prisoners per 100,000 population98.5144.6143.5Sentenced63%77%81%Unsentenced37%23%19%Males95%96%96%Females5%4%4%The prison population in Northern Ireland is comparatively smaller than other jurisdictions, with a lower rate of imprisonment.  However the proportion of people in prison who are unsentenced is higher.Within the Department of Justice, we have established a Prison Population Oversight Group.  This seeks to bring together senior leaders from all relevant organisations, both within the Justice sector and beyond, to take collective strategic responsibility for identifying and implementing measures to limit or mitigate the impact of the rise in the prison population in Northern Ireland, including in respect of remand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many prison escape attempts have been made in the last three years.
answer_text: There have been no prison escape attempts from custody in the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many PSNI officers have been assaulted in the line of duty in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not collect or hold this information.  You may, therefore, wish to request this information directly from the PSNI.However, I wish to place on record my condemnation of all attacks on police officers.  As the high number of assaults on officers is a concerning issue, I intend to bring forward new legislation to reform the law on sentencing by increasing the maximum sentence for such attacks and making serious assaults on front line workers a statutory aggravating factor.  Police officers, and indeed any public servant, who are assaulted while doing their job must have the full support of the criminal justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are there to address the recent flooding incidents in North Down.
answer_text: My Department has responded to multiple recent flooding incidents over recent months in North Down, most notably within the Bangor area. The fluvial flooding experienced will be examined as part of a wider Bangor Significant Flood Risk Area Pre-feasibility Study, which is already on my Department’s Rivers Directorate 10-year capital works programme. The study will be commissioned to an external flood risk consultant based on its current position on the prioritised programme and will consider the recent flood events in Autumn 2023. The study will aim to identify if there are any viable flood alleviation options to mitigate flood risk within Bangor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are there to upgrade the sewerage infrastructure at Kinnegar, Holywood.
answer_text: The query you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans for Grey Point Fort, Helen's Bay.
answer_text: The Department have bid for monies to undertake a feasibility study in 24/25. If this report can proceed it will provide a roadmap for future development. At this time, I can only assure you of our commitment to progress plans for the fort once funding becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to have a train halt at George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), is considering how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.Enhancing the integration of rail with other transport modes is one of 13 objectives within the draft Review. In support of this objective, the draft Review recommends improving existing rail-airport connections at George Best Belfast City Airport. Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. However, prior to implementing any recommendations, including those in relation to rail connectivity to George Best Belfast City Airport, further work will be required to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions. I can confirm that Translink has commenced a Feasibility Study which considers options for accessibility and connectivity to the City Airport. The study includes engagement with both Belfast City Airport and my Department’s Roads Service to develop options for future connections to the airport including the projected costs for each identified option. The feasibility study is due to be completed by the end of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are there to introduce pedestrian crossing islands on Belfast Road, Bangor.
answer_text: A pedestrian survey has previously been carried out at the Belfast Road, Bangor using the criteria set out in the Department’s current policy document: RSPPG E005 (Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures), which can be viewed at: RsPPG_E005 Controlled Crossings : Assessment Procedures (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk). The survey confirmed the area met the Department’s criteria for the installation of a controlled crossing.  However, the existing constraints at this site, including property driveways, road layout, and parking patterns along the road, meant officials were unable to identify a suitable location for a controlled crossing.Alternative options for an uncontrolled crossing were also explored, such as refuge islands which would provide safe passage for pedestrians crossing the road.  Unfortunately, it was not possible to provide a safe space on the road for these measures when taking into account of the existing constraints previously mentioned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has plans to increase the amount of fines for those who have cut down trees that have a Tree Preservation Order.
answer_text: The current penalties for the contravention of tree preservation orders were introduced, under the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, to create a more significant deterrent by increasing the maximum level of fines from £30,000 to £100,000. This change came as a result of a proposed amendment to the Planning Bill by the then Environment Committee, which was accepted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many claims have been recorded for damage to vehicles as a result of potholes, in the last three years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online or via the at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019  Table 5.1 in the above link provides details of vehicle damage claims received during the last five full financial years due to potholes and other defects on the public road network.  I would, however, advise the member and other members using the above link to be cognisant of the caveats noted in respect to the information provided on roads related public liability claims.The link to this information is also available via the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the upgrade of the crossroads at Upper Gransha Road, Killaughey Road, Ballyblack Road and Movilla Road.
answer_text: I am aware there have been a number of collisions at this location over the last number of years. My Department has previously implemented a number of collision remedial measures at this location including provision of enhanced warning and directional signs, advanced slow road markings and high friction surfacing, all of which combine to enhance driver awareness and safety on the approaches to this junction.Officials have engaged the Department’s consultant partner to carry out a feasibility study to explore further potential road safety improvements for this busy staggered junction.  This study is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are there to reduce the speed limit for the A48 Cotton Road.
answer_text: When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, the Department’s policy and procedures guide entitled “Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland” is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads. This takes into account factors such as the characteristic of the road, the speed of existing traffic, the degree of frontage development, and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.A speed limit review was undertaken in 2022 by the Department which concluded that the current speed limit on the A48 Cotton Road is in line with our current policy document and considered to be appropriate. However, since this review the PSNI have updated the collision data for this area, therefore, the Department intends to re-assess the speed limit at The Cotton, this should be completed over the next number of months.The Department has reviewed the existing signs and road markings along this stretch of Cotton Road and have provided slow road markings either side of the junction of Bailie Road.  The Department will also be enhancing the existing junction warning signage at this location. Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding, or parking which causes an obstruction to other road (and footway) users should be directed to the police using the non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the pedestrian crossing for the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor, for St Columbanus' College.
answer_text: The installation of a new Puffin Crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor is identified in the Ards and North Down Borough Council Annual Report 2023/24, for the work to be completed during the 2024/25 financial year. Link to the report; Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2023/2024 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)The exact timing for the delivery of this scheme, subject to confirmation of funding, will be agreed in consultation with the Department’s contractor, taking into account the availability of resources and capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount paid out in claims for damage to vehicles as a result of (i) potholes; and (ii) general road defects, in the last three years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to claims can be found online at:Road Asset Maintenance Directorate - Management Information on roads related claims for the period 2018/2019 to 2022/2023 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Table 7.1 in the above link provides details of the vehicle damage claims expenditure resulting from road defects and other road issues during the last three full financial years.  However, please note as the NICS accounting system does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why his Department has not pursued the creation of a public right of way through Portavoe.
answer_text: NI Water transferred the ownership of its lands of former reservoir site at Portavoe to private ownership June 2020.  The responsibility to assert, protect and keep open public rights of way in Northern Ireland is a matter for local councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department maintains control of the forest at Portavoe.
answer_text: The ownership of the forested land at Portavoe transferred from NI Water to the original landowners, or their successors, in June 2020.  Prior to the transfer, my Department managed the trees on the NI Water site under a management agreement. The transfer of the land to the new owners, allows for my Department to continue to manage the trees, however it does not extend the Department’s responsibilities in respect of site management or access matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the woodlands at Portavoe are maintained and controlled by his Department; and (ii) whether the public can access these woodlands.
answer_text: Further to my reply to AWQ 8118 22-27, I can confirm that my Department’s Forest Service continues to manage the trees at Portavoe. However, as my Department is not the landowner it does not control the access to or use of the woodlands by the public.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether all lands purchased have to be declared through the Land Registry office.
answer_text: Since 2003, a buyer of unregistered land must register the title to the property in Land Registry under the Compulsory First Registration process.  The solicitor for the purchaser makes the application to Land Registry and certifies the title.  On completion of the registration, the title will be held thereafter in Land Registry.Under Section 34 of the Land Registration Act (NI) 1970 if a buyer of registered land fails to register the deed of purchase in Land Registry, the deed does not operate to transfer the land.  The purchaser would not therefore be registered as the legal owner of the land and would be unable to sell or mortgage the property.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why access to fishing rights to Portavoe were refused by his Department.
answer_text: In 2014, my Department signed an agreement with NI Water to manage fishing rights at 28 reservoirs, including Portavoe.  In 2020, Portavoe Reservoir was sold to Portavoe Estates Ltd by NI Water. The Department's fishing rights agreement with NI Water was still in effect until 2024, but the new owner wished to keep the site private. The rights were due to transfer to the new owner in December 2024.The former Minister’s instruction was to re-establish access to the reservoir for use as part of the Public Angling Estate however, if this wasn’t possible, to transfer the fishing rights for an appropriate payment as advised by Land Property Services (LPS). After prolonged negotiation with the site owner, the fishing rights were transferred to the new owner for the remaining time left on the fishing rights,for a payment (set by LPS) in September 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans his Department has to assist in getting young people into employment in North Down.
answer_text: My department offers a range of services, programmes and schemes to support young people across Northern Ireland into employment.Work Coaches across NI provide advice and guidance to all benefit claimants, including young people. This includes help with applying for jobs, referral to my department’s JobStart Scheme and Work Experience Programme; or signposting to sector-based academies; traineeships and apprenticeships, where appropriate. My department’s JobStart Scheme is aimed at young people aged 16 to 24 who are at risk of falling into long-term unemployment by providing job opportunities offering 25 hours of paid employment at the national minimum wage for six or nine months with a view to improving employability and occupational skills, in order to sustain employment. In the Ards and North Down council area 72 employers have registered for the JobStart Scheme offering 113 job opportunities, and there are 54 young people currently participating.  The Work Experience Programme offers an Opportunity Guarantee strand specifically for young people with a guaranteed interview with the employer on completion of the opportunity. My department has also funded the Ards and North Down Labour Market Partnership (LMP) since 2021. Their LMP Action Plan includes a range of projects to help increase employability and skills to participants locally, including young people.The LMP has organised a number of events across the borough to promote employability and skills, including a Careers Convention in Bangor, targeted primarily at young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost to run the Minor Injury Unit at the Ulster Hospital in (i) 2020; (ii) 2021; (iii) 2022; and (iv) 2023.
answer_text: The Minor Injury Unit at the Ulster Hospital is co-located and shares costs with the Ulster Hospital’s Emergency Department (ED). Unfortunately, the Trust is unable provide separate running costs for the Minor Injury Unit as these are included within the overall cost of running the ED.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the benefits of urban wildlife.
answer_text: Providing for urban wildlife is important for increasing biodiversity. The provision of green spaces and connection of habitats within towns and cities can provide additional nesting and shelter options for birds, mammals and insects. Urban Wildlife also provides an important connection to the natural world for many people, with studies showing benefits for mental health and wellbeing. Urban wildlife plays a key role in providing a range of ecosystem services, e.g.  birds and bats help control insect populations, reducing the need for pesticides; bees and other pollinators contribute to urban garden, allotments and green spaces by aiding in plant reproduction; trees and other vegetation help with temperature regulation, absorbing air pollutants and blocking noise pollution.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to continue a specialist therapeutic service for victims of sexual violence and abuse, following the cancellation of the Nexus NI contract.
answer_text: On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for this specialist counselling service for victims of sexual abuse would be extended for a further 12 months.I advised that the one year extension would allow proper consideration and a decision on and implementation of the best way forward for counselling of this nature.  The planned ending of this contract was not a cost saving measure.  The intention has always been to use the funding to continue to support people who are victims and survivors of sexual abuse in line with the draft domestic and sexual violence strategy 2023-2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many PSNI officers are due to retire in 2024.
answer_text: Police officer numbers are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many officers need to be recruited to bring the PSNI up to its maximum operational level.
answer_text: Police officer numbers and recruitment decisions are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many (i) police officers; and (ii) civilian staff have moved from their homes as a result of recent data breaches.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by my Department.  You may, therefore, wish to address your question directly to the Chief Constable given it relates to an operational matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what hedgerow conservation measures are currently in place for agricultural land.
answer_text: There are several measures aimed at hedgerow conservation within the Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS). The Planting New Hedgerows option includes both the planting of a new hedge made up of native species and capital works (stockproof fencing) to protect the new hedge. New hedges fix carbon dioxide in photosynthesis and prevent soil erosion caused by flooding. Planting new hedges also enhances biodiversity, links wildlife habitats, absorbs nutrients, enhances the traditional landscape of the countryside, provides shelter for livestock, reduces noise and reduces the contact between herds limiting the spread of disease. Since 2018, 600km of new hedgerows have been planted under this option. The Hedge Laying option aims to maintain the biodiversity, structure and landscape patterns of field boundaries in the countryside. Since 2018, 35km of hedgerows have been enhanced under this option. Within EFS Tranches 1 and 2 the Tree Enhanced Boundaries option was available. This option increased the wildlife value of existing field boundaries by planting native hedgerow trees and putting up woodcrete bat boxes. This option also linked existing farmland habitats and enhanced the landscape patterns of hedgerows in the countryside. Since 2018, over 400km of hedgerows have been enhanced under this option.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the potholes on Castle Park Road, Bangor, will be repaired.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a very difficult financial position with a number of years of underfunding. That has had a significant impact on the ability to deliver essential maintenance functions.Whilst my officials and maintenance crews have worked very hard to maintain the road network, they have, nonetheless, been forced to concentrate only on the highest-priority repairs. To be clear, this is not a position that any of us want to be in and does not represent good maintenance practice.In response to this situation, I recently addressed the Assembly to announce the allocation of an extra £1million to target areas of highest priority with small scale resurfacing schemes, designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective.Further to that, on 25 February 2024, I announced an additional £8.1million of funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to address this issue. Castle Park Road, Bangor will be considered for inclusion in the Department’s future resurfacing programme subject to budget availability and its relative priority when compared to the many other roads in need of investment. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council which will be published once budgets are finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have had to wait in an ambulance whilst awaiting assessment in the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital, in the last four months.
answer_text: Information on the number of people who have had to wait in an ambulance whilst awaiting assessment in the Emergency Department (ED) at the Ulster hospital is not held centrally. However, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) has provided a response in relation to patient handovers from ambulance staff to ED staff at the Ulster hospital.Table 1 overleaf shows the number of patients handed over at the Ulster hospital ED each month from October 2023 to January 2024, broken down by handover time band. Safe ambulance handover standards indicate that a patient should be handed over to waiting clinicians within 15 minutes of arrival at the receiving ED. NIAS advise that the patients as indicated within Table 1, waited significantly more than would be acceptable.Table 1: Number of Patients Handed Over from Ambulance staff to ED Staff at the Ulster Hospital, by handover time band (October 2023 – January 2024)Month/Year< 1 Hour1 - 2 Hours> 2 HoursTotalOct-23           555            224            565         1,344 Nov-23           503            199            579         1,281 Dec-23           581            213            524         1,318 Jan-24           493            182            578         1,253 Total        2,132            818         2,246         5,196 Source: Northern Ireland Ambulance ServiceNIAS add that patient handover delays from ambulance staff to ED staff and consequent poor ambulance response times, remains a significant cause of concern for the Trust. Reports show that significant risk to urgent and emergency care patients was that of delayed ambulance handovers. NIAS continues to work with all Trusts and the Department of Health, to develop solutions to improve handover times.In December 2023, NIAS experienced a total of 12,202 lost hours due to delayed handovers. This is the equivalent of 33 shifts per day where crews are waiting with patients outside EDs; 29% of NIAS planned capacity. These lost hours were experienced from 9,218 instances where crews waited longer than 15mins to handover their patient at EDs. 3,887 handovers took longer than an hour in December 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time to be fully assessed at the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: Information on the current average waiting time to be fully assessed at the Ulster Emergency Department (ED) is not available.However, information on the median time spent in ED from arrival to discharge or admission at the Ulster ED is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics’ report, the most recent of which covers the period October to December 2023, and is available to view or download at: Emergency care waiting times | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)Information on the time spent in Ulster ED from arrival to discharge or admission to hospital is provided in Table 1 overleaf. This information is based on new and unplanned review attendances only.A new attendance refers to the first unplanned attendance by a patient for a new clinical condition at an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU). An unplanned review attendance relates to any patient who returns to an ED / MIU without written instruction, for the same or related clinical condition within 30 days of the first (initial) attendance.Time to discharge or admission is the total length of time a patient spends in an ED from arrival to discharge or admission to hospital.Please note that urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust is not yet available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records following the launch of the Encompass programme.Table 1: Median Time Spent in Ulster ED from Arrival to Discharge or Admission (October – December 2023)1OctoberNovemberDecemberTime to Discharge (HH:MM)05:1405:46-Time to Admission (HH:MM)16:2719:03-Source: Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts1 Figures for November 2023 are based on clinical care episodes completed up to and including 8th November 2023.

answer_text: The Minor Injuries Unit at the Ulster Hospital is currently located within the previous Emergency Department at the front of the Ulster Hospital site. The building is fully compliant and maintained in accordance with relevant standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many members of staff transferred from the (i) Bangor; and (ii) Ards Minor Injury Units to the Ulster Hospital Minor Injury Unit.
answer_text: I refer you to the figures provided in response to AQW 6910/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans for the old Minor Injury Unit site at Newtownards Hospital.
answer_text: The Minor Injuries Unit Building at Ards Community Hospital has reached the end of its serviceable life. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is currently reviewing options for future development at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assistance his Department can provide to the Kilcooley Women's Education Centre to support their social supermarket.
answer_text: My Department’s Social Supermarket funding is administered by local Councils.  I can confirm that the social supermarket at the Kilcooley Women’s Education Centre received £7,000 of funding in 2022/23 and £9,000 in 2023/24 from the local council.  In terms of future funding I would encourage the Centre to continue to engage with Ards and North Down Borough Council. In addition, I have asked my officials to engage with the organisation to explore if there is any other practical support that can be offered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Priory and Springhill GP Practice, Bangor, will no longer prevent people from transferring to a different GP practice.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group wrote to all GP Practices on 25 October 2023 confirming that as Northern Ireland entered into another winter period, and that practices were actively participating in the Flu and Covid-19 vaccination programmes, a temporary freeze on free movement of patients between all GP Practices would be applied from 1st November 2023 to 31 March 2024. With effect from 1 April 2024, this temporary freeze on free movement of patients will cease. Patients of Priory and Springhill GP Practices will then be able to register with a different GP Practice if they reside within the practice’s boundary area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of social worker vacancies; and (ii) how he plans to fill those vacancies.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have reported the following social worker vacancies as of 31st December 2023;BHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTTOTALSocial Worker Vacancies 14385827891479My Department has been working collaboratively with the Trusts and other stakeholders to address the high level of vacancies in social work services. In an effort to stabilise the HSC social work workforce, a policy decision was taken to cease the use of recruitment agency social workers by the end of June 2023. Former recruitment agency social workers were encouraged to apply for posts in the Trusts and many of them availed of that opportunity. Between June and September 2023, Trusts also undertook a large recruitment exercise to attract almost 200 newly qualified social workers into vacant Trust posts. A similar exercise will be undertaken in 2024.Over the past three years my Department has also commissioned an additional 25 training places for social workers with the Open University NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are plans to uplift the Community Festival Fund to local councils.
answer_text: The Budget for 2024-25 has not been settled. Future funding for the Community Festivals Fund will be considered, along with all the other valuable calls for investment, in the light of decisions on the budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the news that the UK government has removed £150 million of previously earmarked funding through Fresh Start capital funding, what impact this has on the new school build for Bangor Central Primary School.
answer_text: In light of my statutory duties to integrated and shared education, I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract, including Bangor Central Integrated Primary School, into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in planning. This project is in the final stage of design with a planning application submitted in July 2022.  The project will proceed to construction when affordable within the wider education budget context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he plans to reach net zero by 2050.
answer_text: During my recent Economic Vision statement, I stated that one of my strategic objectives is to reduce carbon emissions.  The Energy Strategy – The Path to Net Zero Energy, which was agreed by the Executive in 2021, established the pathway to deliver the 2050 vision of net zero carbon emissions. The Energy Strategy is delivering self-sufficiency in affordable, renewable energy for everyone in this region. To provide visibility on how we are reaching net zero, my Department is publishing annual action plans and publishing an annual report on progress. To date reports have been published for the years 2022 and 2023. The Action Plan for 2024 is currently being finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6653/22-27, what his plans are to address this and build new homes in North Down.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to Homelessness and for the building of new social homes in North Down.  This is an operational matter for the NIHE and you should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to resolve the shortage in the recruitment of nurses.
answer_text: I remain committed to investing in the workforce of the future. My Department has undertaken sustained investment in pre-registration nursing and midwifery training over recent years, reaching an all-time high annual intake of 1335 places, across local universities, in recent years. This investment is bearing fruit, with a 14.9% increase in nursing and midwifery staff in post across the HSC between March 2018 and March 2023.The most recently released HSC workforce statistics show that the current active vacancy rate for nurses and midwives is 6.2%.  This is the lowest level of vacancy recorded since these statistics began being reported in 2017.At the same time the current workforce data provided from the NMC register for Northern Ireland indicates that the retention rate is steady. The number of leavers since April 2023 equates to approximately 1.5 percent of the register, which is the same as five years ago.However, I am not complacent. I fully acknowledge the staffing pressures faced across the HSC system and will shortly be considering proposals from my officials for education and training commissioning for 2024/25 of pre-registration nursing programmes in the context of the funding made available to my Department from the Northern Ireland Executive, which I anticipate will be challenging.In addition, the Nursing and Midwifery Retention Report was published in March 2022, outlining a series of recommendations to support nurses and midwives to remain in their posts.  My officials will continue to work with employers to ensure that those recommendations are implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to address the financial funding for teacher training, to bring it in line with funding provided in England.
answer_text: I understand your question relates to bursary schemes for Initial Teacher Education (ITE), so I am responding on this basis. My Department does not currently operate any bursary schemes for ITE programmes in Northern Ireland as (unlike other parts of the United Kingdom); there has traditionally been an oversupply in the overall number of teachers in Northern Ireland.We are, however, aware of recent concerns about teacher supply issues, particularly in some post-primary subjects. We recognise the need to develop a sector-wide approach to strategic teacher workforce planning. My Department therefore plans to engage with key stakeholders to understand better any workforce shortages and gaps. While consideration may be given to the introduction of ITE bursary schemes to address specific teacher shortages, such schemes would have to be tightly targeted and would remain subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Market Street, Bangor, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.  Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large-scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered and the development of programmes for this is underway. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.
