This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large-scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered and the development of programmes for this is underway. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when (i) Shore Road; (ii) Strand Avenue; and (iii) Seaview Avenue, Millisle, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.  Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large-scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered and the development of programmes for this is underway. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the rationale for spending over £400,000 on the resurfacing scheme for the A2 Groomsport Road roundabout, Bangor.
answer_text: There are a number of factors used when determining the need for road resurfacing, including road usage, general surface condition, structural deformation, public enquiries and public liability claims.Groomsport Road Roundabout forms part of the busy A2 corridor and had started to show signs of structural failure so was included in the 2021/22 resurfacing programme. However it could not be completed due to contractor capacity issues prior to the expiry of their end of their contract November 2021.The scheme was planned for inclusion in the 2024/25 resurfacing programme subject to available resources. However, following an in year reallocation of capital funding the scheme has been brought forward into this 2023/24 year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how they ensure that Peace Plus Programme projects delivered through local councils will (i) be fully accountable; and (ii) achieve their aims.
answer_text: The Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB) is the Managing Authority for PEACE PLUS and is responsible for its implementation, management and oversight. SEUPB will review project activity and monitor outputs and impact throughout the programming period. Financial accountability is ensured by verifying that all expenditures are in line with approved project activities.		Each PEACE PLUS project application from local councils will be assessed, and conditionality placed, through a Letter of Offer, on the funding that is awarded. This will include achieving the agreed outcomes and activity as set out in the Programme. 		Council Action Plans will also be subject to a specific, independent impact evaluation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what review process is in place to ensure projects funded by the Peace Plus Programme are delivering outcomes that support peace and reconciliation.
answer_text: All PEACE PLUS projects must deliver against the overarching peace and reconciliation objectives of the Programme document and through their application, are expected to demonstrate how they will meet this requirement. 	The Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB) is responsible for management and implementation of the PEACE PLUS Programme and they will monitor and review projects on an ongoing basis to ensure outputs are achieved in line with agreed project targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a breakdown of financial contributions from other public bodies towards projects funded by the Peace Plus Programme.
answer_text: The PEACE PLUS programme is worth approximately £996m / €1.145bn. The financial contributions to the programme are: European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) funding provided by the British Government (£593m / €681m) and EU (£204m / €234m); andMatch funding provided by the Executive (£148m / €170m) and Irish Government (£51m / €59m).  Project partners may also bring their own private match funding contributions. The total value of these is not yet known as the programme is at an early stage of project assessment and selection.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how much Peace Plus Programme funding is allocated by the Executive Office.
answer_text: The Executive Office is the Accountable Department for PEACEPLUS Investment Areas 1.1 Co-designed Local Community Peace Action Plans (€110m/£95.7m); 1.3 Building Positive Relations (€35m/£30.4m); and 4.3 Victims and Survivors (€25m/£21.7m). These investment areas combined represent €170m (£147.82m) of investment, of which TEO will contribute €25.29m (£21.99m) in match funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reopen the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive scheme.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the required staff complement at the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The funded staffing level for nursing including health care assistants and registered nurses at the Ulster Hospital, Emergency Department (ED) is 150.99 Whole Time Equivalents (WTE)  With regard to Medical Staffing, the South Eastern Trust has emergency medicine led services on three hospital sites namely Downe, Lagan Valley and the Ulster. Medical staff work across all three sites.  The funded staffing level for all medical staffing across these sites, which includes Consultants, Junior Doctors and Specialty, Associate Specialist and Specialist (SAS) doctors is held in one cost centre.  The estimated proportion, however, of funded posts associated with the Ulster Hospital ED is 69.00 WTEs. The funded staffing level for administrative roles, which include reception staff, medical secretaries and patient trackers is 40.98 WTEs In addition to the posts listed above, the ED is supported by other staff groups who are not specifically assigned to ED. The following is not exhaustive but shows the range in support staff who deliver care in ED:·       Patient experience (Porters, Domestics & Catering)·       Allied Health Professions·       Pharmacy·       Laboratory·       Radiology·       Social work·       Mental health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place for the recently closed Minor Injury Unit at Newtownards Hospital.
answer_text: The Minor Injuries Unit building at Ards Community Hospital has reached the end of its serviceable life.  This was one of the factors driving change as outlined in the Trust’s consultation on The Future Provision of Urgent and Emergency Care Services.  The Trust is currently reviewing options for future development at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for a patient (i) to be seen; and (ii) to be treated at the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: Information on the average waiting time for a patient (i) to be seen; and (ii) to be treated at the Emergency Department (ED) at the Ulster is not available. However, information on the median and 95th percentile (i) time spent in ED from arrival to triage; and (ii) time spent in ED from arrival to discharge or admission at the Ulster ED is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics’ report, the most recent of which covers the period October to December 2023, and is available to view or download at: Emergency care waiting times | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)Information on the time from arrival to triage at the Ulster is provided in Table 1 overleaf, whilst information on the time from arrival to discharge or admission to hospital at the Ulster is provided in Table 2 overleaf. This information is based on new and unplanned review attendances only.A new attendance refers to the first unplanned attendance by a patient for a new clinical condition at an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU). An unplanned review attendance relates to any patient who returns to an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU) without written instruction, for the same or related clinical condition within 30 days of the first (initial) attendance.Time to triage refers to the time from arrival at ED to the start of a patient’s triage by a medical professional.Time to discharge or admission is the total length of time a patient spends in an ED from arrival to discharge or admission to hospital.Please note that urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust is not yet available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records following the launch of the Encompass programme.Table 1: Median and 95th Percentile Time from Arrival to Triage at the Ulster ED (October – December 2023)1OctoberNovemberDecemberTime to Triage (HH:MM)Median00:2500:30-95th Percentile03:1303:32-Source: Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts1 Figures for November 2023 are based on clinical care episodes completed up to and including 8th November 2023.Table 2: Median and 95th Percentile Time from Arrival to Discharge or Admission at the Ulster ED (October – December 2023)1OctoberNovemberDecemberTime to Discharge (HH:MM)Median05:1405:46-95th Percentile13:4715:50-Time to Admission (HH:MM)Median 16:2719:03-95th Percentile49:0650:24-Source: Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts1 Figures for November 2023 are based on clinical care episodes completed up to and including 8th November 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans for GP practices to return to a pre-pandemic delivery model.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that patients will be able to continue to access high quality, sustainable GP services now and in the future and is taking forward a wide range of work with stakeholders across the system to help achieve that.  GP services have been experiencing sustained, high levels of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic.  Practices have therefore continued to adapt how services are delivered in response to increasing pressures. In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example,  reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  It is essential that we continue to use of all the tools at our disposal to ensure that we can help our services to work as effectively as possible to meet the need for access to primary care support and treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who attended the Ulster Hospital Emergency Department, broken down for each month in 2023.
answer_text: Information on the number of new and unplanned review attendances at the Ulster emergency department (ED) is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics’ report which is available to view or download at: Emergency care waiting times | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)The number of new and unplanned review attendances at the Ulster ED in each month in 2023 has also been provided in Table 1 overleaf. Please note that the number of attendances does not equate to the number of patients treated, as a patient may attend an emergency care department(s) more than once during each month or year.A new attendance refers to the first unplanned attendance by a patient for a new clinical condition at an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU). An unplanned review attendance relates to any patient who returns to an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU) without written instruction, for the same or related clinical condition within 30 days of the first (initial) attendance.Table 1: Number of Attendances (New and Unplanned Reviews only) at the Ulster ED for each month in 20231YearMonthAttendances2023January8,057February8,068March9,526April8,828May9,267June9,515July8,625August9,360September7,975October7,847November2,031December-Source: Health and Social Care Trusts1 Figures for November 2023 are based on clinical care episodes completed up to and including 8th November 2023. Please note that urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust is not yet available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records following the launch of the Encompass programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of staff employed at the Ulster Hospital Minor Injury Unit, broken down by grade; and (ii) the required staff complement at the Ulster Hospital Minor Injury Unit, broken down by grade.
answer_text: The Minor Injury Unit (MIU) at the Ulster Hospital is a consultant led service, delivered by a highly skilled multidisciplinary team across 7 days per week.In relation to the current staffing complement, the Whole Time Equivalents (WTE) dedicated to MIU are:•	1 WTE x Band 8A First Contact Physiotherapist•	12.35 WTE x Band 7 nursing (2 additional starting in next month)•	2 WTE x Band 5 nursing•	2 WTE x Band 4 Patient Tracker•	6.9 WTE x Band 3 administration•	5.2 WTE x senior medical staffThere is a small number of vacancies currently in the process of recruitment with new appointments expected to start in the coming months.•	1.6 WTE x Band 7 nursing•	1 WTE x Band 6 nursing•	2 WTE x band 3 nursingIn addition to the posts listed above, the service is supported by other staff groups who are not specifically assigned to MIU. The following list of staff roles is not exhaustive but they provide essential support to the delivery of care in the MIU:•         Patient experience (Porters, Domestics & Catering)•         Allied Health Professions•         Pharmacy•         Laboratory•         RadiologyThe Trust continues to review staffing in line with best practice and professional evidence in order to address demand whilst prioritising patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health following the closure of the units, to detail the number of staff that moved from (i) Newtownards Minor Injury Unit; and (ii) Bangor Minor Injury Unit, to work in the Ulster Hospital Minor Injury Unit.
answer_text: All Minor Injury Unit staff in Bangor Hospital and Newtownards Hospital were based at the Minor Injury Unit in Newtownards prior to moving to the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald in September 2023.In total, 7 members of nursing staff (5.15 Whole Time Equivalents) and 5 members of the administrative staff (2.96 Whole Time Equivalents) moved in September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who attended the Ulster Hospital Minor Injury Unit, broken down for each month in 2023.
answer_text: Information on the number of new and unplanned review attendances at the Ulster Minor Injury Unit (MIU) is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics’ report which is available to view or download at: Emergency care waiting times | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)The number of new and unplanned review attendances at Ulster MIU in each month of 2023 has also been provided in Table 1 overleaf. Please note that the number of attendances does not equate to the number of patients treated, as a patient may attend an emergency care department(s) / Minor Injury Unit (MIU) more than once during each month or year.A new attendance refers to the first unplanned attendance by a patient for a new clinical condition at an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU). An unplanned review attendance relates to any patient who returns to an ED / Minor Injury Unit (MIU) without written instruction, for the same or related clinical condition within 30 days of the first (initial) attendance.Table 1: Number of Attendances (New and Unplanned Reviews only) at Ulster Minor Injury Unit in each month of 20231,2YearMonthAttendances2023September2,410October2,848November684December-Source: Health and Social Care Trusts1 Ulster Minor Injury Unit opened on 6th September 20232 Figures for November 2023 are based on clinical care episodes completed up to and including 8th November 2023. Please note that urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust is not yet available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records following the launch of the Encompass programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the size of the new Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital; and (ii) how this compares with the size of the previous Emergency Department.
answer_text: The Urgent and Emergency Care model at the Ulster Hospital currently consists of a new Emergency Department and new Minor Injuries Unit. (i)The floor space of the new Emergency Department within the Acute Services Block, Ulster Hospital is 2,555 m2. In addition, the Trust also provides a Minor Injury Unit, located within the former Emergency Department, with a floor space of approximately 1,237m2. This equates to a total Urgent and Emergency care service floor space of 3792m2 on the Ulster Hospital campus.(ii)The floor space in the previous Emergency Department building, which incorporated minor injuries, was 2,660 m2.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures can he put in place, in conjunction with the Minister for Infrastructure, to improve public transport links for residents from rural communities to access the Minor Injury Unit at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: Prior to the transfer of minor injury services last year, the Trust met with Translink to discuss the connectivity of the Ulster Hospital to the Ards/Bangor area following the service transfer. In addition to this, Trust officers have attended each of the Multi-Agency meetings held in Ards and Dundonald.   These meetings involve Translink, Department for Infrastructure, PSNI, the Trust and local elected representatives where the issue has been raised.Translink has been monitoring passenger usage and to date has not noticed any increase in demand for a bus service compared to the months prior to the change in minor injury services. Translink is not aware of any specific complaints made regarding the frequency of the service.  Translink has agreed to forward bus usage data to the Trust more frequently to jointly monitor the uptake of the bus service and they have agreed to meet the Trust if a noticeable increase in demand occurs.A formal review and evaluation of the new care model is not due until March 2024, however, the Trust has been monitoring patient attendances since the service commenced.  Feedback from patient questionnaires has shown thatFrom 6th September 2023 to 15th February 2024:6% of patients had arrived at the MIU using a taxi or public transport92.7% arrived using their own private transport1.3% arrived by road transport having obtained a liftThe Trust has implemented additional mitigations following the transfer which include:At least four scheduled meetings per year with Translink and this topic is now a standing item agenda to discuss if there is an increased demand for a bus service.Travel information is available on the publicly accessible Trust website which includes links to the Translink Travel Planner.There are public display timetables at the main entrances of the Trust’s larger acute sites providing real time departure information to bus users.Furthermore, as part of the consultation process, the Trust completed an Equality Impact Assessment and Rural Needs Impact Assessment which is still available on the Trust website.Given the above measures that the South Eastern Trust has taken since the service change, there is no further action I intend to take with the Minister for Infrastructure to improve public transport links to the Ulster Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the resurfacing schemes for (i) Beechwood Avenue; (ii) Beechwood Gardens; (iii) Hawthorne Drive; and (iv) Silverbirch Drive, Bangor, will commence.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road networkDetails of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the backdated pay awards for nursing staff will be paid.
answer_text: I said, on taking office, that my first priority was to get pay settlements over the line for staff.   Pay negotiations with AfC Trades Unions completed on 22 February.  I must commend the constructive negotiations that have taken place with trade unions representing Agenda for Change staff.Unions will now consult their members and it is important they be given the time and space for this to happen.  Should the offer be accepted, my Department will seek to implement the award as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to address the shortfall in car parking spaces at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on car parking provision and management in the Health and Social Care (HSC) sector for Northern Ireland states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.The Ulster Hospital has approximately 2200 parking spaces of which approximately 900 are public spaces and 1300 staff spaces.On a typical day public car parking capacity at the Ulster Hospital meets and exceeds the demands of vehicle users although some delays may be incurred at peak times. There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with an on-site car parking space, however the South Eastern HSCT provides over 100 free off-site staff parking spaces at Dundonald Omniplex with a free staff shuttle buses to and from work, cycle to work and car share schemes as well as evening and weekend passes for parking out of hours.The trust is working collaboratively with government departments to provide viable alternatives to single occupancy car use, to increase off-site staff car parking capacity and to potentially stagger the delivery of outpatient services over a longer period of the day / week to reduce peak numbers arriving on-site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the savings made by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in relation to the closure of (i) Newtownards Minor Injury Unit; and (ii) Bangor Minor Injury Unit.
answer_text: The Minor Injury Services at Bangor Hospital and Newtownards Hospital transferred to the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald in September 2023.  The South Eastern Trust has confirmed that there were no savings made as a result of this service change. As detailed in the public consultation, the decision was not taken to make financial savings but rather to reshape and sustain urgent and emergency care services in the North Down and Ards area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what plans his Department has for the DOH Core Grant Scheme in 2024-2025, following the suspension of the 2023-2024 competition which had no funding awarded; and (ii) what processes he can put in place to ensure openness and transparency in any future scheme.
answer_text: Once the budget position for 2024/25 is known, I will be in a position to consider the availability of funding for the Core Grant Scheme for next year.Any future open call for applications to a Core Grant Scheme will be informed by engagement and co-design with the Community and Voluntary Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will address women's mental health as part of his Women’s Health Action Plan and Women’s Health Strategy.
answer_text: I outlined during a debate on the development of a Women’s Health Strategy on 13 February, that my Department is in the process of developing a short-to-medium term women’s health action plan that builds on ongoing work and identifies the priority actions across women’s health services that can be taken forward within our current budget, as well as those actions for which additional investment will be required.During the debate I provided an overview of some of the ongoing work in relation to women’s mental health, including the establishment of perinatal mental health teams in each HSC Trust and the Department’s commitment, subject to budget affordability, to establish a Mother and Baby Unit.A copy of the Hansard Report on the debate can be found at https://aims.niassembly.gov.uk/officialreport/reportssearchresultsreport.aspx?&eveDate=2024/02/13&rID=387395&hwcID=4205663&aID=0&pg=3&sesID=26&dID=0&init=W#4206172The Public Health Agency (PHA) commissions a wide range of programmes focusing on emotional health and wellbeing and almost all of these are open to women.  The Lifeline service is also available 24/7 for anyone in distress or despair on 0808 808 8000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on new accommodation for Bright Sparks Preschool, Crawfordsburn.
answer_text: As a non-statutory provider Bright Sparks Pre-school is not entitled to capital funding from the Department.  On this basis, the Department has no plans to provide new accommodation for Bright Sparks Pre-school, Crawfordsburn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people who are homeless in North Down.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin January – June 2023 was published on 21 September 2023 and contains information on the numbers presenting as experiencing homelessness and is available here:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-january-june-2023According to this Bulletin and its Accompanying Table 1.3, there was a total of 689 Homeless Presenters (or 4.2 Presenters per 1,000 population) in Ards and North Down between January and June 2023.This Bulletin (Table 2.3) also states that there was a total of 531 Households accepted as homeless in Ards and North Down between January and June 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has for the Mental Health Support Fund for 2024-2025.
answer_text: The Mental Health Support Fund, administered and managed by the Community Foundation NI (CFNI), was established using unspent Covid-19 monies. The fund was a one-off, non-recurring fund for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. The monies allocated to the fund are now exhausted. Due to the financial challenges which the Department continues to face, there is no budget to extend this fund and it will end, as planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what proposals he will take forward to support organisations within the Community and Voluntary Sector, who assist unemployed and economically inactive people with access to pre-employment and training support, given that the UK Shared Prosperity Fund has 14 months left to deliver.
answer_text: My Department continues to work collaboratively with the Department for Communities in the development and provision of skills-based interventions which support the unemployed to engage/re-engage with the labour market. This includes support and participation in a range of emerging initiatives such as ‘Labour Market Partnerships’, the Disability and Work Strategy and refreshed approaches to addressing economic inactivity.The Skills Strategy also highlights the need for a full review of investment in the skills system. Additional steps will be taken to address economic inactivity in the process of rolling out my Economic Vision and through the work of Ministerial colleagues.I continue to support the Finance Minister who leads on future funding and call on the British Government to fully replace EU funding and to re-establish Executive control over that funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is on track to move to a 24/7 regional thrombectomy service for stroke by the end of 2024, as detailed in the Stroke Action Plan.
answer_text: The expansion of the regional thrombectomy service to 24/7 remains a key priority in Northern Ireland.  This will require significant investment in the current financial climate and the recruitment of specialist posts with a limited workforce pool. Therefore, it is unlikely the service can be expanded to 24/7 by the end of 2024. However, I am committed to ensuring as many people as possible from across Northern Ireland continue to benefit from the service within the current hours of operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment on whether the UK Shared Prosperity Fund has met its commitment to fully replicate the level of funding previously provided by the EU in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The British Government committed to fully replacing EU Structural Funds through the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) and made clear that decisions made and allocations to projects from that fund would be made solely by DLUHC as the responsible Whitehall Department.The £42m per annum SPF allocation (£127m three-year total) compares poorly with the £65m average per annum (£195m across three years) Executive Departments would have expected from ESF and ERDF, before taking inflation into consideration. The funding provided by the British Government to date has not represented full replacement. The Finance Minister leads in negotiations of replacement funding with the British Government and I will continue to support her efforts in that regard. DoF leads the cross-departmental Future Policy & Finance Workstream that provides input and a channel for engagement with DLUHC on funding streams associated with the Levelling Up agenda, including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). With our support, DoF also continues to press for NI Executive control over distribution of this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what proposals he will bring forward to support organisations which provide help to economically inactive women, who were not supported in the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: I will continue to call on the British Government to fully replace EU funding and to re-establish Executive control over that funding.  Additional steps will be taken to address economic inactivity in the process of rolling out my Economic Vision and through working collaboratively with other Ministers, including on Childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the resurfacing scheme of Clandeboye Heights, Bangor.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Ards and North Down council area was presented to Council members and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf  The resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the potential closure of AMH Promote by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I am aware that Action Mental Health (AMH) Promote provides a valuable service to enable people with learning disabilities to access diverse day activities and develop greater levels of independence. Action Mental Health informed the South Eastern HSC Trust (SEHSCT) in November 2023 that it would not be renewing its contract to deliver the Promote Day Opportunity Service in Bangor after 31st March 2024. Aware of the impact this decision by Action Mental Health would have on the service users and their families, SEHSCT immediately began to find an alternative provider. The Orchardville Society have today, 16 February 2024, confirmed that they will take over the Promote Contract from AMH on 1 April 2024. SEHSCT will now work at pace alongside Orchardville and Action Mental Health to agree transitional arrangements including engagements this week with AMH staff and Promote families.This is welcome news for all concerned and the Trust and providers will now work together to ensure a seamless transition for their Promote service users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the how recommendations contained in the Bengoa Report have impacted decisions made by Health and Social Care Trusts to close Minor Injury Units.
answer_text: The recommendations in the Bengoa Report have not impacted on the Trusts’ decisions to close Minor injury Units.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new build for St Columbanus College, Bangor.
answer_text: St Columbanus’ College, Bangor, is not currently on my Department’s Major Capital Works Programme for a new build scheme. A School Enhancement Project for the school is, however, progressing in design. The project will provide a two-storey permanent teaching block for science, PE and drama, as well as a 3G multi use games area. Approval to proceed to construction will be dependent on the availability of capital funding.  My Department is also progressing a Home Economics refurbishment scheme at the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the schools in North Down to be included in the 20mph speed limit zoning.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with the Department’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070The program to date has been rolled out in two tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that the Education Authority does not have a province-wide accident reporting system, for his assessment of whether the number of assaults on education sector staff is being accurately captured.
answer_text: The EA does have a 'Province wide' accident reporting system. However, they cannot report for other education employers (e.g. CCMS, Voluntary Grammar Schools and Grant Maintained Integrated schools) as the law requires each employer to record and report accidents on behalf of the injured employee.  Some of these accidents (including assaults) are required by law to be further reported to the HSENI. This occurs if the accident exceeds the threshold for reporting to the HSENI in compliance with the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrence Regulations (RIDDOR).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education on his plans to prioritise a joint childcare strategy with the other Executive departments.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have made the development and implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a priority and while I have stated that our ambition should not be limited, it is important that we take time to carefully consider our options.  Actions will involve input from a range of Government Departments. Early Learning and Childcare Strategy requires Executive agreement, including the scale and scope of any expanded provision and the allocation of budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many assaults on staff have been recorded in all education sectors between quarter one 2020-21 and quarter two 2023-24.
answer_text: Assaults on school staff are reported to the relevant employing authority rather than the Department of Education.  The Education Authority (EA) and Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) have advised there have been 4,452 accidents recorded as assault on employees between April 2020 and September 2023 in controlled and maintained schools.  90% of these assaults took place in Special Schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what policies or procedures have been put in place by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Education Authority to mitigate the number assaults on education staff.
answer_text: Staff who work in schools are employed by employing authorities or directly by the school, not by the Department of Education.  It is the responsibility of the employing authority to put in place these policies and procedures.Following a period of consultation, the Education Authority is currently in the final stages of reviewing its recently developed draft Preventing Violence at Work policy. This includes “Guidance For Staff In Preventing And Responding To Children And Young People’s Behaviours That Challenge Within The School/Educational Settings”. This policy has been developed in collaboration with all Employing Authorities and Unions representing teaching and support staff. In addition to the development of the Policy, a toolkit of resources and training is also in preparation, to support the implementation in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the the Queens Parade redevelopment project in Bangor, including (i) planning applications; (ii) timescales; and (iii) funding issues.
answer_text: My officials, in the Department’s Regional Development Office, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work collaboratively with the developer, Bangor Marine, to progress the regeneration of the Queen’s Parade site, and the broader regeneration of Bangor City.Bangor Marine secured Planning Permission for their proposals for the Queen’s Parade site in September 2022. Since then, Bangor Marine has been working to develop the final scheme design, and work through the pre-commencement conditions that emerged from the planning approval. Bangor Marine and Ards and North Down Borough Council are also now working together to secure the approvals required for the developer’s proposals that fall on the section of the site belonging to the Crown Estate. Officials in my Department’s Regional Development Office are working closely with Bangor Marine and Council teams to ensure this work progresses as quickly as possible. According to Bangor Marine’s latest plan, they will be on-site in the latter part of this year. Funding is not currently an issue as my Department has, over the past 11 years, acquired all the land and property within the Development scheme boundary.The Ards and North Down Borough Council is also currently developing its proposals for the regeneration of Bangor Waterfront, being funded by the NI Executive under the Belfast Region City Deal programme. Following the approval of the Outline Business Case and the issue of a Contract for Funding for the Council’s proposals, work is progressing well to appoint design teams and further develop the proposals to inform a Final Business Case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Spencer Street, Holywood, becoming a residents-only parking area.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes has been completed and the report will be published when I have considered the review findings .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he plans to increase the size of forests across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the significant contribution that our forests make to our environment, the rural economy and the health of wellbeing of our people. One of my key priorities is to focus on increasing forests and woodlands, from the current 8.6% of land area to 12% by 2050. I intend to take this forward as a critical element of the Department’s contribution to climate change mitigation and a balanced pathway to Net Zero carbon emissions.My immediate focus is to encourage a greater level of afforestation by partners and stakeholders. Expanding the forest and woodland area provides great opportunities to protect and enhance biodiversity, landscape and water quality in our environment for citizens and future generations to enjoy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans to protect the flora, fauna, and funga around our coastlines.
answer_text: A significant proportion of the Northern Ireland coastline is already protected for its biodiversity or earth science interest through statutory designation, e.g. as Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSI).  This places a duty on public bodies to take reasonable steps to further the conservation and enhancement of their flora and fauna and other important features.  NIEA officials are currently carrying out a review of existing ASSIs.		My Department has developed an Environmental Improvement Plan, that I anticipate will be published later this year, and are preparing a Nature Recovery Strategy.  These will include proposed actions and targets to enhance protection for nature on land and at sea.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions are being taken to address the backlog of MOT appointments.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition will launch on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/change-cancel-or-view-your-mot-test-appointment-online.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much extra funding his Department will receive for the repair of potholes for the rest of this financial year.
answer_text: My Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities including patching, has been underfunded for the last decade as a result of Tory cuts and austerity. My Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.As a result, there has been a continued deterioration in our infrastructure with large numbers of potholes and other defects developing across the road network, especially after periods of particularly cold or wet weather.Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many roads require (i) resurfacing; and (ii) partial resurfacing in North Down.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads within every council area, including Ards and North Down, that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to review the policy which allocates more funding to rural areas for roads resurfacing.
answer_text: My Department is committed to delivering the best possible services with the funds available. However, I recognise that there has been historical under-investment in our road network for a significant number of years and that many rural roads are in need of repair. This is why I have recently announced an additional investment of £1m to begin to address this this issue. The level of resurfacing of rural roads that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.I have asked my officials if funding can be identified in the 2024/25 financial year for a dedicated rural roads maintenance and resurfacing scheme once these deliberations have concluded I will make an announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much extra funding his Department will receive for road resurfacing schemes for the rest of this financial year.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their Department's priorities for the rest of this financial year.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many pot holes were reported for repair in North Down in 2023.
answer_text: Following the reform of local government in 2014, the Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the new district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many pot holes have been reported in North Down in 2024; and how many have yet to be repaired.
answer_text: Following the reform of local government in 2014, the Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the new district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Roads Policy and Procedure Guide – RSPPG E019, and current Director of Engineering Memorandum – DEM 160/21, whether the policy on repair of pot holes will revert to the original category depths to enable all pot holes to be repaired.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. I hope that critical services and infrastructure such as our roads are provided with the investment we need and I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much new funding his Department will require to clear the backlog of pothole repairs.
answer_text: All maintenance and repair works are carried out by outside contractors under a Department of Finance framework agreement, to which the Assembly Commission is a named party.No maintenance or repair works are carried out by secretariat staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures he will take to take to assist the Children’s Hospice from having to reduce its bed capacity.
answer_text: I recognise and applaud the vital work of the NICH and all hospices in Northern Ireland.As requested by me, DoH officials have engaged in detailed discussions last week with NI Hospice.It is clear to me that the financial pressures facing NICH go much deeper than any reduction of Departmental support.  This reduction involved the ending last summer of an additional but temporary annual payment of £170k. This decision was notified to the NICH in June 2023 and took effect from September 2023 which resulted in £85K reduction in this financial year.  I took the decision on Friday 09/02/2022 to reinstate that funding in full for this year.This payment is in addition to the core annual funding of £1.6m which my Department is providing directly to NICH this year. This core funding included a £420,000 component which had not been recurrent, meaning it would have to be subject to confirmation each year. I can confirm that, going forward, this will be made recurrent, giving the Hospice greater certainty.I am keenly aware that the financial challenges facing NICH go much deeper than this or next year. Like all health care providers, it has encountered significantly rising running costs, reflecting inflationary pressures.Regrettably, the NICH has advised my Department that the restoration of the full £170k for this year will not lead to the reversal of the service reduction made public this week.The NICH and NI Hospice parent body will need to develop longer-term solutions, to which I am sure Government will want to be supportive.Given the current absence of a budget for next year, and the well-documented financial uncertainties and challenges for Government, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.I am also very conscious that other hospice organisations, and many other health and care providers, are also grappling with growing budget pressures and will be seeking further statutory finance.I am grateful for the support and personal interest of the First Minister and deputy First Minister regarding NICH’s position. I am also aware of the fact that other NI Departments have provided financial support to NICH in the past.I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given his role in implementing the UK's net zero targets, for his assessment of the use of solar panels, ground source heat pumps, triple glazed windows and airtightness in newly constructed homes and businesses.
answer_text: The UK wide Net Zero Strategy which was published in 2021 recognises the important role that solar, heat pumps and other building energy efficiencies will play in supporting the decarbonisation of the UK economy. The Strategy, for example, highlights that low carbon heating options such as heat pumps are significantly more energy efficient than gas boilers at present to run. In Northern Ireland, my department leads on the climate change agenda and is responsible for preparing and publishing the first Climate Action Plan for 2023-27. However, all departments have a role to play in addressing climate change and they are legally responsible under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to provide policies and proposals for the Climate Action Plan to reduce emissions across a range of sectors including Residential and Public Buildings.The Department of Finance is the lead department for the setting of building standards. As such, DoF have provided input to this response, and they have provided the following information:The Department of Finance (DoF) has responsibility for the policy and legislation on Part F (Conservation of fuel and power) of the Building Regulations, which sets minimum standards for energy efficiency and a ’target emissions rate’ (TER) requirement for almost all new-build projects. Any standards set by Building Regulations are required to be reasonably attainable, however, those developing building specifications can, where they wish, set more rigorous standards provided this does not result in contravention of any other aspect of the regulations.The current TERs are set relative to a Notional Dwelling or a Notional Building of a standard specification. The approach includes certain restrictions to prevent undue relaxation of any particular aspect but still provides a high degree of flexibility for building designers to combine their preferred fabric and services, so long as the overall ‘as-built’ emissions rate is no worse than the TER.A recent impact assessment prepared by DoF suggests that most new buildings are likely to be built with photovoltaic solar panels and improved fabric, even if built solely to the standard in the most recent Part F uplift, implemented in June 2022. For further detail see the ‘Final Stage Impact Assessment’ within Consultation Proposals for amendment of Technical Booklet Guidance to Part F (Conservation of fuel and power) | Department of Finance (finance-ni.gov.uk)Using all the technologies listed may enable further emissions reductions but would likely have to be balanced against any higher capital or running costs and higher embodied carbon impacts.  Other related potential issues include risks with grid infrastructure connectivity, skills capacity and inadvertent negative impacts on indoor air quality, overheating and moisture risks.DoF Officials are currently collating the responses to consultation which closed on 15 December 2023 on a Discussion Document outlining considerations in this a
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will give a commitment to his legal obligation to lead Northern Ireland to net zero.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering on our obligation to meet the net zero target by 2050 as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. Achieving this will require significant effort and transformation across all aspects of our economy and society and I look forward to working with Northern Ireland Executive colleagues on this critical issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people visited Ballycopeland Windmill in 2023.
answer_text: There were 2853 visitors in the 2023 calendar year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will provide assurances that he will not introduce a badger cull.
answer_text: A judicial review decision, delivered on 25 October 2023, quashed the Departments former policy to advance a non-selective cull of badgers in targeted intervention areas as part of the Northern Ireland Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy. 		I will be discussing with my officials, in the time ahead, next steps for the bTB Eradication Strategy in addition to engaging with stakeholders to hear their views on this emotive subject. Any future wildlife intervention proposals will be subject to further public consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what protection or warning measures can be placed along the (i) A21 Newtownards Road; (ii) A2 Belfast Road; and (iii) Ballysallagh Road, Bangor, to make drivers aware of deer crossing.
answer_text: My Department erects ‘Wild Animals’ warning signs, which include a deer symbol, but are not specifically ‘Deer’ warning signs.  Numerous requests for the erection of such signs are received, following reports of near miss incidents, however signs are generally only erected following confirmation from the PSNI that deer (or other wild animals) have been a factor in Road Traffic Collisions (RTCs), particularly on high volume, high speed routes.  They will also be considered where there is evidence that animals regularly cross or move along a road, and where visibility is poor.  My Department has provided such signage in a number of locations in North Down including A21 Newtownards Road (near Conlig), Belfast Road (Kiltonga) and Crawfordsburn Road.  There are currently no plans to erect further signage at these locations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what plans she has for ending violence against women and girls.
answer_text: Violence against women and girls is unacceptable and must not be tolerated in Northern Ireland.  Lead responsibility for the development of a Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy falls to the Executive Office (TEO) and I understand that this work is well advanced.  My Department has a strong interest in this work, given the interdependencies with tackling domestic and sexual abuse. My officials will continue to work with TEO to support the development and delivery of this strategy and to ensure that there is appropriate alignment with the cross-Departmental strategic response to domestic and sexual abuse, which is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health, with inputs from the Department of Education, the Department for Communities and TEO.  I hope to publish a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) strategy jointly with my ministerial colleague, the Minister for Health, later this year.  This DSA Strategy will take forward a range of work to protect and support victims and to address abusive behaviours which will benefit all victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including women and girls who are disproportionately affected by these types of abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when public sector pay awards will be resolved.
answer_text: Working together with Ministerial colleagues, I want to ensure all our public sector workers receive a pay award as soon as possible reflecting the vital role they play in delivering public services.The amount included for pay in the December financial package only provides funding for one year and falls short of what is required. The Executive will take a united case for a fair and just settlement for public sector workers and public services to Westminster.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assistance his Department can provide Ballycopeland Windmill cafe.
answer_text: I have directed my officials to bring forward recommendations on how best to maintain access to this important heritage asset and the café. I understand the significance of the Windmill and café to the local community and its importance in attracting visitors to the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will make it a priority to address the shortfall in police officer numbers.
answer_text: Whilst police officer numbers remain an operational matter for the Chief Constable to address, I will continue to do all I can to support him to ensure safe and effective policing.  In doing that, I remain committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what protections (i) are already in place; and (ii) can be put in place for red squirrels in Clandeboye Estate, Bangor.
answer_text: Red Squirrels are protected in Northern Ireland under the terms of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. This protection is wide ranging and includes (but is not limited to) the intentional, (or reckless) killing, injuring or taking of red squirrels as well the intentional (or reckless) destruction of any structure or place that they use for shelter or protection. It is an offence to disturb red squirrels whilst they occupy these structures or places. In 2021, the local volunteer squirrel group, centred on the Clandeboye Estate, reported the loss of its relatively small red squirrel population because of a suspected squirrel pox outbreak, a condition fatal to them. Sightings within the last year suggest that red squirrels have returned there. Grey Squirrels are vectors of the squirrel pox virus and their reported population reduction within the estate is the likely result of an increase in the local Pine Marten population which preys on them, benefitting the red squirrels.  Additional removal of grey squirrels by trapping takes place at several sites across North Down, with a view to protecting remaining red squirrel populations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans does he have to address the backlog of road resurfacing in North Down.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads within every council area, including Ards and North Down, that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the introduction of legislation in England and Wales in relation to XL Bully dogs, what plans does he have to bring similar legislation to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: A proportionate and effective way forward to deal with potentially dangerous dogs is a key issue for me.I fully recognise the need to move quickly in this area and am therefore keen to engage stakeholders to hear their views, so that any decision I take will be based on the best evidence available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what protections (i) are already in place; and (ii) can be put in place to protect fallow deer in Clandeboye Estate, Bangor.
answer_text: Fallow deer are protected across Northern Ireland under the terms of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. This makes it an offence to kill, injure or take this species during a specified close season or at night. It also prohibits the use of certain firearms and ammunition to shoot deer. Additionally, it is an offence to take or remove any live deer or to mark or attach tags to any live deer. The PSNI enforce the terms of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. My officials in NIEA work closely with the PSNI and jointly chair the Partnership for Action Against Wildlife Crime NI (PAW NI) which brings together statutory, non-statutory agencies & interested parties with the common goal of combating wildlife crime through education and publicity. Whilst Clandeboye is a private estate, my officials are not aware of any reports of listed offences occurring there that would require additional protections for its fallow deer herd. Any concerns should be reported immediately to the PSNI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Members will be given an opportunity to speak to staff involved in the trial of the new stationery system before it is fully implemented.
