question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Members will be able to vote on whether they want the new stationery system before it is implemented.
answer_text: A new integrated finance, HR, payroll and procurement system, approved by the Assembly Commission, is under development and will be introduced at a later stage.  This will enable Members to submit claims for the recovery of expenses incurred in running a constituency office electronically.  As a precursor to the integrated finance/HR system the new stationery contract allows Members to process orders online for direct delivery to constituency or party offices or to an office within Parliament Buildings.  The nature of support provided to Members is a matter for the Assembly Commission.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will meet with me and NIPSA to discuss the potential implications for MLAs and their staff of the new financial system for submitting office expenditure claims electronically and ordering stationery.
answer_text: A new contract for the provision of stationery supplies will commence shortly. The new contract has afforded the opportunity to modernise and streamline the process for ordering and paying for stationery, as well as making the service to MLAs more flexible. MLAs will be provided with more information on the new process in the coming days.A new system for managing finance, HR, payroll and procurement processes across the Assembly is being developed and will be introduced at a later stage. This will enable MLAs to submit claims for the recovery of expenditure incurred in running a constituency office electronically. MLAs will be provided with more information on the new system in due course.Assembly Commission officials would be happy to meet with you regarding your written question, however NIPSA has no remit in relation to MLAs or their staff.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Members will be consulted on the new financial system that is being developed to enable Members to submit claims for office expenditure electronically and order stationery, before it is implemented.
answer_text: A new contract for the provision of stationery supplies will commence shortly. The new contract has afforded the opportunity to modernise and streamline the process for ordering and paying for stationery, as well as making the service to Members more flexible. Members will be provided with more information on the new process in the coming days.A new system for managing finance, HR, payroll and procurement processes across the Assembly is being developed and will be introduced at a later stage. This will enable Members to submit claims for the recovery of expenditure incurred in running a constituency office electronically. All parties were contacted in October 2020 to inform them that the Assembly Commission would be developing a new financial system, and to invite them to attend an end user workshop to seek their views and provide input to the project and end user requirements. A further presentation was offered and provided to Members in March 2022. A copy of the presentation is on the Members’ Portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update why the Bangor Minor Injury Unit remains closed.
answer_text: The temporary consolidation of the Bangor and Ards Minor Injuries Units on the Ards Hospital site was undertaken as part of the South Eastern Trust’s Covid-19 response in order to support and protect the provision of Trust services during the pandemic. As such, this remains under continuous review as part of the Trust’s plans for rebuilding services.The staffing of MIUs will also be reviewed as part of the Department of Health’s ‘Review of Urgent and Emergency Care Services in Northern Ireland’.In the future, should there be any proposed permanent changes to minor injuries services the Trust will consult with all relevant stakeholders including the public and service users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why the Education Authority decided that an Oracle based platform would be used to make payments to the NI Supply Teacher Register.
answer_text: This question was raised in October 2022Education Authority does not use an Oracle based platform to make payments to the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR). The Education Authority does not make payments to the NISTR.  NISTR is a booking system for schools to source vetted supply staff and is not an Oracle based platform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether the incumbent company was advised that it should not tender to run payroll for the NI Supply Teacher Register; and (ii) to outline the rationale for this position.
answer_text: This question was raised in October 2022.The incumbent company (at that time) was not advised that it should not tender to run payroll for the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register.EA tendered for a Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) via the Digital Outcomes and Specialist public sector framework.  NISTR is a booking system for schools and educational settings to source vetted supply teachers, not a payroll system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether the Education Authority refused help from the incumbent company to run the NI Supply Teacher Register payroll and call centres; and (ii) to outline the rationale for this position.
answer_text: This question was raised in October 2022.The incumbent company kindly offered to extend the expired contract to supply the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR), which the Education Authority declined as unfeasible.(ii) Migration of all teacher and school data from the incumbent solution was successfully completed in early August 2022.  Registration and vetting of all new supply teachers and updating of existing records was completed in the new solution:  it was therefore unfeasible at that stage to revert to stand up the incumbent solution as it was out of date and incomplete.

answer_text: Figures from the 2021/22 annual school census exercise show that there were 98,804 pupils entitled to free school meals. The school census is undertaken in October each academic year. The 2022/23 census data collection is currently under way, finalised figures will be available in Spring 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will address the lack of electric car charging points across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network. ESB can be contacted directly regarding its plans for its charge point infrastructure. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV on 30 March 2022. Derry City and Strabane District council is best placed to advise about the application. My Department is represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force, which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the process of recruiting Civil Servants and the average time it takes to complete the process.
answer_text: The current recruitment process broadly contains the following stages; departmental funding approvals; agreement on the method to fill the vacancy; recruitment competition design; advertisement; selection process (es); offer of employment; pre-employment checks; acceptance; and notice period. The time taken to complete these steps varies depending on the vacancy and the recruitment competition.   For example, if there is available supply from merit lists, there is no requirement to run a new recruitment competition, compared to managing a competition that requires security vetting as part of the pre-employment checks.From March 2021 the majority of vacancies filled were General Service grades (4873). The three most recent General Service competitions to reach offer stage were Executive Officer 2, Executive Officer 1 and Staff Officer.  These competition timelines averaged 15.6 weeks from advertising to merit list. An end to end review of recruitments processes has been completed by DoF Business Consultancy Services which aims to enhance the current recruitment processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many times a year the reservoir site at Portavoe is maintained by the new owner.
answer_text: Portavoe Reservoir is privately managed and my Department encourages all reservoir managers to ensure the safety of their reservoirs in accordance with their common law responsibility. In the absence of the full commencement of the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 my officials are unable to compel reservoir owners or managers to undertake or report on maintenance works at their reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why fishermen are still not allowed access to fish at the Portavoe Reservoir, Groomsport, despite a Service Level Agreement.
answer_text: DAERA continues to manage the fishing rights at Portavoe Reservoir until 18 December 2024 under a Service Level Agreement with the previous owner, Northern Ireland Water. This is subject to the Department having an access agreement from the new owner, Portavoe Estates Ltd. Discussions have taken place between officials and the new owner, who have confirmed that they wish the site to remain private and not open to the public.My Officials are continuing to engage with the new owner to either seek access for DAERA permit holders while the Department retains the fishing rights or to agree a financial arrangement for the transfer of the fishing rights.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many times a year are the trees at Portavoe Resevoir, Groomsport, inspected by the Forest Service.
answer_text: Forest Service carry out a risk-based programme of tree safety inspections across its Forest estate. The trees at Portavoe are inspected annually with the most recent inspection having taking place on 26 October 2021. The next inspection is planned to take place before the end of the 2022 calendar year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much savings his Department will make following his announcement to eliminate the use of off-contract agencies.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring a world class health service in Northern Ireland, in which services are delivered primarily by our Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce. In recognition of the vacancy pressures across the health service, which compound the reliance on agency, I have already taken action to ensure more people are recruited and more people are trained for roles in our HSC. Actions I have taken include:Investment in the highest level of locally commissioned pre-registration nurse training places, 1,335 new Nursing undergraduates this academic year;Supporting the recruitment of international nurses across our HSC with an allocation of £8m in 22/23 allowing the recruitment of 600 new nurses during this financial year;Expanding the number of places on undergraduate Allied Health Professional places commissioned by the Department by 41 places this year; andIncreasing the number of places for a primary medical degree to 306 with the opening of the new Graduate Entry Medical School at Magee.Each HSC Trust has been given a savings target and work is already underway within each organisation to deliver these savings within the current financial year.The drive to reduce agency spend will run in parallel with ongoing initiatives to build the health service workforce. Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he intends to fill the gap in the shortage of staff which was made up through the use of Agency staff.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring a world class health service in Northern Ireland, in which services are delivered primarily by our Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce. In recognition of the vacancy pressures across the health service, which compound the reliance on agency, I have already taken action to ensure more people are recruited and more people are trained for roles in our HSC. Actions I have taken include:Investment in the highest level of locally commissioned pre-registration nurse training places, 1,335 new Nursing undergraduates this academic year;Supporting the recruitment of international nurses across our HSC with an allocation of £8m in 22/23 allowing the recruitment of 600 new nurses during this financial year;Expanding the number of places on undergraduate Allied Health Professional places commissioned by the Department by 41 places this year; andIncreasing the number of places for a primary medical degree to 306 with the opening of the new Graduate Entry Medical School at Magee.Each HSC Trust has been given a savings target and work is already underway within each organisation to deliver these savings within the current financial year.The drive to reduce agency spend will run in parallel with ongoing initiatives to build the health service workforce. Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why Clandeboye Road, Bangor, which was on the previous year’s plans for resurfacing but has not been resurfaced, was not added to the new resurfacing program for 2022-23.
answer_text: I can confirm that Beechwood Avenue, Beechwood Gardens, Hawthorne Drive and Silverbirch Drive, Bangor along with Kinnegar Drive, Holywood were included in my Department’s 2021/22 resurfacing programme but were not completed nor carried forward into the 2022/23 year’s programme. I can also confirm that Clandeboye Road, Bangor was not included in the 2021/22 resurfacing programme.The asphalt resurfacing contract covering the Ards and North Down Council area expired in November 2021 and due to its replacement contract not being available it was not possible to complete the proposed programme. The replacement contract is planned for award in early 2023. This obviously limits the amount of resurfacing possible during the current financial year and this is reflected in the limited number of schemes included within the Council report. The roads named will be considered for inclusion in future works programmes.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

I can also confirm that Clandeboye Road, Bangor was not included in the 2021/22 resurfacing programme.The asphalt resurfacing contract covering the Ards and North Down Council area expired in November 2021 and due to its replacement contract not being available it was not possible to complete the proposed programme. The replacement contract is planned for award in early 2023. This obviously limits the amount of resurfacing possible during the current financial year and this is reflected in the limited number of schemes included within the Council report. The roads named will be considered for inclusion in future works programmes.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what areas of the Strangford Peninsula have been identified as areas of potential coastal erosion and flooding in the future.
answer_text: My Department has developed the 2nd Cycle Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP) 2021 -2027 which has highlighted the most significant areas of risk in the North from rivers, the sea and surface water.  The Strangford Peninsula has not been identified, through that work, as an area of potentially significant flood risk and there are presently no plans to carry out any additional works in this area. However, my officials have worked with partner organisations to develop a coastal flooding emergency response plan to detail the arrangements for a co-ordinated multi-agency emergency response to anywhere coastal flooding may occur here.I understand DAERA are undertaking an extensive programme of coastal surveys to expand the coastal data baseline, and over future years with repeated surveys will provide detailed information on how our coast is changing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many Civil Service job vacancies exist in each Department.
answer_text: Details of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service Department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a Department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all Departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources.At the end of September 2022 NICSHR was managing a caseload of 3,086 vacancies – breakdown by department is as follows:DepartmentNo. of VacanciesDAERA732DE75DfC728DfE97DfI318DoF434DoH110DoJ456PPS32TEO104TOTAL3,086		Source: NICSHR

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many civil servants in his Department are working from home.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect information on staff numbers attending offices as this changes on a daily basis. Up until 6 June 2022, in line with Executive guidance that all staff who could work from home, should work from home, the vast majority of my Department’s staff had done so, supported by enhanced IT provision which enabled them to perform their duties effectively.  Those staff who had to be in the workplace to deliver front line services were working on site. With the lifting of the “Work from Home” guidance this is allowing more staff to return to the workplace, fully utilising New Ways of Working including, implementing the Hybrid Working Policy, which provides for a mixture of office and home working and use of the Connect 2 Hubs. This means staff have more options as to how they work, depending on business need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the number of people who are using food banks.
answer_text: My Department does not directly fund foodbanks and does not compile statistics on the number of people accessing them.  However, throughout the cost of living crisis I have been engaging with grassroots organisations and people in local communities and there is clearly a significant increase in the number of people who have to access foodbanks.The Trussell Trust operates the largest network of foodbanks here and publishes 6 monthly statistics which support my assessment.  The statistics for the last five years, by Council area, can be viewed at: https://www.trusselltrust.org/news-and-blog/latest-stats/end-year-stats/ I remain absolutely committed to finding ways to provide as much support as possible in the coming months but the challenges we face are being made greater due to the continued absences of a functioning Executive and an agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline plans for the future of the old Green Road railway bridge.
answer_text: My Department received a request in May 2019 to formally abandon a disused bridge and road at Green Road, Conlig, Bangor which is no longer maintained. I am advised by officials that should the road be abandoned, the bed and soil will revert to the title owners of the land. The Department does not have title ownership to the old road or the bridge and so have no input or plans for the future of the bridge following any abandonment.I can confirm that officials are currently preparing a submission relating to the proposed abandonment which I will consider in due course. Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are any plans to have an area designated as an Enterprise Zone in North Down.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for designating enterprise zones which is a matter for HM Treasury (HMT).  We have been given no information on plans to create an Enterprise Zone in North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of extending the proposed Donaghadee to Newtownards greenway to Bangor, as an alternative to the Holywood to Donaghadee cycle path.
answer_text: As the member is aware the development of greenways is the responsibility of local Councils rather than my Department.  However I am keen to support the development of greenways and have invited councils to submit applications for grant in support of greenways and active travel generally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many badgers were tested in the Test and Vaccinate or Remove project, in the Banbridge area; and (ii) whether it resulted in a reduction in Tuberculosis incidents.
answer_text: The Test and Vaccinate or Remove (TVR) Project was carried out over five years between 2014 and 2018.  It encompassed an area of approximately 100 Km² near Banbridge in Co Down. During the project 824 unique badgers were captured, some of which were captured and tested more than once.  Over the course of the TVR project there were 1520 badger captures, 146 of which gave a positive result to the DPP (sett-side) blood test for bovine TB.  This equates to 10.3% of the total tests.Whilst valuable field data, and deployment experience, was gained through the project results, it did not provide evidence to determine the effectiveness of TVR as a means of reducing TB in cattle.  To determine the effectiveness of TVR as means of controlling TB in cattle, similar activity in other study areas would have been required for comparison.  This would have added significantly to the overall cost of the project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment has been made of the impact on Bloomfield Primary School of the proposed new build for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: Bangor Central Integrated Primary School has been selected for a major capital investment project to improve or replace its existing accommodation.  The project will provide brand new facilities for the school at Balloo Road in Bangor.  There will be no change to the school’s approved enrolment numbers as a result of the project.Where the Department determines that the relocation of a school will not materially change its pupil catchment area, as in this instance, a Development Proposal is not considered necessary.There is no evidence of sustainability issues affecting Bloomfield Primary School and any impact on pupil numbers at this school is unlikely to be of a scale that would require any consequential planning action by the Education Authority, as managing authority for this school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new school build for Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that Planning Approval for the Crawfordsburn Primary School project was received on 15th June 2022.The Education Authority has submitted a Business Case to my Department for consideration and once this has been approved, the project will progress to RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design & Pre-tender).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed pedestrian crossing on the A2, at St Columbanus College, Bangor.
answer_text: Local officials have confirmed that a controlled pedestrian crossing, which will improve road safety for pedestrians crossing this heavily trafficked A Class road, is included within a programme of works for this financial year.  Timescales for delivery of this scheme will be dependent on the availability of contractor resources.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to list the schools in North Down in need of urgent repairs; and (ii) the estimated timeframes for the work to be completed in each school.
answer_text: The information relating to urgent repairs and timeframes for schools in North Down will take some time to collate. I have asked my Estate Operations Team to forward this information to you as soon as it is collated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new school build for St Columbanus College, Bangor.
answer_text: There are currently no plans for a new build for St Columbanus’ College, Bangor.St Columbanus’ College, Bangor, was announced on 21 January 2019 to advance in design under the Second Call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2).  The Protocol for SEP2 detailed that if a school was successful in securing SEP funding that it would remove the school from consideration of a wholly new build school for seven years from the date of approval of funding to their SEP project. St Columbanus’ College is therefore not currently eligible to be considered for a new build project.  A Design Team has been appointed for the SEP2 project and RIBA Stage 2 (Scheme Design) is underway. The SEP2 project, together with a series of Minor Works projects, will enhance the provision at the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the process to find replacement doctors and contractors for the Springhill and Priory surgeries.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) have initiated a process to find a new contractor to take over the Priory & Springhill GMS Contract from 1st February 2023.  There have been a number of expressions of interest put forward.  SPPG officers are currently engaging with potential new contract holders and I remain hopeful that one will be successful.  Whilst I am hesitant to provide a timeframe, I can confirm that SPPG officers are working hard to find a solution that will ensure that the full range of GMS services are provided to all registered patients at Priory & Springhill Surgery from 1st February 2023.  Patients and the wider public will be kept informed of any developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of introducing Harper's Law to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland a life sentence is already mandatory in any murder case, and, in circumstances considered appropriate by the Court, can be imposed where a defendant is found guilty of manslaughter.   My Department’s review of sentencing policy in Northern Ireland, which resulted in 54 recommendations, examined the need to introduce new legislation in relation to sentencing for attacks on frontline workers. I have agreed that the seriousness of assaults on a range of frontline workers should be recognised in the magistrates’ courts with a higher maximum penalty than is currently available and that, in more serious cases, the fact that the victim is a frontline worker should become a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the Courts will be obliged to specifically recognise emergency workers in their sentencing decisions.  The report on the way forward setting out my decisions was published on 21 April 2021 and can be seen online at Sentencing Policy Review - Way Forward Report.I have no plans to make further changes to the law in Northern Ireland at this time.  However, I have asked my officials, who are taking preparatory work forward with a view to introducing a sentencing bill during this mandate, to monitor the application of Harper’s Law in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the process for the adoption of roads.
answer_text: New roads are normally adopted by my Department under Article 8 of the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980. This process involves my Department first determining the street for adoption under Article 3, as part of the planning process. Prior to development commencing, the developer is required under Article 32 to enter into an agreement with my Department. Once the developer joins the street to a public road and constructs it to an adoptable standard in conformity with the determination, my Department may issue a preliminary certificate. Following a one year maintenance period, during which time the developer is responsible for making good any defects, my Department may then issue a further certificate and the street is then adopted.My Department can also adopt existing streets which have not previously been determined for adoption, under Article 9 of the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980, provided they meet the following criteria:My Department is of the opinion that the street should become a public road and a majority of the owners or occupiers of the land, in or adjoining the street, consent to this in writing; orA majority of such owners or occupiers request in writing that the street should become a public road and my Department is of the opinion that the request be granted; and (in either case)My Department is satisfied that the street is, in all respects, fit and proper to become a public road.Further information on the adoption process is available on my Department’s website via the following link: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/development-control.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when will the derelict houses on Kings Street be demolished as part of the regeneration of Queen's Parade, Bangor.
answer_text: The Developer for Queen’s Parade, Bangor Marine, last week secured full planning permission for the regeneration of the Queen’s Parade site. This is a significant milestone, taking us closer to seeing the much needed regeneration of this City centre site.  The Developer’s team is now working to refine the timeline for the necessary detailed design work, after which site preparation and construction work will begin. Further announcements will be made in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to media reports that the Ulster Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals believes there are 14,000 badger baiting incidents a year in Northern Ireland, what measures is his Department taking to stop this illegal hunting.
answer_text: Badgers are afforded protection in Northern Ireland under the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) has responsibility to investigate breaches of the Wildlife Order. The PSNI works closely with DAERA officials, who have relevant scientific knowledge, to gather information which assists prosecutions. The Partnership for Action Against Wildlife Crime Northern Ireland (PAWNI) is a forum which is co–chaired by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and the PSNI.  The work of this collective aims to educate the public on what constitutes a wildlife crime and how to report it.  The PAWNI 2016-2018 report recorded 155 reports over the 3 year period that may be potential wildlife-crime against badgers.My Department will continue to support the fight against all wildlife crime, including badger baiting, through its work with PAWNI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what actions his Department is taking to preserve current EU Environmental laws or create similar equivalent ones.
answer_text: Following EU Exit my Department delivered a significant programme of legislation to ensure the preservation of environmental protections and standards in domestic legislation. My Department continues to assess the implications of UK Government Bills currently before Parliament.It is my intention going forward that Northern Ireland continues to have a clear statutory basis for protecting the environment as has been the case throughout the changes resulting from the UK’s withdrawal from the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment as to whether republican paramilitary organisations were involved in the recent murder of a man at a West Belfast social club.
answer_text: This was a shocking incident and I extend my sympathies to all those affected. There is no place for this violence in our society. As you will appreciate, this is both an operational matter and a live investigation and as such you may wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice further to media reports that, according to the Ulster Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals, 150 people are involved in animal baiting incidents, how many people have been prosecuted for such acts, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence for animal baiting.  Such offences may be prosecuted under the Protection of Badgers Act 1992 or under the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985.  Information has been provided in relation to the number of prosecutions and convictions at courts, for cases involving offences under this legislation that relate to killing or injuring wild animals, or interfering with their shelter, that were dealt with in the period 2017 to 2021, the most recent five years for which information is available. Defendants prosecuted or convicted at courts for offences related to killing or injuring wild animals, 2017 – 2021YearProsecutionsConvictions20173*2018002019*02020*02021*0Total7*Note:1. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI or other bodies. 2. Figures relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 3. Figures relate to defendants who were prosecuted or convicted at court, or received an out of court diversion for at least one offence under the legislation specified..4. Offences included are relevant offences under Article 10 of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 and under the Protection of Badgers Act 1992. * means a figure less than three has been treated under rules of disclosure. As well as the convictions highlighted in the table, three individuals were dealt with by way of out of court diversionary disposal in the time period reported.  It is possible offences involving animal baiting were prosecuted under other animal welfare legislation during the period.  However, Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain detail on the circumstances in which an offence occurred.  Therefore, it is not possible to identify whether prosecutions or convictions at courts for offences under other animal welfare legislation related to animal baiting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what help he can give to businesses with their heating and electricity costs this winter.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure that businesses are facing across the board, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. Coupled with ongoing global supply chain disruptions, these unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy, including input cost pressures for businesses across all sectors. As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level – where interventions are measurable in the tens of billions. That is why in recent weeks and months I have taken every opportunity to engage with UK Government to highlight the inflationary pressures being felt by households and businesses in Northern Ireland. Following these engagements, I welcome the UK Government’s commitment to deliver the much needed £400 Energy Support Payment Scheme in Northern Ireland. In addition, the announcement by the Prime Minister of a domestic Energy Price Guarantee and a non-domestic Energy Price Bill Scheme – and her commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland via a parallel scheme. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their bills over the coming months.  My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.The outlook remains challenging but I am hopeful for the future. I am investing in those areas where we can gain the best long-term outcomes, which will help us build greater economic resilience for the future.  For example, I have published an energy strategy which will deliver a step change in how we move away from our reliance on overseas, imported fossil fuels. Alongside our investment in skills this will support the delivery of our ambitious 10X Economic Vision for the benefit of all our people.In practical terms, Invest Northern Ireland’s Energy and Resource Efficiency Support for businesses provides a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable local businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.Businesses seeking support should primarily contact Invest NI’s Business Support team on 0800 181 4422 or enquiry@investni.com to discuss what may be available. The Business Support Team can also provide information on a wide range of support options currently available from other organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what help she can give to churches with their heating and electricity costs this winter.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

answer_text: Approximately £140m was distributed to NI individuals via the Spend Local Card. Around £3.5m (around 2.5%) remained unspent at scheme end.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what help can be given to help the Trusts pay for their heat and electricity this winter.
answer_text: I understand that the Trusts will qualify for the energy support that is being offered to all non-domestic energy users under the scheme recently announced by the UK Government. As yet I do not have any details on exactly how this will apply to Northern Ireland, but I would expect it to go some way to mitigating the increased costs that they are currently experiencing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Bangor Minor Injury Unit is going to reopen.
answer_text: The temporary consolidation of the Bangor and Ards Minor Injuries Units on the Ards Hospital site was undertaken as part of the South Eastern Trust’s Covid-19 response in order to support and protect the provision of Trust services during the pandemic. As such, this remains under continuous review as part of the Trust’s plans for rebuilding services.A key consideration for the Trust is the availability of sufficient numbers of qualified emergency nurse practitioners to support the effective operation of two separate units. The Trust is actively recruiting and it is anticipated that subject to pre-employment checks, further appointments may be made soon.The staffing of MIUs will also be reviewed further as part of the Department of Health’s ‘Review of Urgent and Emergency Care Services in Northern Ireland’.In the future, should there be any proposed permanent changes to minor injuries services the Trust will consult with all relevant stakeholders including the public and service users.Please note there is a minor injuries stream in the Ulster Hospital Emergency Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what help she can give to charity groups with their heating and electricity costs this winter.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what help she can give to schools with their heating and electricity costs this winter.
answer_text: I can assure you that I fully recognise the challenges that rising heating and electricity costs present for our schools, and my officials seek regular updates from the Education Authority (EA) in relation to this. The most recent update estimated an additional pressure of £13.3m relating to rises in natural gas, heating oil and electricity costs in the 2022-23 financial year. This was after factoring in the anticipated impact of the UK government’s recent announcement of an Energy Bill Relief Scheme for the period October 2022 to March 2023. These, and other pressures have been communicated to the Department of Finance in anticipation of an agreed budget for 2022-23 in due course. In addition to this, since August 2020, the EA has proactively sought to mitigate the impact of increasing utility and energy costs by engaging an independent energy advisor to provide advice on purchasing energy, tariff changes and hedging purchase prices for electricity and gas on behalf of our schools. Notwithstanding all this, if any school is facing financial difficultly, I would encourage them to contact the EA, as funding authority for grant aided schools, in the normal way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why services have not returned to normal across all Trusts after the Covid Pandemic.
answer_text: A return to pre-pandemic levels of activity across the HSC is imperative in order for the HSC system to realise the expected benefits from any transformation activity. Achievement of this outcome is therefore the primary focus for the HSC for the remainder of 2022/23.  An integrated HSC Service Delivery Plan which brings a single focus to primary, community and acute services has been agreed.  The Plan, which is effective from 1 July 2022, sets out the performance trajectories required on an individual service area basis to achieve a return to, as a minimum, pre-COVID-19 levels of service provision across a range of primary, community and acute services by the end of September 2022 or as soon as possible thereafter, but no later than end of March 2023.
