question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why all the areas under the previous year’s resurfacing programme for North Down have not yet been resurfaced.
answer_text: As advised in the response to AQW 3277/22-27, I can confirm that the resurfacing contract for the Ards and North Down area expired in November 2021 resulting in delays to planned resurfacing works. Following legal challenges to the award of resurfacing contracts in spring 2021, the Department has been working at pace to develop and implement a new contract strategy. My Department is currently aiming to have a replacement contract available for the Ards and North Down area by spring 2023. Schemes that were delayed will be considered for inclusion in future programmes of work.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what is the amount of the Housing Executive's Landlord Reserve Fund.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the closing balance of its Stock Investment Reserve as at 31st March 2021 was £234m. The final accounts for 2021-22 are scheduled to be published at the end of October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has informed utility companies that a scheme for energy efficiency measures will be launched next spring.
answer_text: The Minister invited representatives from energy companies to a round table meeting on 14 June 2022 to discuss the rising energy prices for domestic consumers. Attendees included representatives from network operators, energy suppliers, the NI Utility Regulator and NI Consumer Council. The proposed multi-year energy efficiency flagship initiative that will deliver an accelerated and systematic scaling up of domestic retrofit across Northern Ireland was discussed at the meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will freeze all planned Housing Executive rent rises for this financial year.
answer_text: I announced in a statement to the Assembly a Housing Executive rent freeze in 2022-23. I have already implemented a rent freeze this financial year for Housing Executive tenants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list the housing estates across Northern Ireland that have benefited from cavity wall insulation schemes, over the last three years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that it has not started any new cavity wall insulation (CWI) schemes for its stock since 2017/18.  All potential CWI schemes were held pending the findings and recommendations of the BBA’s report on CWI in the Housing Executive’s stock and our subsequent development of an Action Plan for addressing these.  The Housing Executive’s CWI Action Plan was published earlier this year. It recognises that a CWI replacement/remediation programme for Housing Executive stock will be necessary, but that the considerable level of funding required for this is not available at this time or in the short term.  Until such time as this required funding is available the Housing Executive will implement a small annual CWI programme directed at addressing instances where the condition of CWI is having a detrimental impact on the structure of its properties.  For 2022/23 this programme currently comprises the following locations:•	Lisnabreen, Bangor•	Helens Bay and Crawfordsburn•	Limavady•	Whitehead

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Southwell Road, Bangor will be resurfaced.
answer_text: As previously advised, resurfacing programmes in Ards and North Down are now, as is the case in a number of other Council areas, being prepared on the basis of a number of factors, primarily linked to road condition. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report for Ards and North Down which is to be presented to Council on 11 October 2022. The Report will then be published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.  A web-link will also be issued to the respective MLAs in the Council area.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a proposed new school build for Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that Planning Approval for the proposed layout was received on 15th June 2022.The Education Authority has submitted a Business Case to my Department’s Finance Division for approval. Once this has been approved, the project will progress to RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a proposed new school build for Holywood Primary School.
answer_text: Holywood Primary School is not currently within the Department’s Major Capital Investment Programme of announced schemes.  It is the responsibility of the Education Authority, as the school Managing Authority, to submit the school for consideration for a new build at any future Major Capital Works Call.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a proposed new school build for Bangor Central Primary School.
answer_text: Bangor Central Integrated Primary School has been selected for a major capital investment project to improve or replace its existing accommodation.  The project will cater for the school’s current approved enrolment of 618 pupils.The planning application for the new school at Balloo Road in Bangor has been lodged with Ards & North Down Borough Council and was validated in August 2022.  The Planning Authority have issued their Statutory Consultations and neighbours have been notified.  The scheme was advertised in the local press in September 2022.  The design team will monitor the progress of the application and respond to any queries raised through this process.In tandem with the planning application the design team are working with the school stakeholders to complete the final design stage, which will allow the project to be tendered.  The pre-qualification process to short list contractors to tender for the construction of the project is due to commence shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether plans for a badger cull are on hold due to a Judicial Review.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) breakdowns can have devastating financial and emotional impacts on farming families across Northern Ireland. The disease also costs the public purse around £40 million per annum. The eradication of bTB from Northern Ireland’s cattle herd therefore remains one of my top priorities.My Department’s new bTB Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland aims to reduce, and eventually eradicate, bovine TB from the cattle herd by comprehensively addressing all the key factors, including the role played by wildlife, which are recognised to contribute to the maintenance and spread of the disease.  I want to see all the actions within the Strategy implemented as soon as possible. This includes the proposals for time limited badger removal in specific intervention areas where there is a high density of badgers and high incidence of bTB in herds. Following this it is the Departments intention to bring forward a programme of badger vaccination to protect both wildlife and cattle from this dreadful disease. The specific proposals relating to the wildlife intervention elements of the Strategy are currently subject to ongoing judicial review proceedings which will be required to conclude prior to any implementation of badger removal on the ground.My Officials will however, continue to work closely with industry to develop the necessary processes and guidance for a badger removal programme in Northern Ireland, and develop the relevant legislation required for an intervention to take place. 	It remains my intention to see this delivered as quickly as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans for a safe road crossing area for residents living on the Belfast Road, Bangor.
answer_text: When installing crossings my Department must adhere to modern safety standards and unfortunately, due to a number of factors including the proximity of junctions and locations of businesses, it was not possible to identify a safe and suitable place to provide additional pedestrian measures at Belfast Road in Bangor. My officials have considered if there are any alternative nearby locations that would address the needs of pedestrians, however unfortunately I have been advised there are no other options available that would meet current safety standards and as such the proposal has been removed from the current work programme.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to reduce the speed limit on the A48 Cotton Road.
answer_text: I refer to my previous responses to AQW 1766/22-27 and AQW 1769/22-27 which issued in July 2022.  I am advised that a review was undertaken by my Department within the past year which concluded that the existing national speed limit was appropriate, however I have asked officials to review this position once the findings from an ongoing PSNI investigation are known.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the sewage system upgrade for Cannyreagh Road, Donaghadee.
answer_text: The status of work at wastewater infrastructure across the North is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how home heating oil customers in Northern Ireland will receive a one-off payment of £100 to help with rising oil costs.
answer_text: The Prime Minister’s announcement on 21st September 2022 included United Kingdom Government’s (UKG) further plans to support households in Northern Ireland. Domestic consumers will be supported by the Energy Price Guarantee Scheme. This scheme will provide a £100 payment to those who do not use natural gas to heat their homes but use alternative fuels, for example oil, coal etc. The scheme will also apply a discount to domestic consumers’ electricity and natural gas tariffs, backdated to 1st October and will be in place until April 2023.More details of the energy support schemes are available on the government website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the £400 Energy Bills Support payment will be made to NI residents.
answer_text: The United Kingdom Government (UKG) announced the £400 support in April and the Chancellor stated on the floor of the House in May that “we will legislate to deliver this support on the same terms in every part of the United Kingdom, including Northern Ireland.”  I called for UKG to provide the support to local households as quickly as possible.The current plan, which is not yet finalised, is for the payment of £400 to be provided by electricity suppliers directly to electricity consumers in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will be commencing a significant energy efficiency scheme next spring.
answer_text: Within the Northern Ireland Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero Energy, there is a commitment to deliver an energy efficiency pilot in 2022. In the scoping and development of this initial pilot it has become clear that more urgent action, with much more ambition in terms of scale of intervention, is needed to support people across Northern Ireland. This situation has of course been exacerbated by the current global energy price crisis.With that in mind, my officials are now scoping the development of a multi-year Energy Efficiency Flagship Intervention Programme that will scale up quickly to deliver the volume of retrofits required to both meet our net zero commitments and tackle the cost-of-living crisis. While our initial focus will be on the most vulnerable and the worst performing homes, the programme will then broaden out to take in all homes in Northern Ireland and determine appropriate levels of funding required to ensure this happens.The launch of this flagship will also be a signal for the energy efficiency industry to invest in people and their skills, which in turn will stimulate local economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the National Insulation Association and its members have withdrawn support for her Department's Affordable Warmth Scheme.
answer_text: The National Insulation Association of Ireland notified the Department in June 2022 that its members had withdrawn from the Affordable Warmth Scheme. Officials continue to engage with the Chair of the Association to seek resolution and return of its members to the Scheme.  The Scheme continues to operate with contractors who are not members of the Association.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the reasons for the delay in the resurfacing of Beechwood Avenue, Beechwood Gardens, Hawthorne Drive and Silverstream Drive.
answer_text: (i) Expenditure on Active Travel, including cycle paths for Roads Northern Division in each of the last three financial years, 2019-20 to 2021-22 has been provided in the Table 1 below. Table 1Roads Division2019-202020-212021-22NorthernActive Travel£’k6909992,809Active Travel expenditure includes Pedestrian and Cycling Measures. Additionally there may be some active travel expenditure carried out as part of network development and Strategic Road Improvement projects, however, it is not possible to disaggregate that investment from the relevant scheme expenditure. (ii) Expenditure on Structural Maintenance for Roads Northern Division has been provided in Table 2 for 2019-20 to 2021-22 as follows:Table 2Roads Division2019-202020-212021-22NorthernCapital and Resource Structural Maintenance£’k25,95124,41129,989Structural Maintenance includes Carriageway and Footway resurfacing, Patching (repair of defects), Surface Dressing, Carriageway refurbishment and Structural Drainage and Stability.Expenditure outturns will have been determined by budget availability and capacity in a given year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his officials have met with Ards and North Down Council to discuss the level of debt the council has with his Department.
answer_text: The Local Government Finance Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 places a duty on each council to ascertain the appropriateness of any loan and the purpose for which it is being sought, to determine and keep under review how much money it can afford to borrow and to remain within its affordable borrowing limit at all times.I can confirm that no officials from my department have met with Ards and North Down Council to discuss the level of debt the council has with the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the 2022/23 pay award for the Civil Service and related arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: I have been liaising with officials how far public sector pay can be progressed in the absence of an agreed budget. I met recognised trade unions on 28 September 2022 to discuss the very significant budgetary and political issues that we face at this time. Civil servants due to receive an increase under the terms of any pay award for 2022 will receive any arrears backdated to 1 August 2022 when negotiations are completed.My Department does not set pay awards for staff in arm’s-length bodies (ALBs); as employers, these are determined by the bodies themselves.  However, where an ALB has chosen to mirror the Civil Service pay award for its own staff groups, my Department has put a new streamlined approval process in place to help ensure the payment of such awards are not delayed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to the list roads in North Down in need of resurfacing.
answer_text: I can confirm that, as previously advised under AQW 20134/17-22 and AQW 1034/22-27, the Department no longer maintains a list of roads within the Ards and North Down area that are in need of resurfacing.Resurfacing programmes in Ards and North Down are now, as in other areas, being prepared on the basis of a number of factors primarily linked to road condition. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report for Ards and North Down which is now being developed following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. In due course the Report will be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans are in place to facilitate payments for the Energy Bills Support Scheme.
answer_text: As I announced on 26 August 2022, the British Government has confirmed that it will deliver the £400 Energy Bill Support Scheme here in the North.I made it clear to the Chancellor and Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy that any delay in issuing these payments to citizens here is completely unacceptable, and I have written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury seeking clarity on when payments will be made. Despite this, the British Government has yet to provide an update on how or when people here will receive the payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans there are to build a new mental health facility beside Tor Bank School in Dundonald.
answer_text: The original plan for South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) was to provide Acute Psychiatry and PICU services at a new build at Tor Bank site, Dundonald.The Trust are currently revising the Outline Business Case (OBC) to re-visit the analysis of need, refresh available options and to address increases in cost.  We await receipt of the OBC which will set out the options for the new facility including the location and will be subject to the normal approval process within both the Department of Health and the Department of Finance.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care including the provision of a new mental health facility for the South Eastern Trust area, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. However, my ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty, which is being increasing felt as a result of the absence of a functioning Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will give a guarantee that all MLA representatives for North Down will be informed of any public visits to health facilities in their constituency.
answer_text: I can confirm that I will continue to follow the Executive Committee agreed guidance issued in March 2020 in relation to visits.  This guidance states ‘Ministers intending to make an official visit within Northern Ireland must inform MLAs for the relevant area. It is recognised that there will be occasions when visits are organised or the details confirmed at short notice, but as much advance notice as possible should be given’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether trees will be cut down at the Grey Point Fort site, Helen's Bay, under the new redevelopment plans for the Fort.
answer_text: Any future development or conservation work by my department at Grey Point Fort will be guided by the Conservation Management Plan for the site which is currently being finalised following a public consultation event.  The plan advises that tree surveys should be carried out for the site every five years, considering both plant health and public safety, with resulting actions implemented. The Conservation Management Plan recommends that, subject to the necessary statutory consents, trees to the north of the Fort should be reduced in height to reinstate the historic views of Belfast Lough and should be re-coppiced on a five yearly basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will agree to a public meeting with patients belonging to the Priory and Springhill surgeries.
answer_text: I appreciate that this is a worrying time for registered patients of the practice, and indeed, the wider population in the Holywood and Bangor areas.  I also understand the importance of keeping patients and the wider public updated regarding developments in the search to find a replacement doctor/practice.Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) Officers have written to all registered patients aged 16 and above.  The letter provides assurance that the Surgery will remain open until 1st February 2023 whilst the search for a replacement contractor to care for the patients of Priory & Springhill Surgery is carried out.  A dedicated SPPG telephone number was provided for patient enquiries.SPPG Communications Officers continue to respond to media enquiries and have issued an initial statement to local MLAs, MPs and councilors as well as update statements to the local press.  The messaging on the practice website has also been updated to provide reassurance to patients that the practice will remain open until 1st February 2023 whilst the search for new doctors is carried out.SPPG Officers have initiated a process to find a replacement contractor to take over the care of patients from 1st February 2023 and I feel that their efforts are best spent on these endeavors at this time.  Patients and the wider public will continue to be kept informed of any significant developments.  Patients have been provided with a dedicated SPPG telephone number and can make enquiries at any time during business hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1034/22-27 and AQW 1133/22-27, why he has not provided the information in relation to road resurfacing across North Down.
answer_text: I can confirm that, as previously explained in AQW 20134/17-22, a reserve list for resurfacing work is no longer maintained for the Ards and North Down Council area. A list of resurfacing schemes is developed when funding allocations are made available to each DfI Roads Division and is based on road condition at that time.  I would also refer you to a previous response, AQW 23078/17-22, which outlines the need to present the Department’s Report to each Council before its general release.Resurfacing programmes within the Ards & North Down area are now, as is the case for other areas, being finalised following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report to Ards and North Down which will in due course be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many agency staff have not yet received their £500 special COVID recognition payment.
answer_text: The Department does not currently maintain a record of the total number of Agency health care workers employed in the independent sector.As I am sure you are aware the Special Recognition Payment Scheme is being taken forward in a number of phases. I can confirm that a letter was issued on 28 June 2022 by Business Services Organisation (BSO) to Agencies registered with RQIA asking them to identify employees who were contracted in the independent care sector during the relevant qualifying time period. The letter detailed the relevant qualifying criteria and outlined how to submit claims on behalf of their employees. Claims should be submitted before 31 July 2022.Whether staff worked for the full qualifying period or in part, it is the Agency’s responsibility to ensure a claim is submitted and make subsequent arrangements to pass this payment on to the relevant individual.I can confirm that as at 29th June 2022, under Phase 1 of the Special Recognition Payment scheme which covers Agency staff who worked in the statutory sector, a total spend of over £2.23m has been paid out to 5,664 individuals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether William Street, Donaghadee is on the reserve list for resurfacing work.
answer_text: I can confirm that, as previously explained in AQW 20134/17-22, no reserve list for resurfacing work is held for the Ards and North Down Council area.Resurfacing programmes within the Ards & North Down area are now, as is the case for other areas, being finalised following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report to Ards and North Down which will in due course be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Ashbury Grove, Bangor, is on the reserve list for resurfacing work.
answer_text: I can confirm that, as previously explained in AQW 20134/17-22, no reserve list for resurfacing work is held for the Ards and North Down Council area.Resurfacing programmes within the Ards & North Down area are now, as is the case for other areas, being finalised following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report to Ards and North Down which will in due course be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the protocol that is followed to notify MLAs of a visit by the Minister to a hospital in their constituency.
answer_text: In March 2020 the Executive committee agreed guidance which set out the procedures to be followed by Ministers in the effective exercise of their duties and responsibilities and in circumstances where those duties and responsibilities may engage with personal, party political or business interests. In relation to visits the guidance states ‘Ministers intending to make an official visit within Northern Ireland must inform the MLAs for the relevant area. It is recognised that there will be occasions when visits are organised or the details confirmed at short notice, but as much advance notice as possible should be given’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many retrospective planning applications have been submitted to Ards and North Down Council over the last four year period.
answer_text: Following the transfer of planning powers to Council in April 2015, the information you seek should be requested from Ards and North Down Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the plans for Grey Point Fort, Helen's Bay.
answer_text: My officials have produced a draft conservation management plan for the State Care Monument.  They are currently liaising with community stakeholders to arrange a virtual public meeting in July/August 2022 to discuss the conservation management plan and aspirations for the future of the site.This summer the fort is open to the public on the last Saturday of June, July and August and also for European Heritage Open Day on Saturday 10th September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why NI Water erected fencing at Seacourt Lane without first receiving planning permission.
answer_text: The management of fencing at Seacourt Lane is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why NI Water have erected fencing at Seacourt Lane, Bangor.
answer_text: The management of fencing at Seacourt Lane Bangor is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to introduce legislation to regulate the use of two wheeled electric scooters.
answer_text: The position remains that it is illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces in the North. You may be aware that the Department for Transport has recently announced its intention to create regulations which will legalise the use of electric scooters in England. The proposed change in regulations in England will not apply here. However, my officials are currently monitoring developments there and in due course I will consider the way forward for the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why Councillors are informed of new road projects before MLAs.
answer_text: Article 128 of The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a statutory duty on my Department to consult with Councils.  To fulfil this obligation my Department normally meets with Councils in the spring and autumn of each year.Prior to each meeting, my Department presents the Council with Report for the area.  Autumn reports typically include details of progress on the year’s works programme and officials take the opportunity to seek council’s views on schemes that might be included in the following year’s programme.  Spring reports include details of the work completed in the previous year and work proposed for the coming year.  These reports are issued to Council officials a few days in advance of the meeting when they are to be presented, for prior distribution to councillors and are subsequently published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library shortly after the Council meeting.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the plans in Westminster to stop pensioners using the Post office to get their pensions and move to a bank account system will be introduced to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Post Office card account (POca) contract that was used by customers as the main payment exception service is due to end in August 2022. To replace POca, a new payment exception service (nPES) was launched on 2nd August 2021.nPES users can use the PayPoint and Post Office network to cash vouchers giving them access to funds across a wider spectrum of locations. All POca customers who are moved across to nPES are issued with a card to access their payments. Further information is available on NI Direct - https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/post-office-card-accounts

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what is his Department doing to support Clandeboye Golf Club to host the Northern Ireland Masters PGA EuroPro Golf event.
answer_text: As the PGA EuroPro Tour is Europe’s leading development tour, the tournament received financial support of £6,000 from the Tourism Northern Ireland National Tourism Events Sponsorship Scheme for the 2022 tournament. The NI Masters has been promoted in spring campaign activity in both GB and the Republic of Ireland in the Irish Golfer and National Club Golfer magazines, as well as in a supplement with Bunkered Golf Breaks. The tournament has also been listed in the Tourism Northern Ireland summer campaign supplement and will continue to be promoted throughout the summer campaign. During the event Tourism Northern Ireland will supply Northern Ireland Made for Golf branding to the event, as well as promotional support through its paid and organic social channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what is her Department doing to support Clandeboye Golf Club to host the Northern Ireland Masters PGA EuroPro Golf event.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for funding events primarily rests with Department for Economy and Tourism NI.At my request, officials from my Department met with you and representatives of Clandeboye Golf Club on 23 June 2022 to discuss this year’s Masters PGA EuroPro Golf event. At this meeting, Clandeboye GC were advised that neither the Department, nor Sport NI, have any funding programmes available which the club could apply to in order to support this golf event.  I understand that officials from Sport NI, an Arm’s Length Body of my Department, have also engaged separately with the club on the matter. DfC officials contacted Tourism NI to advise that a meeting had taken place and to highlight the Club’s request for financial support to host this year’s tournament.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many prosecutions have occurred for building on or disturbing a badger sett over the last five years.
answer_text: Badgers are protected under the terms of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 which is enforced by the PSNI.  My Department does not have details on the number of prosecutions for building on or disturbing a badger sett over the last five years.  The PSNI may be able to assist and provide the requested detail.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures can be put in place to sustain a badger sett that is under threat of development.
answer_text: Badgers setts are occasionally located in areas that are proposed for various types of developments, e.g. to accommodate social or private housing needs.  In these situations, the local planning authority will initially ask the developer to provide them with the results of a badger survey of the area, (undertaken by a professional ecologist) before any further consideration can be given to granting full planning permission.  The badger survey report will give specific details of the number and types of badger setts in the area and propose various actions / mitigation to facilitate the development. It is illegal to disturb a badger whilst it is in its sett and therefore NIEA advises that any work within 25 metres of a badger sett may require a licence.  There may also be other recommendations to minimise disturbance, depending on the local conditions.  The 25 metre protection zone should be clearly demarcated by using coloured tape or some other form of visible marking. Scrub and vegetation should not be cleared from the sett area.  All contractors and sub-contractors should be notified of the presence of badgers and instructed to keep out of the protection zone. Planting the protection zone with dense native shrubs can enhance the long term protection of a sett.  In some cases the addition of a ‘buffer zone’ of underdeveloped land between the nearest gardens and the periphery of the protection zone will further enhance badger security. Badgers foraging needs should be taken into account during the planning consideration stage.  This may result in the construction of various ‘wildlife corridors’ to permit travel around various parts of a badger group’s home range to facilitate feeding opportunities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether car parking places provided for Housing Executive tenants are for the exclusive use of those tenants.
answer_text: The Housing Executive in tandem with DfI Roads provides parking provision in a number of ways:At some properties in-curtilage parking is available and is for the use of the occupier and their visitors.At some tower blocks, for example at Rathcoole and Belvoir, there is controlled access to the surrounding communal areas and parking spaces, the likelihood therefore is that these spaces will primarily be used by the Housing Executive tenants and occupants of leasehold properties, their visitors and others who have cause to visit the blocks.Other communal parking provision, for example parking laybys and parking areas, are for general use and not exclusively for tenants.    There may be occasions in which parking spaces in communal areas have been designated for disabled parking, these are available for the use of any blue badge holder.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many badgers are required to make the sett sustainable.
answer_text: Badgers are social animals and live in groups. Each social group will normally have a number of setts within a defined territory, and the size of these groups is related to the suitability of the habitat and the availability of food. There are normally different types of setts within a socials group’s range which differ in terms of their size and frequency of use. For example, main setts are normally larger and in continuous use whilst outlier setts are smaller and tend to be used on an intermittent basis.   The social group size of badgers can range from 2 to around 20 individuals, although some badgers are known to live alone. If the reference to a ‘sett’ is considered to mean a ‘social group’ then the sustainability of the sett would require at least a breeding pair. All badger setts (regardless of size or frequency of use) are protected under the terms of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how much foraging space is required for a badger to survive.
answer_text: Badgers are omnivores and have an extremely varied diet. Their favoured food items are earthworms and in areas where these are in high abundance they have can consume several hundred a night.  In areas with lower earthworm densities they will switch to other invertebrates such as slugs, snails, and various grubs. Badgers also eat carrion, rodents and birds eggs, along with bulbs, cereals and fruits when seasonally available. Badgers in urban areas are often fed in gardens by homeowners and will exploit food scattered around bird tables.  The availability and types of food resources in badger group territories will vary significantly, and individual badgers are also likely to have different energy requirements. It is therefore not possible to quantify the minimum foraging space required for a badger to survive, but individual badger social groups will have territories in the range of 5 to 300 Ha,  area increasing as food abundance decreases.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to deal with MOT test backlogs, which are preventing car owners from being able to tax their cars and has resulted in car owners facing prosecution.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; andkeep checking the booking system for an earlier appointment.If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test  and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

answer_text: Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure here are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). The OZEV website can be viewed, via the following links, where details of funding to support the early market for ultra-low emission vehicles (ULEVs) can be found:https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/office-for-zero-emission-vehicles

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the current health policy on the distribution of defibrillators in all health service premises.
answer_text: There is no Department of Health policy dealing with the distribution of defibrillators in all health service premises.  However, the Health & Social Care Trusts endeavour to follow the quality standards developed by the Resuscitation Council UK.  The quality standards can be accessed at: https://www.resus.org.uk/library/quality-standards-cpr   For healthcare premises owned/occupied by other Departmental Arm’s Length Bodies, it would be a matter for the organisation to consider the needs of their staff and visitors and undertake a risk assessment.  Should it wish to install a defibrillator it can approach the NI Ambulance Service for advice. As part of the regional Community Resuscitation Strategy (2014), the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) Community Resuscitation Team is funded to provide a supporting role in advising facilities or bodies who wish to site a defibrillator on their premises. The NIAS guidance documents can be found at: https://nias.hscni.net/our-services/aed/ These guidance documents are available to anyone who has a defibrillator or is considering purchasing one. The documents include best practice guidance for Automated External Defibrillators, information on cabinets and frequently asked questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has for the installation of defibrillators in all public buildings.
answer_text: The Department has no plans to fund the installation defibrillators in all public buildings.  The Department launched the Community Resuscitation Strategy in July 2014 with the aim of improving the survival rate for those who suffer an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.  The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), working with the Public Health Agency and the Health and Social Care Board, led on the action commitments set out in the Strategy. As part of the regional Community Resuscitation Strategy, the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team is funded to provide a supporting role in advising facilities or bodies who wish to site a defibrillator on their premises. Locations of publicly accessible Automatic External Defibrillators across Northern Ireland can be viewed on the NIAS interactive map at: AED Locations Across Northern Ireland | Northern Ireland Ambulance Service Health & Social Care Trust (hscni.net)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the reasons for the length of time taken to fill a job vacancy in the Health Service.
answer_text: Agenda for Change postsThe HSC does not specifically report on the average time to fill a job vacancy for a number of reasons including:Limitations of the HR / Recruitment systems reporting functionality; andComplexity of differing pipeline options which can be impacted upon by a wide range of factors.The key issues impacting recruitment timelines will be different depending on the nature of the role being recruited.  BSO report some of the key reasons to be:Labour Market Forcesthe shortage of supply in key professional areas including but not limited to certain medical specialities, Nursing, Social Work, Allied Health Professions, Pharmacy;Limited pools of suitable applicants necessitating wider promotion of posts and recruitment processes, including international recruitment; andLower levels of unemployment and competing with private sector for the same people.DemandUnprecedented demand to meet increasing service needs across the HSC; andCompeting service pressures on managers and recruitment teams within the Trust.Resources Outdated technology which relies on many manual processes;Resourcing challenges within teams responsible for recruitment due to high demand and other service pressures.Medical & Dental positions often require approval from the Royal Colleges and Royal College representation on selection panel with additional lead in time to selection required to facilitate this.Medical and Dental positionsOther recruitment for Medical and Dental positions is managed by Trust Retained Recruitment and Medical HR teams. The HSC is working collaboratively to continually respond to the above challenges including:A review of the current recruitment model and processes across HSC; Development of a range of Attraction strategies such as of online / Visual careers information as well as improving engagement with universities to attract students to the HSC by promoting the wide range of careers which facilitate service delivery;Implementation of streamlined approaches to Pipeline Management for different staff groups; andImplementation of new software to further enhance the Governance, Experience and speed of the Pre-employment Checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will undertake a review of the speed limit on the A48 Cotton Road, Donaghadee.
answer_text: I was very saddened to hear of the recent fatal collision on the A48 Cotton Road and extend my sympathies to the family and friends of the man who tragically lost his life. You will also be aware that all fatal collisions are investigated by the PSNI, who will in due course share any relevant findings with officials from my Department.Regarding the speed limit on the road, I am advised that a review was undertaken by my Department within the past year which concluded that the existing national speed limit was appropriate. I have asked officials to review this position once the findings from the PSNI investigation are known.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why grass cuttings along central reservations are not collected.
answer_text: The Department undertakes grass cutting for safety reasons and to prevent overgrowth of carriageways and footway surfaces and the obstruction of sight lines and traffic signs, however, it is not undertaken for aesthetic or amenity purposes. Under current policy grass cuttings are not collected across the road network, including along central reservations. However I am determined to do all that I can to protect important habitats and I have asked officials to review the existing policy and explore options such as the introduction of grass cutting earlier in the year and lifting grass cuttings to help protect the environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the cost to vaccinate the 781 badgers that were tested and vaccinated against Bovine TB in the Banbridge area.
answer_text: A research project was undertaken by the Department between 2014 and 2018 to consider the effects of implementing the Test and Vaccinate or Remove (TVR) approach to address tuberculosis in badgers, in terms of logistical implementation and with a view to developing expertise. During the project 781 unique badgers were vaccinated at least once. The total cost of the project was approximately £4.5M with overall TVR costs broken down into staff costs, training, laboratory work and consumables/other.  It is not however possible to extract the cost of vaccinating badgers  from the overall TVR costs as, for example, some staff costs, training and capital equipment were not dependent on the whether a captured animal has been vaccinated.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, of the 781 Badgers vaccinated and tested for Bovine TB, how many of these badgers actually had Bovine TB when tested.
answer_text: The Test, Vaccinate or Remove (TVR) research project which took place over five years in a 100 square kilometre area of County Down, concluded its field work in 2019.	During this project, 824 unique badgers were captured, some of which were captured and tested more than once.  There were 1,520 badger captures in total, 146 of which gave a positive result to the Dual Path Platform (sett-side) blood test for bovine TB. Over the course of the project 781 unique badgers were vaccinated at least once.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, of the 781 Badgers vaccinated against Bovine TB in the Banbridge area, what were the actual results in terms of the percentage reduction in Bovine TB in cattle in that area.
answer_text: My Department undertook a five year research  project over a 100km2 area around Banbridge in County Down to consider the effects of implementing the Test and Vaccinate or Remove (TVR) approach on badgers in an area with high levels of confirmed bTB in cattle and high cattle herd and badger density. Its purpose was to develop expertise within the Department in relation to the TVR of badgers and identify the operational field logistics as well as the associated potential costs of a wildlife intervention. Whilst valuable field data and deployment experience was gained through the project results, it did not provide evidence to determine the effectiveness of TVR as a means of reducing TB in cattle. To determine the effectiveness of TVR as means of controlling TB in cattle, similar activity in other study areas would have been required for comparison.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education since the closure of the Cotton Primary School in Newtownards, (i) how much has the school cost to maintain; and (ii) what is the cost in rates to the Education Authority.
answer_text: The maintenance costs since 2014 for the former Cotton Primary School were £1749.58.The rates costs to the Education Authority from 2014/15 to 2021/22 were £25,638.68.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average waiting time for a police report to arrive with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive for the allocation of points under the Housing Selection Scheme.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it does not collect metrics on the time taken for a police report to be received and therefore cannot provide an average waiting time for a police report to be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the plans for the former Cotton Primary School site, Newtownards.
answer_text: The former Cotton Primary School is being retained by the Education Authority for potential alternative educational use by Children and Young People's Services for Special Educational Needs Provision.  The review on its potential usage is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how the cost of living package announced by the Chancellor will be distributed to households in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The package of measures announced by the Chancellor on 26th May included the following financial support: - £650 for those on low income support; £300 for pensioner support; and £150 targeted for those on disability benefits.  These payments will be automatically applied through the welfare system.  Treasury are continuing to examine possible options to deliver equivalent support here in relation to the Household Energy Bills Support Scheme. I met with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 22 June and my Department will continue this engagement to ensure a solution is found to deliver support locally.In addition, a Barnett consequential of approximately £14 million will be provided in relation to the extension of the Household Support Fund.  The distribution of this funding will require an Executive decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what services in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust have yet to return to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: The Southern Trust is working to return services to pre-pandemic levels of core activity, but challenges as a result of enhanced infection control measures, workforce and estate issues continue to impact service delivery.The following services have demonstrated a trajectory in April and May 2022 which indicates they are around pre-pandemic levels (allowing for some natural variation):Out Patient ServicesIn-Patient Surgical ServicesDiagnostic Imaging Mental Health ServicesCommunity Paediatric Out-Patient ServicesThe following services have yet to reach pre-pandemic levels:Endoscopy / Day Case Surgical ServicesCancer Wait TimesAllied Health Professional ServicesDay Centre / Day Opportunities ServicesCommunity Dental Services

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have yet to return to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: The Northern Trust is working hard to return services to pre-pandemic levels, but challenges as a result of social distancing, enhanced infection control measures and workforce issues continue to impact on service delivery.  The following services have demonstrated a trajectory in April and May 2022 which indicates they are nearing pre-pandemic levels (allowing for some natural variation) - outpatient services;diagnostic imaging; and allied health professions (except for physio and occupational therapy).The following services have yet to return to pre-pandemic levels – 	Service					inpatient/daycase surgery 			CAMHS 						day centres 						adult short breaks 					district nursing 					health visiting 					autism (children’s)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what new crematorium planning applications have been submitted for approval; and (ii) their locations in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department is not currently dealing with any planning applications for crematoriums. A planning application for a crematoriums is not considered to be a regionally significant application as defined by the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (DM Regulations), as amended, and the jurisdiction for such an application would be for local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the new 5G mast planning applications that have been submitted to his Department for approval; and (ii) their locations across Bangor and Donaghadee .
answer_text: The Department is not currently dealing with any planning applications for 5G masts. A planning application for a 5G mast is not considered to be a regionally significant application as defined by the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (DM Regulations), as amended, and the jurisdiction for such an application would be for local councils, in this case Ards and North Down Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Donaghadee Health Centre is no longer providing travel vaccinations.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  Under the Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contract) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004, there are some travel vaccinations that GPs are paid to provide to patients who are entitled to them under the Health Service.  Travel vaccines available under the Health Service are: choleradiphtheria, polio and tetanus boosterhepatitis AtyphoidVaccines that are not available on the Health Service can be provided by a GP Practice under a private arrangement or alternatively through a specialised Travel Clinic. The GMS Regulations allow for General Practitioners to levy a fee for travel vaccinations that are outside the Health Service and for which they do not receive payment as part of their GMS contract.  Donaghadee Health Centre’s website sets out information for patients on travel vaccination. It notes that the Practice only provides NHS travel vaccinations.  If a patient requires vaccinations which are available on the NHS, they will be given the opportunity of having these, along with the associated travel advice, free of charge at the Practice.The Practice has agreed to update the travel vaccination information on their website.  Patients will be advised that vaccinations that are available on the Health Service (as detailed above) and the related advice, are available at the Practice, free of charge, without the need to first attend a Travel Clinic at a different site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what services provided by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have not returned to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust is committed to returning services back to pre-pandemic levels as quickly as possible.  The Trust has a service delivery plan in place with agreed performance measures, and performance against this plan is closely monitored and reported to the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group on a monthly basis.  During May 2022, the following services delivered less than 90% of the activity delivered in the pre-pandemic baseline (May 2019). The Trust continues to prioritise and focus on treating the most urgent cases first, and as a result some patients will have to wait longer than we would like. PhysiotherapyOccupational therapyPodiatry  DementiaChildren’s AutismCommunity DentalEndoscopy (April 19 vs April 22)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what powers he has to make decisions in the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: I continue to have the power of direction and control over the Department of Finance, and can also exercise any other functions vested to me directly as Minister.  However, in the absence of a functioning Executive, taking decisions on matters that ordinarily would require referral to the Executive carries the risk of successful legal challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what powers she has to make decisions in the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: I am required under the terms of the Ministerial Code to refer certain matters to the Executive for agreement. These include significant or controversial matters and matters which cut across the responsibility of two or more Ministers.I am continuing to carry out all my ministerial duties within my executive authority and I am deeply frustrated at the lack of a functioning Executive, however, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what powers he has to make decisions in the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: From the Office of the Minister 	Gordon Lyons MLA									As per the Departments (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, as Minister I continue to exercise executive authority within my Department.		As always, if a matter is deemed cross cutting or controversial I would however ensure that it is brought to the attention of Ministerial colleagues as required under the Ministerial Code.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what powers he has to make decisions in the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: Each Minister exercises executive authority within their department, but is required under the terms of the Ministerial Code to refer certain categories of matter to the Executive for agreement.  These include significant or controversial matters and matters which cut across the responsibility of two or more Ministers
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what streets in North Down are scheduled for footpath resurfacing work.

In due course the Report will be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on how many pupils applying to pre-school have not been provided with a school place across North Down.
answer_text: The Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) has a two-stage admissions process which is based on parental preference.  Stages 1 and 2 of the process ended on 28 April and 16 June 2022 respectively. At 22 June, the Education Authority (EA) has advised, that all children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school place across North Down, and stayed with the process to the end have been offered a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. Funded pre-school education places remain available and a list of settings is available on the EA website. Any parent who did not fully engage with the process may still access a funded place for their child by contacting pre-school education settings directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what streets in North Down are scheduled for road calming measures.
answer_text: Details of work programmes, including traffic calming measures, for the 2022/23 financial year will be published within my Department’s Council Report for Ards and North Down which is now being finalised following recent confirmation of my Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. In due course the Report will be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on how many pupils applying to Year 1 have not been provided with a school place across North Down.
answer_text: As at 9 June 2022, no children who reside in North Down who had applied to Year 1 (primary) school for September 2022 admission remain unplaced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a list of roads that are in need of resurfacing in North Down.
answer_text: I can confirm that, as previously advised under AQW 20134/17/22, my officials no longer maintain a list of roads within the Ards and North Down area that are in need of resurfacing.Resurfacing programmes in Ards and North Down are now, as in other areas, being prepared on the basis of a number of factors primarily linked to road condition. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Council Report for Ards and North Down which is now being developed following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. In due course the Report will be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a list of streets in North Down for which new street lighting is planned.

Work programmes are now being finalised following recent confirmation of the Division’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation.
