question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the call by Macmillan Cancer Support for an annual progress report on closing health inequalities in cancer care.
answer_text: My Department are currently progressing necessary measures to deal with health inequalities in cancer care. This is a common theme across many of the key actions identified in the new Cancer Strategy which was published on 22 March 2022, and these actions will help to close identified inequalities in cancer care.  During the development of the Cancer Strategy much work was undertaken to better understand workforce need, including nursing requirements. This included my Department commissioning a census of the nurses and support staff delivering cancer care in September 2021 from Macmillan Cancer Support which has recently been published. (https://www.macmillan.org.uk/dfsmedia/1a6f23537f7f4519bb0cf14c45b2a629/5837-10061/2021_Census_of_the_cancer_workforce_in_NI)We have identified the need for more than 100 additional cancer clinical nurse specialist across Northern Ireland over the next 10 years. A workforce planning steering group is being established to oversee the development and implementation of a multi-professional workforce plan and associated training plan to support improvement of cancer services.Whilst planning is underway, in the context of the ongoing budget uncertainty the timing for implementation is currently unknown. My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty and there are continues to be pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  Funding elements of the health service on a month-to-month or quarter-to-quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will implement the recommendations of the Bengoa Report in full.
answer_text: Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together sets out my Department’s response to the recommendations included in the Bengoa Report.  Delivering Together sets out to stabilise, reconfigure and to transform Health and Social Care services.  Since the launch of Delivering Together in October 2016, three reports on the progress of the ring-fenced transformation funding projects have been published in 2017, 2019 and 2021.  These reports are available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/progressreportintroduction.  The evidence contained within these three progress reports, particularly the most recent report, confirms that this programme of activity continues to be successful in stabilising, reconfiguring and improving our health and social care services.I have also published strategies for mental health, elective care and cancer services and have launched a consultation on urgent and emergency care.  Together, with Health and Wellbeing 2026, these strategies articulate a clear trajectory to stabilise, reconfigure and improve our Health and Social Care Services.  I have however made it clear that delivering this agenda will require sustained recurrent funding and support for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the call from Macmillan Cancer Support for the funding of an extra 100 cancer nurse specialists.
answer_text: My Department are currently progressing necessary measures to deal with health inequalities in cancer care. This is a common theme across many of the key actions identified in the new Cancer Strategy which was published on 22 March 2022, and these actions will help to close identified inequalities in cancer care.  During the development of the Cancer Strategy much work was undertaken to better understand workforce need, including nursing requirements. This included my Department commissioning a census of the nurses and support staff delivering cancer care in September 2021 from Macmillan Cancer Support which has recently been published. (https://www.macmillan.org.uk/dfsmedia/1a6f23537f7f4519bb0cf14c45b2a629/5837-10061/2021_Census_of_the_cancer_workforce_in_NI)We have identified the need for more than 100 additional cancer clinical nurse specialist across Northern Ireland over the next 10 years. A workforce planning steering group is being established to oversee the development and implementation of a multi-professional workforce plan and associated training plan to support improvement of cancer services.Whilst planning is underway, in the context of the ongoing budget uncertainty the timing for implementation is currently unknown. My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty and there are continues to be pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  Funding elements of the health service on a month-to-month or quarter-to-quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the criteria for disabled people accessing transport from the Ambulance Service for hospital appointments.
answer_text: The Department of Health has a statutory duty under the Health and Personal Social Services (Northern Ireland) Order 1972 Article 10 to “make arrangements, to such extent as it considers necessary, for providing or securing the provision of ambulances and other means of transport for the conveyance of persons suffering from illness, expectant or nursing mothers or others persons for whom such transport is reasonably required in order to avail themselves of any service under this Order”.As a general principle, patients should be expected to make their own way to hospital, either using their own transport, or that of friends and relatives, or by using a number of schemes that exist to help people who have transport difficulties. Eligibility for access to non-emergency transport services is based on medical need as determined by a medical practitioner.  The definition of medical need is: “Where the patient’s condition could be significantly worsened if there were a failure to provide transport or, where the patient’s condition is of such severity that it renders them unfit to travel by any other means, except non-emergency ambulance transport, or some other form of non-emergency transport”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the original cohort of nursing staff from the Bangor Minor Injuries Unit have not been returned to their pre-pandemic roles.
answer_text: The reopening of the unit is an operational decision for the HSC Trust, which has already been explained in detail in AQW 57/22-27.Some staff within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have chosen to move to different roles and whilst recruitment is ongoing the current cohort of staff is not sufficient to run the 2 units.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will visit Donaghadee Health Centre to discuss the problems faced by the surgery.
answer_text: All requests for visits need to be made via correspondence to my Private Office; I am unable to respond to requests via Assembly Questions.I am aware that the practice is facing pressures with regards to high demand for their services and difficulties sourcing locum doctors.  These issues were flagged to Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) colleagues at a recent GMS visit.  SPPG colleagues offered advice and support to the practice during the visit.The Department of Health has invested significant funding within General Practice in order to help GP practices adapt to the challenges of the Covid-19 pandemic and meet the high demand for their services.    Funding of up to £5.5million was made available to support General Practice in Northern Ireland over the winter period. This included up to £1.7million to further improve telephony infrastructure and accessibility, in addition to £1.7million already invested to upgrade GP telephony.Investment and support will continue to be provided, including but not limited to, an increase in the number of NI GP training places in 2022/23 and also the continuing roll out of Multidisciplinary Teams within GP practices which should help to alleviate a number of the issues currently being experienced across general practice, particularly in relation to high demand for GP services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on proposed infrastructure changes planned for the Northern Ireland rail network.
answer_text: As you may be aware, work is ongoing to take a strategic look at our rail network to determine how best it can deliver for our citizens in the future. One element of this work is the All-Island Strategic Rail Review. The Review is being undertaken to allow us to consider our rail network across this island with a view to how it can be improved for everyone. It will examine the potential for rail connections across the island including how our existing connections could be improved and where new rail links would be best placed. It will also examine how we can connect our rail network to areas across the Island of Ireland, our international gateways through our ports and airports, the potential for transporting freight using the rail network whilst also considering how we can move to a low or zero emission rail network. It is anticipated that the review will be completed in the autumn of 2022.My Department is also currently developing the new Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan (RSTNTP) which will set out priorities for investment in our road, rail and inter-urban limited stop bus network, including park & ride, through to 2035. This will, in association with Translink, include high level assessments on a variety of potential rail improvements. The RSTNTP will be subject to public consultation and I will be pleased to consider representations on the plan including those in relation to the rail network.In addition, Translink’s current capital plan includes significant investment in the existing rail network to renew track signalling and structures as assets are life expired. These include several major projects such as Lisburn Area Renewals, Cullybackey Area Renewals, Central Area Renewals and Phase 3 of the Coleraine to Derry programme.   The wider Belfast – Derry programme will consider introducing additional passing loops on the corridor to increase capacity and optimise timetable development. These projects however are subject to appropriate business case approvals and budget being available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will visit Millisle to discuss with the local community group the challenges they face and the investment that is needed for the village.
answer_text: DAERA, together with DfC and DfI, has developed a Covid Small Settlement Regeneration Programme for settlements with population of under 5,000, and that programme is being administered by DfC and delivered by District Councils.				The Ards and North Down Council Regeneration Plan includes a proposed Village Accessibility & Connectivity programme for a number of villages, including Millisle. This scheme is at an early stage of development, and each individual village element may be subject to change due to the requirement of Council approval.  At present this is the only DAERA Programme that would have relevance for Millisle and, in the circumstances there is little to be gained from a visit at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will visit Bangor Hospital to discuss the reopening of the Minor Injuries Unit.
answer_text: The reopening of the unit has already been referenced in detail in AQW 57/22-27.I recently had a very useful visit to the hospital following an earlier invitation from one of your constituency colleagues; I met with many of the staff and was glad to see the range of services on offer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional resources he is putting into respite care for parents.
answer_text: Respite services play a critical role in supporting disabled children, young people and their families to stay together and reduce the need for intensive late interventions by Health & Social Care. Due to increasing complexity presented by children with disabilities, there has been growing demand for short breaks and long term residential care placements, which has been further impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic. Respite services for families with children with learning disabilities continue to be prioritised on the assessed needs of the individual and their family. There is a recognition at a regional and departmental level that there are insufficient short breaks and residential care placements to meet the growing complexity of Children with Disabilities (CwD).To meet the future service needs, the HSCB (now the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group) developed a Regional Framework for Children’s Disability Services, which proposes a new overarching model of care for children with a disability in Northern Ireland. This draft framework outlines a new model of practice based on the principles of: (i) early intervention; (ii) collaboration and multi-agency working; (iii) co-design and decision making with families; and (iv) person centred care.The implementation of the framework will be underpinned by four strategic work streams, which will require significant transformation across Health & Social Care services:Improving our early offer of help and community based supports; Developing additional effective residential short breaks, fostering and intensive family support;Rethinking our approach to residential provision and how we support CwD effectively in out of home placements; andGetting Transition right The CwD draft framework is currently under consideration by the Department.  It should however be noted that the recommendations set out in the framework will require significant re-design of existing services, workforce development and investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans her Department has to address the lack of four-bedroom properties across the Housing Executive stock in North Down.
answer_text: The Housing Executive will continue to work with housing associations on a scheme-by-scheme basis to ensure that an appropriate mix of homes is provided to address the needs of individuals and households on the waiting list.I have developed a Housing Supply Strategy which sets out a long term framework for collaborative action to increase supply, including how we can deliver homes of different types to meet a range of housing needs. I am currently considering options for progressing the Strategy in the continuing absence of an Executive and agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the powers held by schools to address bullying.
answer_text: Schools have the power to address bullying through The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (NI) 2016 which provides a common definition of bullying; establishes a new duty for schools to record all incidents of bullying, their motivation and their outcome; and requires the schools Boards of Governors (BoG) collectively to take responsibility for the development, implementation, monitoring and periodic review of the school’s anti-bullying policies and procedures.The Principal and Board of Governors of a school can take any action necessary to respond to incidents of bullying through application of their school discipline/positive behaviour policy and have been provided with support and resources to do so by the Department and Education Authority.

Location data is based on postcode of principal practice.List size is the number of patients registered to a GP Practice.  Patients refers to individuals with a live medical registration at a Northern Ireland GP Practice.A number of individual practices operate from the Newtownards Road Bangor Health Centre location and can be differentiated by the Practice number.A full list of GP practices and registered patients is provided in Table 1.7 of the General Medical Services for Northern Ireland Quarterly Series at General Medical Services Quarterly Statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans his Department has to stop the use of Russian coal at Kilroot power station.
answer_text: Kilroot power station is a commercial enterprise. It is therefore a decision for EPUKI, owners of Kilroot, as to whether they choose to use Russian coal. The UK Government Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy issued energy security of supply guidance that permits a gradual phasing out of Russian fuel by the end of the year. My Department will encourage NI firms to abide by that guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the findings of the Test and Vaccinate or Remove project where taken into account when developing the programme of badger intervention in areas of high incidence of Bovine Tuberculosis.
answer_text: In developing the new bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland, my Department considered in detail different methods for wildlife intervention and how these might be delivered.  Officials considered  the approaches to badger intervention currently operating in England, Wales and the Republic of Ireland (extrapolating these costs to the NI situation as far as possible), and took into account experience from the five year Test and Vaccinate or Remove (TVR) research project in County Down.  In reaching my final decision on the approach to wildlife intervention, I took account of the recommendations of the business case; the scientific and policy advice provided by my officials; the views expressed in the public consultations; and the information within the Strategic Environmental Assessment.  My view remains that a farmer-led, non-selective cull in specified areas provides the best means of reducing the role played by badgers in the spread of bTB and offers the best value for money to the public purse.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to his intention to introduce a programme of badger intervention in areas of high incidence of Bovine Tuberculosis, (i) for his assessment of how many badgers will be culled; and (ii) how many hot spots are planned.
answer_text: The new bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland includes proposals for a wildlife intervention to address the role of the badger population in the maintenance and spread of bovine Tuberculosis. The aim of the cull is to lower the badger population density in order to reduce the overall infection load in badgers, and thereby reduce the bTB incidence in cattle.	In order to deliver an effective badger intervention programme it is necessary to reduce and maintain the badger population, within an area, to 30% of the original population. There is a high level of uncertainty when estimating badger populations but a pragmatic assumption based on the mid-point of two recognised methods for population calculation, literature review and genetic sampling results, would indicate the starting population in a notional 100 Km2 intervention area to be around 400 badgers.  To identify those areas with greater than average badger population density the Department will construct a spatial prediction model using previous survey data. Wildlife intervention will take place only in those areas where badgers are believed to be playing a significant role in the maintenance of bovine TB. Such areas will be characterised by having both an established bovine TB hotspot and an above average badger population density. However, when finally defining area boundaries other factors will also be considered such as local epidemiological knowledge and making best use of natural barriers such as major rivers or roads. There is no planned number of intervention areas, rather an assessment of all parts of Northern Ireland will be carried out by applying the above criteria. In any event no more than 30% of the total farmed area of Northern Ireland will be involved at any one time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many objections to badger culling were received as part of the consultation on tackling Bovine Tuberculosis.
answer_text: The public consultation on the Department’s Proposed Implementation and Next Steps of the bTB Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland was launched on the 16 July 2021 and closed on the 10 September 2021.Overall there were 3,367 responses to the consultation.In answer to the consultation question ‘Do you agree with the Department’s preferred option for Wildlife Intervention’ the Department received 3,334 responses.In favour – 2,853; Against – 481; and Not answered – 33.Those who responded against are considered to object to the proposal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why he did not seek the approval of the Assembly on his decision on the way forward for a new strategy to eradicate Bovine Tuberculosis.
answer_text: There was no requirement for the Northern Ireland Assembly to approve my Department’s new bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Strategy for Northern Ireland. However, noting the significant interest in the proposals, I was pleased to be able to announce the Strategy by way of oral statement on 24 March 2022 providing Members with the opportunity to ask questions on the detail of the proposals.  		In addition, my officials met with the AERA Committee on a number of occasions during the development of the Strategy.  This included updates before and after the public consultation, and following my statement on 24 March 2022.  The recommendations within the Committee’s 2022 position paper on this matter were also taken fully into account when I made my final decisions on the way forward.  The Committees position paper was supportive of most aspects of the new Strategy and I was grateful for the constructive comments and recommendations made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to his statement to the Assembly on 24 March 2022, whether his decisions on the way forward for a new strategy to eradicate Bovine Tuberculosis require Executive approval.
answer_text: When I launched the consultation on the new bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Strategy on 16 July 2021, I wrote to Executive colleagues informing them of the consultation and advising that I would return to seek their support once I made final decisions on the way forward. Having published the new Strategy on 24 March 2022 it is my intention to write to Ministerial colleagues in the coming weeks on the matter noting that Executive approval cannot be sought at this time and until a new Executive is in place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will provide funding to councils to help people with mental health issues access free gym membership for a period of time.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, published in June 2021, recognises the benefit of physical activity for all, including those experiencing mental health issues.While the Department for Communities is responsible for funding local Councils, the Public Health Agency (PHA) supports a number of programmes that seek to improve the physical and mental health of people through physical activity.  In 2021/22, the PHA provided funding to 84 projects across the community and voluntary sector to promote positive mental health, emotional wellbeing, suicide prevention and self-harm.The PHA works in partnership with Sport NI and Governing Bodies of Sport on the ‘Wellbeing in Sport’ Action Plan and is also the implementation lead for the Department’s ‘A Fitter Future For All’ obesity prevention strategic framework.  In addition, both the Department and the PHA are key stakeholders in the development of the ‘Active Living: More People, More Active, More of the Time’ Sport and Physical Activity Strategy.  These programmes aim to improve physiological, psychological and psychosocial health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will advocate (i) for the inclusion of (a) basic first aid; and (b) mental health training, in the syllabus; and (ii) for the improvement of the syllabus in relation to (a) personal finance; and (b) the dangers of drug and alcohol abuse.
answer_text: There are opportunities for schools to educate pupils on the matters you reference through the ‘Personal Development and Mutual Understanding’ (PDMU) area of learning in primary schools and through ‘Learning for Life and Work’ (LLW) in post-primary schools.  Pupils begin learning the basic principles relating to first aid from when they start school.  For example, at Foundation Stage and Key Stages 1 and 2 they learn how to look after their health and stay safe in different environments.  At Key Stage 3 pupils build on this and have the opportunity to develop an awareness of emergency first aid procedures and my Department and the Education Authority has issued guidance in respect of concussion awareness.  You may also be aware that, from September 2022, Key Stage 3 pupils will be required to undertake cardiopulmonary resuscitation training and be aware of the use of automatic external defibrillators. Teaching about mental health issues is included within the Personal Development strand of the LLW at post-primary level.  This provides pupils with the opportunity to develop an understanding of how to maximise and sustain their mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing including managing emotions and reactions to on-going life experiences; enhancing self-awareness; developing preventative and coping strategies; and exploration of the effects of different influences.  The Council for the Curriculum Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides curricular guidance and teaching support materials for use in schools. My Department has published a Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework which builds on existing support structures and multi-disciplinary working to ensure that systems and pathways are in place to provide individualised support and help to build resilience, self-esteem and confidence in our children and young people. CCEA has established a Wellbeing Hub as one of the programmes under the Framework, which contains a range of age appropriate, up-to-date, relevant resources to support the emotional health and wellbeing of pupils.  Financial Capability, including personal finance, is embedded as a statutory element of the Northern Ireland curriculum at Key Stage 3.  Building on earlier learning at primary school, pupils have the opportunity to develop their knowledge and understanding of personal finance issues, learn skills to enable competent and responsible financial decision making and to apply mathematical skills in everyday financial planning and decision making.  In addition, Using Mathematics is a cross-curricular skill at each Key Stage and pupils should be enabled to develop financial capability skills.  Financial capability is also an integral part of the Home Economics curriculum at post-primary school.In relation to drugs and alcohol abuse, in primary schools, pupils are taught about the importance of making good choices and being aware of the effects of drugs and alcohol on their own health a

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans are there to develop more hybrid degrees, combining part-time study with training in the workplace.
answer_text: My Department’s Higher Level Apprenticeship (HLA) programme provides funding for the delivery of off the job training at levels 4 to 7, which is equivalent to Foundation Degree through to Masters level study.  HLAs are demand-led, work-based programmes designed around the needs of employers, offering recognised Higher Education qualifications to new employees and existing employees taking on new roles which require significant training and development.The HLA programme is delivered across Northern Ireland by further education colleges, the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and local universities.  HLAs directly align to DfE’s vision for a 10x Economy, inspiring and preparing a future generation of workers that can respond flexibly to our future skills requirements, while enabling employers to grow and scale their businesses.HLAs continue to add to Northern Ireland’s talent pool, with a large number of participants supporting the growth of priority sectors such as, Digital and ICT, Advanced Manufacturing and Engineering and Fintech.HLA providers have been asked to submit proposals for programmes they intend to deliver to a 2022/23 cohort by 10th June 2022. Where employer demand exists, proposals for new HLAs may be received.  Proposals will then be reviewed by employer led Sectoral Partnerships and Department Officials before being approved for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans he has to reduce rates for small to medium sized businesses.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive, it is not possible to bring forward any further rates support for small to medium sized businesses, aside from the Small Business Rate Relief scheme and rate holidays that are currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans to ensure that all Housing Executive properties have adequate cavity wall insulation and the timetable for completion of this work.
answer_text: In March 2022 the Housing Executive published its Action Plan for addressing cavity wall insulation (CWI) issues in its stock.  The Action Plan can be found at the following link. 	https://www.nihe.gov.uk/About-Us/Corporate-Strategies/Cavity-Wall-Insulation-Action-Plan

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of a Health and Wellbeing Centre in North Down, including the timetable and proposed locations.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust submitted a proposal for a new Health and Care Centre for the North Down and Ards area as part of a recent capital planning review exercise for the period 2022 - 2032.I can confirm that plans to replace Bangor Health Centre and Bangor Hospital with a new Ards and North Down Primary and Community Care Centre are included in the Primary Care Infrastructure Development Strategic Implementation Plan.However, capital investment plans are being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.The Trust is currently developing a business case which will consider all the options for locations and costs.  A timetable will be agreed when a business case is approved and funding confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he can give a commitment to holding a job fair event in Bangor.
answer_text: My Department recently contacted Ards and North Down Borough Council on 1 April 2022 with an offer to work in partnership to deliver a face to face or virtual job fair during this current year.  We await a response from Ards and North Down Borough Council to confirm their preferred date and location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what new bus routes are being considered for the North Down area.
answer_text: The management of Translink services in the North Down area is an operational matter for Translink. You should therefore direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when GP practice services will return to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: I would refer you to the response provided to you in AQW 266/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health where the Bangor GP Unit staff were transferred to following the temporary closure of the Bangor GP Unit.
answer_text: Staff were transferred to the Ards Minor Injuries Unit and supported the establishment of the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust’s COVID swabbing centre, the vaccination programme and the core minor injuries service from the Ards site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recruitment of additional nurses.
answer_text: As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.I remain committed to sustained investment in growing the local nursing and midwifery workforce to meet ever increasing demands, for example, this year we have commissioned pre-registration nursing and midwifery training places at a record high level, (1,335). This is alongside a highly successful International Nurse Recruitment campaign through which, 1,023 nurses have been recruited as at 30/04/2022.Whilst recruitment is an operational matter for Health and Social Care Trusts as employers, I can advise that there were 16,772.7 WTE registered nursing & midwifery HSC staff in post at 31st March 2022. This represented an increase of 397 WTE (or 2.4%) from the previous March or an increase of 1,660 WTE (or 11%) from the position at 31st March 2018.Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority & the Department has undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures, however, I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans she has to extend the Uniform Grant scheme to include more families struggling with the cost of living.
answer_text: Uniform Grants are available to families who meet certain qualifying criteria: https://www.eani.org.uk/financial-help/free-school-meals-uniform-grants/free-school-meals-uniform-grant-eligibilityThe Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review is considering a number of options for free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for certain year groups or all pupils and the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation exercise will take place later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on funding for the recruitment of new PSNI officers.
answer_text: The Member will recall that earlier this year, I advised that the PSNI received an in-year funding allocation in 2021-22, which enabled the PSNI to retain their police numbers and enabled additional recruitment. However, I also advised that the proposed draft Budget allocation for police numbers in 2022-25 is insufficient to maintain the police officer numbers at 7,100 and further progress to increase police officer numbers toward the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) target of 7,500 Officers will not be possible unless further funding is made available. I have raised concerns regarding the Police Budget with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues on several occasions since the publication of the draft Budget 2022-25.In the absence of an Executive, a budget for 2022-23 cannot be agreed. In order to assist with decision-making, the Department of Finance has provided each department with a Contingency Planning Envelope for 2022-23 for both Resource and Capital DEL, to allow the continuation of service delivery. The Contingency Planning Envelope for PSNI did not contain specific funding for police numbers and is insufficient to maintain, let alone increase, police numbers.The 2022-23 financial position for the Department looks extremely challenging at this stage and, if confirmed, that would require difficult decisions to be made.There have been a number of changes to the funding position for 2022-23 from the announcement of the Draft Budget, and there is now a significant amount of additional resource funding available for allocation in 2022-23. In the absence of an Executive, this funding cannot be allocated and is currently being held centrally by the Department of Finance. The reality is there is no Executive or Assembly in place to approve a Budget.   It is imperative that we come back together as an Executive and work collectively to ensure that all available funds are effectively utilised.The prioritisation of policing resources, including recruitment, is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting his operational independence and the role of the Policing Board.  In the meantime, I remain committed to continue working with the Minister of Finance and the Chief Constable, to ensure that we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on all Translink services in North Down returning to pre-COVID levels.
answer_text: The management of Translink services in the North Down area is an operational matter for Translink. You should therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans has he to reduce bureaucracy in the health service.
answer_text: I have already significantly reduced bureaucracy through closure of the Health and Social Care Board and the removal of an entire layer of administration. The closure of the HSCB is an important first step in transforming how services are planned and managed and building on this, work has begun on the development of an integrated care system for NI – ICS NI. ICS NI is based on collaboration and partnership working, reaching across sectors and traditional organisational boundaries. Its aim is to create a more agile and responsive system that can focus on meeting local needs.As we move towards more integrated ways of working my Department will be vigilant to identifying further opportunities to reduce bureaucracy.

answer_text: My Department has already allocated an additional 50 Year 8 places for September 2022 admission at Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College and will consider any further increases requested by the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to list the nurseries across Bangor that still have places available for September 2022.
answer_text: Funded pre-school education places are provided through the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) which has a two stage preference based admissions process administered by the Education Authority (EA).Stage One of the process concluded on 28 April 2022 and the EA published a list of settings that still had funded pre-school places available at that time (see link below).Funded Pre-school Places Available at Close of Stage 1 (28 April 2022) (eani.org.uk)Stage Two of the admissions process is currently ongoing and will conclude on 16 June 2022 when parents will be advised of the outcome of their applications.  A further updated list of settings with funded places still available at the end of Stage Two will be published by the EA on its website on 16 June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when GP services will return to pre-pandemic levels.
answer_text: I recognise how important it is that people can access GP services when they need to do so.  It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate. The general practice telephone first consultations allow patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.  This approach helps the GP to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of us who seek clinical support and advice from our primary care practitioners.  I recognise that patients have experienced issues in contacting their GP services and understand the frustration this can cause.  My Department has established a working group to examine issues around access to GP services, with a view to recommending actions to be taken in the short and long term to improve the situation.  This will include scoping a new GP tech platform to facilitate an improved telephony service.In recognition of the increasing demand for primary care services, my Department has continued to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I recently announced that my Department would be increasing of the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the review and subject to the availability of funding.It is important to note however that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Rather this is part of a wider programme of work to help improve patient access to services in primary care.  Other elements include the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams as well as the introduction of Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans there are for a new GP practice building for Silverbirch Medical Practice in Bangor.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), formerly the Health and Social Care Board, continues to deliver a programme of work to improve primary care infrastructure across Northern Ireland based on the Hub and Spoke model through the Primary Care Infrastructure Development Programme. Over the last four years the Department of Health (DoH) has ring-fenced capital funding to support investment in primary care infrastructure. This funding allocation has increased over this time, with an allocation of c. £7m currently available in 2022/23.As an independent contractor, the Practice is responsible for the identification and exploration of options in respect to their premises needs.  GP practices operating under the General Medical Services contract can apply to the SPPG for Improvement Grants under the Health and Personal Social Services (NI) Order 1972 – Premises Costs Directions (NI) 2015. These grants can be used for the purposes of improving or extending GP premises and can be applied for by practices operating in either GP owned or leased premises.  GP Practices in GP owned or leased premises are also entitled to apply to the SPPG for reimbursement of rent and rates for their premises.SPPG funding support in respect to the implementation of any identified premises proposal would be subject to the completion of a business case. The approval of any such business case would be subject to an assessment of value for money and affordability within the budget available. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement. My ability to strategically plan capital investment is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of Ards and North Down Council's proposals in relation to the location of the cycling and walking greenway along the North Down Coastal Path and the impact this might have on the coastal path.
answer_text: ‘Exercise – Explore – Enjoy: a Strategic Plan for Greenways’, as published by my Department in 2016, includes a route along the North Down Coastal Path.  This plan provides a framework to enable Councils and other bodies to develop their own local schemes as part of a greenway network for the entire region, including cross border links.I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives. However, the North Down Coastal Path proposal is the responsibility of Ards and North Down Borough Council, including any consultations with local landowners and communities regarding the impact of their proposals.I would advise the member to contact the Council with his query.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans are in place to protect badger setts from development in the Bangor area.
answer_text: Badgers and their setts are protected under the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985 (as amended), and are a consideration for NIEA officials when considering planning applications and providing recommendations to the relevant planning authority. My response to an earlier question of yours (AQW 27928/17-22) detailed this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding will be distributed to football clubs.
answer_text: I have received legal advice in respect to the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer and this confirms that I have a responsibility to bring my proposals to the Executive for the necessary approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when work will commence on the junction at Killaughey Road, Upper Gransha Road, Movilla Road and Ballyblack Road.
answer_text: My officials are aware there have been a number of minor collisions at this location over a number of years. My Department has previously implemented a number of collision remedial measures including provision of enhanced warning and directional signs, advanced slow road markings and high friction surfacing, all of which combine to enhance driver awareness of and safety on the approaches to this junction.There are no plans to implement further measures at this time, however I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the location and to implement any additional measures that are deemed appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new building for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: Bangor Central Integrated Primary School has been selected for a major capital investment project to improve or replace its existing accommodation.  The project will cater for the school’s current approved enrolment of 618 pupils.The new design proposal for the school is progressing well.  A Proposal of Application Notice (PAN) was submitted to Ards and North Down Council planning service on 16 February 2022 commencing the statutory 12 week period prior to making a planning application.  As part of the PAN process a pre-application community consultation was undertaken via a public website from 18 March to 8 April, including a public event at the school on 30 March.The design team is now collating information from this process and preparing to make a full planning application in summer 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when traffic calming measures will be put in place at Ballyree Drive, Bloomfield Estate, Bangor.
answer_text: Local officials are actively considering the introduction of traffic calming measures at this location the timing and delivery of which will be largely dependent on the availability of funding and consideration of other competing priorities.  Therefore, once budgets and the works programme for 2022/23 have been finalised, details will be included in my Department’s Report to Ards and North Down Council which will be published on my Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library after its presentation to council.  The most recent Report can be accessed via the link: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-20212022Any viable proposals which are not included within the works programme for this financial year remain under consideration of potential inclusion in a future work programme, along with other competing schemes identified at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when resurfacing work will commence for Kinnegar Drive, Holywood.
answer_text: Works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year cannot be finalised until my Department’s annual budget has been confirmed. Once confirmed, details of the agreed work programme for the Ards and North Down Council area will be included within the Department’s Council Report which will, in due course, be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.I can confirm that my officials are aware of the condition of Kinnegar Drive, Bangor and its potential inclusion in a future resurfacing programme is under consideration.I can also advise that the previous resurfacing contract for the Ards and North Down area expired in November 2021 and following legal challenges to the award of new resurfacing contracts in spring 2021, the Department has been working at pace to develop and implement a new contract strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on resolving issues with the sewerage infrastructure at Canneyreagh Road, Donaghadee.
answer_text: The management of sewerage infrastructure at Canneyreagh Road, Donaghadee and across the North is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there is a double glazing scheme for the Churchill Estate area in Millisle.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has confirmed a double glazing scheme for the Churchill Estate area of Millisle is programmed to commence in 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when will the grass cutting of central reservations and verges take place along the A2 Circular Road, Bangor.
answer_text: I can confirm that the first cut of grass along the central reservation and verges of the Bangor Ring Road is scheduled to take place in early June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many grass cuts are planned for the North Down area this year.
answer_text: In line with my Department’s current policy, there are currently two grass cuts planned for the Ards and North Down Council area this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration has been given to a Public Realm and Environmental Improvement scheme for Main Street, Millisle.
answer_text: A Public Realm or Environmental Improvement scheme for Main Street, Millisle would fall under the remit of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there has been any notification of a judicial review against his decision to cull the badger population.
answer_text: My Departments new bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Strategy aims to put Northern Ireland on a path towards bTB eradication.International experience in this area has shown that it is essential to tackle all of the factors involved, in both the spread and continuance of this terrible disease, if the ultimate goal of eradication is to be achieved. This includes tackling the emotive issue of TB in wildlife.It is important to note that my recent decision, taken as part of a wide ranging holistic Strategy, is not for an indiscriminate cull of the badger population in general but, rather, a non-selective cull of badgers only in those specific localities where they may be playing a significant role in the maintenance of bovine TB. I can confirm that this particular matter is currently the subject of ongoing judicial proceedings.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has not reopened Bangor Minor Injuries Unit.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 57/22-27 for the answers to these questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Bangor Minor Injuries Unit will reopen.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 57/22-27 for the answers to these questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has plans to close the Bangor Minor Injuries Unit.
answer_text: The consolidation of the Bangor and Ards Minor Injuries Units (MIUs) on the Ards Hospital site remains under continuous review as part of the Trust's plans for rebuilding services. At a time when there are nationally recognised workforce shortages, including the availability of sufficient suitably qualified emergency nurse practitioners, to have two units operating five miles apart also has to be taken into account. The Trust is actively trying to recruit to these posts, with the most recent advertisement having closed on 25th May 2022 with interviews scheduled for the week commencing 13th June. The staffing of Minor Injury Units (Type 3 Emergency Care Departments) will need to be reviewed further as part of the ‘Review of Urgent and Emergency Care Services in Northern Ireland.’ Given all of the above, there is currently no date planned for the re-opening of Bangor Minor Injuries Unit nor are there any plans to close it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to list the NHS dentists operating in the Donaghadee area.
answer_text: Dentists who have arrangements in place with the Department to provide Health Service dentistry in Donaghadee are listed in Table 1. This list of dentists refers to those with an arrangement to provide health service dentistry at 31 December 2021, the latest National Statistics publicly available.Table 1: HSC Dentists at 31st December 2021 in Super Output Areas covering DonaghadeeDental PracticePractice AddressDentist NameHarbour Dental13 The Parade, Donaghadee,BT21 0AEMr M A LONGDR C KIRKPATRICKDonaghadee Dental Surgery55/57 High Street, Donaghadee,BT21 0AQMrs R CAMPBELLMiss H SIRIBORMr S SHERRARD

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when will Beechwood Avenue, Bangor be resurfaced.
answer_text: A programme of substantial works, including resurfacing, for the 2022/23 financial year cannot be finalised until my Department’s annual budget has been confirmed. Once confirmed, a programme of improvement works for the Ards and North Down Council area will be included within the Roads Council Report which will, at the appropriate time, be presented to council, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.I can confirm that my officials are aware of the condition of Beechwood Avenue, Hawthorne Drive, and Beechwood Gardens in Bangor and these roads will be considered for inclusion in a future resurfacing programme of work.I can also confirm that work was completed on Clandeboye Close, Bangor in March 2022 to upgrade footways and resurface the carriageway and that repairs are planned for Cleland Park North, Bangor to address the areas of greatest concern.I must also take the opportunity to explain that the resurfacing contract for the Ards and North Down area expired in November 2021 and that following legal challenges to the award of resurfacing contracts in spring 2021, the Department has been working at pace to develop and implement a new contract strategy. My Department is currently aiming to have a replacement contract available by late 2022. Subject to availability of funding, the majority of schemes that are currently on hold due to these contractual issues will be carried out as quickly as possible after the contract has been awarded.In the meantime, my Department will continue to undertake routine visual inspections of all roads in the council area and collect road related defects that meet the intervention levels set out in the current road maintenance standards which will be prioritised for repair.
